Sancho insisted they were only windmills. Don Quixote refused to listen and charged the nearest one with his lance, calling out to it as a giant.

The lance struck the sail of the windmill, the wind turned the sail with great force, and Don Quixote and Rocinante were thrown to the ground. As Sancho ran up, the knight insisted that some enemy enchanter had turned the giants into windmills at the last moment to rob him of his victory. The two went on from there in search of further adventures, and a man who attacks windmills as if they were giants has been called a Don Quixote ever since.
zh: 拉曼恰乡间一位上了年纪的小绅士阿隆索·吉哈诺，因为读了太多骑士小说，竟相信自己也是一名游侠骑士。他擦亮一套破旧铠甲，把瘦马取名为洛西南特，又给自己起名「堂吉诃德」。他说动当地一位农夫桑丘·潘萨给自己当侍从，许诺等冒险成功就赐他一座岛屿管辖。
两人骑行在拉曼恰的旷野上，堂吉诃德远远看到三四十架风车。他对桑丘说，那是一群巨人，与之交战将是莫大的功业。桑丘连声否认，说那不过是风车。堂吉诃德不肯听，挺枪策马朝最近的一架冲过去，对它高声叫阵，把它当作巨人。
长枪正刺进风车的翼板，狂风一来翼板猛然一转，把堂吉诃德连人带马甩到了地上。桑丘赶上前去，骑士却坚持说，是某个邪术师在最后关头把巨人变成了风车，要夺走他的胜利。两人又一同上路寻找下一桩冒险。从此以后，凡是把风车当成巨人去厮杀的人，就被人称作「堂吉诃德」。

en: In Prague in the sixteenth century, Rabbi Judah Loew was the chief rabbi of the Jewish community, which faced repeated threats and false accusations. To protect his people, the rabbi turned to the Kabbalistic tradition and decided to make a man of clay. With two helpers, fasting and praying, he gathered clay from the bank of the Vltava and shaped it into a human form. He spoke the proper holy names and inscribed them in the figure, and the Golem stood up alive.

The Golem was enormously strong but could not speak. It served the rabbi as a guardian of the Jewish quarter, walking the streets at night, frightening off attackers, and doing heavy work in the synagogue and the rabbi's house. On the Sabbath the rabbi removed the holy name from its mouth or forehead so that the creature would rest, as people did. For a time the Jewish quarter felt safe.

One Sabbath the rabbi forgot to deactivate the Golem in time. The creature grew uncontrolled and began to rampage through the streets. The rabbi caught up with it just before sunset, removed the name, and the Golem fell into pieces of clay. He carried the pieces up to the attic of the Old-New Synagogue, where, the story says, the inert clay still rests, waiting to be raised again if it should ever be needed.
zh: 十六世纪的布拉格，犹大·勒夫拉比是犹太社区的首席拉比，社区不断遭受威胁和栽赃。为保护族人，他决定借助卡巴拉传统，造一个泥人。在两名助手帮忙下，斋戒祈祷之后，他从伏尔塔瓦河岸取来泥土，捏成人形。他诵念神圣的名字，并把名字刻入泥人之中，魔像便活了过来，立起身来。
这具魔像力大无比，却不能说话。它给拉比当犹太街区的守卫，夜里在街上巡逻、吓退闯入者，又在会堂和拉比家里干粗活。每逢安息日，拉比都会把魔像口中或额上的神圣之名取下，让它和人一样休息。有一段时间，犹太街区因为它的存在而显得安稳了许多。
某个安息日，拉比来不及在日落前关停魔像。它失去控制，开始在街上横冲直撞。拉比赶在日落前最后一刻追上它，取走神名，魔像便散成一堆泥块。他把那堆泥块抬到「老新犹太会堂」的阁楼上。传说至今那堆泥土仍静静躺在那里，万一某一天族人再次需要，它仍可被重新唤起。

en: In Mesopotamian tradition, the high god Enlil grew angry that humanity had become too numerous and noisy on the earth. He decided to wipe out the human race with a great flood. He made the other gods swear not to warn anyone. But Enki, the god of wisdom, could not bear the thought of all humanity destroyed.

Enki went to the wall of the reed hut of a man named Atrahasis and spoke to it, so that he could say truthfully that he had not spoken to any human. Atrahasis, listening on the other side, was told to tear down his house and build a great boat instead. He was to take his family, animals of every kind, and supplies on board. He did everything Enki said.

The flood came, and it rained for seven days and seven nights. When the waters subsided, Atrahasis and his boat came to rest, and he stepped out and offered sacrifice. The gods, who had been hungry without human offerings, gathered greedily around the smoke. Enlil, seeing that a human had survived, was furious, but the other gods persuaded him; in the end the gods accepted humanity again, but set limits — death, miscarriage, and infertility — to keep the population in check.
zh: 在美索不达米亚的传说中，主神恩利尔对人类的繁盛与喧嚣大为震怒，决定用一场大洪水把人类全部毁灭。他要其他诸神立誓，不许向任何人发出警告。然而智慧之神恩基不忍看到人类整族灭亡。
恩基走到一个名叫阿特拉哈西斯的人居住的芦苇屋外，故意对着屋墙说话——这样他可以理直气壮地说，自己并没有对任何凡人开口。阿特拉哈西斯在墙的另一边听到指示：拆掉屋子，造一艘大船，把家人、各种牲畜和补给都带上船。他完全照办。
洪水如期而至，连下了七天七夜。等水退去，阿特拉哈西斯的船搁浅在一处高地，他走下船献上祭品。诸神已经因为没有人间供奉而饿了好几天，闻着烟气一齐围拢过来。恩利尔看见竟有人活了下来，怒气冲冲，其他诸神反复劝解，最后达成妥协：诸神重新接纳了人类，但加上了死亡、流产与不孕等限度，用来控制人口的增长。

en: Ali Baba was a poor woodcutter who lived in a town in old Persia. One day in the forest he saw a band of forty thieves ride up to a hidden cliff. Their captain spoke the words "Open Sesame," and a door in the rock swung open. The thieves rode inside with their loot, came out empty-handed, and rode off.

Ali Baba came down from his hiding place, said the same words, and entered a cave piled with gold, silver, and silks. He took only a modest sack of gold and rode home. His brother Cassim, learning the secret, went to the cave with greater greed, but inside he forgot the password and was trapped. The thieves returned and killed him.

The thieves traced the loss to Ali Baba's house and tried twice to take revenge — once by hiding men in great oil jars sent as a gift. Ali Baba's clever servant Morgiana noticed the men breathing in the jars and poured boiling oil into each. When the captain himself came in disguise as a guest, Morgiana danced at the meal and stabbed him during the dance. Ali Baba lived on safely, and Morgiana was rewarded with her freedom.
zh: 阿里巴巴是古波斯一个穷樵夫，住在城外的小镇里。有一天他在林中砍柴，看到一队四十大盗骑马来到一处隐秘的山崖前。盗首一声「芝麻开门」，山石之中竟开出一道门。盗匪们驮着抢来的财物进去，又两手空空骑马离开。
阿里巴巴从藏身处下来，照样念出那句话，走进了一座堆满金银和绸缎的山洞。他只取了一小袋金子骑马回家。哥哥卡西姆得知秘密后贪心大起，单独跑去取财，进了洞里却忘了开门的咒语，被困在里面。强盗回来后将他杀害。
强盗顺着线索追到阿里巴巴家，先后两次报复——一次是把同伙藏在送来的大油坛里。阿里巴巴聪明的女仆莫吉娜察觉坛中有人呼吸，向每只坛里浇下滚油，把藏在里面的盗匪一一烫死。盗首后来又乔装成客人来赴宴，莫吉娜在席上献舞，借舞势把匕首扎入他胸口。阿里巴巴从此安然度日，莫吉娜也因功获得自由。

en: King Shahryar discovered that his wife had been unfaithful and had her put to death. Determined that no woman should betray him again, he took to marrying a new wife each night and having her killed in the morning. The kingdom was emptying of young women. The grand vizier's elder daughter, Scheherazade, asked her father to give her to the king.

On the wedding night Scheherazade asked permission to say goodbye to her younger sister, who would sit in the room with them. The sister, by arrangement, asked Scheherazade for a story. Scheherazade began a tale of marvels — but at dawn she stopped at the most thrilling point and said the rest must wait. The king, eager to hear how it ended, decided to spare her for one more night.

The next night she finished the first tale and at once began another, again breaking off at daybreak. So she went on, night after night, for a thousand and one nights. By that time she had borne the king three children, and he had come to love and trust her. He gave up his cruel custom, and the stories she had told became the great cycle of the Arabian Nights.
zh: 国王沙赫里亚尔发现王后不忠，将她处死。从此他决意不再让任何女人有机会背叛自己，便每晚娶一位新妻子，天亮就将她杀掉。王国里的年轻女子越来越少。宰相的长女山鲁佐德，主动请求父亲把自己献给国王。
新婚之夜，山鲁佐德请求让她与年幼的妹妹道别，让妹妹陪坐房中。妹妹依事先约定向山鲁佐德求一个故事。山鲁佐德便开始讲一个奇异的故事——可天将破晓时，她故意停在最扣人心弦的一段，说后面要等下回再讲。国王想知道结局，便决定多留她一晚。
第二夜她讲完前一晚的故事，又紧接着开了一个新故事，黎明时再次中断。如此一夜接一夜，整整讲了一千零一夜。这段时间里，她为国王生下了三个孩子，国王也渐渐爱她、信她。他放弃了那条残忍的规矩，而她讲过的那些故事，便汇成了举世闻名的「一千零一夜」。

en: According to the Hebrew Bible, God formed the first man, Adam, from the dust of the earth and placed him in the Garden of Eden. From one of Adam's ribs God then made a woman, Eve, to be his companion. God allowed them to eat from every tree in the garden except the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, which stood in the middle of the garden. They lived in the garden naked and unashamed.

A serpent came to Eve and told her that, if she ate the forbidden fruit, she would not die as God had said, but would become like God, knowing good and evil. Eve took the fruit and ate, and gave some to Adam, who also ate. At once their eyes were opened, and they realized they were naked. They sewed fig leaves together to cover themselves and hid from God among the trees.

God called for them and learned what they had done. He cursed the serpent to crawl on its belly, told the woman that she would give birth in pain, and told the man that he would have to work the earth by the sweat of his brow. Then he drove them out of the garden and set angels with flaming swords at the entrance, so they could never come back. From that exile descend all later human stories of sin, labor, and the search for what was lost.
zh: 据希伯来圣经记载，神用泥土造出第一个人亚当，将他安置在伊甸园中。神又从亚当的一条肋骨造出女子夏娃，让她作他的伴侣。神许他们吃园中各样树上的果子，唯独不许吃园中央那棵分辨善恶的树的果子。两人在园中赤身露体，并不羞耻。
一条蛇来对夏娃说，吃了那禁果不会像神所说的那样致死，反倒会如神一般明白善恶。夏娃伸手摘下果子吃了，又给亚当一同吃。两人的眼睛立刻明亮起来，发觉自己赤身露体，便用无花果叶缝起来遮身，藏在园中树下躲避神。
神呼唤他们，问明了原委。他咒诅蛇此后只能用腹爬行，又对女子说她生产必受痛苦，对男子说他必须汗流满面才能从地里得粮。然后神把两人逐出伊甸园，在园门口立下手执火焰之剑的天使，使他们再也无法回去。从这次被逐起，人类此后所有关于罪、劳苦与寻回失去之物的故事，都在这一脉延续。

en: In Egyptian tradition the sun god Ra, grown old, learned that humanity was plotting against him. He sent his eye, the fierce goddess Sekhmet, in the form of a lioness to punish them. Sekhmet went down to the earth and slaughtered without restraint, drinking the blood of those she killed. The fields ran red, and humanity faced extinction.

Ra repented and wanted to stop her, but Sekhmet's bloodlust had grown beyond his command. He ordered seven thousand jars of beer to be brewed, dyed red with ochre to look like blood, and poured out over the fields where the goddess would pass. When Sekhmet came down and saw the red flood, she drank deeply, mistaking it for blood.

She grew drunk on the beer, lay down, and fell asleep, and when she woke her fury had cooled. From then on she returned to her gentler aspect, the cow-headed goddess Hathor, who loved music, dance, and joy. People remembered both faces of her — the lioness who could destroy and the lady who could bless — and celebrated festivals where beer was poured out in red jars to keep her anger at bay.
zh: 古埃及传说中，太阳神拉年老之后，得知人类暗中谋反。他派出自己的眼睛——化身狮形的女神塞赫麦特下凡惩罚人间。塞赫麦特一到地上便不分青红皂白地大开杀戒，喝被杀者的血。田野上血流成河，人类面临灭顶之灾。
拉后悔起来，想叫她停手，但塞赫麦特的嗜血已经不再听他号令。他便命人酿了七千坛啤酒，用赭石染成血色，全部倾倒在女神将经过的田野上。等塞赫麦特下来看到那一片殷红，以为是新鲜的血，便俯身大口畅饮。
她喝得酩酊大醉，就地倒下沉沉睡去，醒来时怒气已经消散。从此她重新显出温柔的面相，化成牛首女神哈索尔，主掌音乐、舞蹈和欢乐。世人把她的两副面孔都牢牢记下——会毁灭一切的母狮和会施福的女主——并设节庆把红色啤酒倾倒在地，以平息她可能重新升起的怒火。

en: Qays, a young Arab poet, fell in love with Layla, a girl of his tribe, when both were children at school. As they grew older his love deepened, and he praised her in poems that everyone in the desert came to know by heart. Her family was offended by the public verses and refused to let her marry him. They married her instead to another man.

Qays could not bear it. He left the settled life of his tribe and wandered into the wilderness, composing more and more poems about her. People began to call him Majnun, "the madman," because he spoke only of Layla and would speak to no one else. He lived in the desert with wild animals and grew thin and ragged, refusing all attempts to bring him home.

Layla, in her marriage, also pined away, and she died of her sorrow. When the news reached Majnun, he made his way to her grave, lay down beside it, and died there as well. The two were buried together at last. Their story spread through Persian and Arabic literature as the great image of a love that found its only fulfillment in death.
zh: 年轻的阿拉伯诗人盖斯，从童年起就在学塾里爱上了同族的少女莱拉。两人长大后，他的爱越发深沉，写出无数颂赞她的诗，沙漠中的人无不传诵。莱拉的家族却为这些四下流传的诗句感到难堪，拒绝把她嫁给盖斯，又把她许配给了另一个人。
盖斯心中无法承受这一切。他离开族群的定居生活，独自走入荒漠，不停地为莱拉写诗。世人渐渐改叫他「玛吉侬」，即「疯子」之意，因为他口中只说莱拉，旁人的话一概不听。他与野兽为伴，形容枯槁，衣衫褴褛，谁都劝不回家。
莱拉在那段被许配的婚姻里也终日憔悴，最后忧郁而死。消息传到玛吉侬耳中，他赶到她的坟前，倒在墓侧也死去了。两人最终被一同安葬。这一对恋人的故事，从此在波斯与阿拉伯文学中流传，成为一段「唯有以死方得团圆」的爱情的永恒形象。

en: Dido was a princess of the Phoenician city of Tyre. After her brother Pygmalion murdered her wealthy husband for his gold, Dido fled by ship with a band of loyal followers and her late husband's treasure. She sailed across the Mediterranean and came to the coast of North Africa. There the local king offered her as much land as she could enclose with the hide of a single ox.

Dido cut the ox-hide into very thin strips and laid them end to end, drawing a great circle that took in a whole hill. On that ground she founded the city of Carthage, which grew into a prosperous trading power. Years later, the Trojan prince Aeneas was shipwrecked on her coast, and she gave him hospitality. The two of them fell in love.

But Aeneas had been told by the gods that he must found a new nation in Italy, and at last he set sail again, leaving her behind. Dido, abandoned, ordered a great funeral pyre to be built. She climbed onto it with Aeneas' sword and stabbed herself, and the smoke from the pyre was seen by Aeneas' ships as they sailed away. The hatred she swore in her last moments was remembered as the deep source of the long wars later fought between Carthage and Rome.
zh: 狄多是腓尼基城邦推罗的公主。她那位富有的丈夫被她兄长皮格马利翁为夺金财而暗杀后，狄多带着一群忠心的随从和亡夫的财宝乘船出逃。她横渡地中海，最终在北非海岸登陆。当地国王答应：她能用一张牛皮围出多大地方，就给她多少土地。
狄多把牛皮剪成极细的条，首尾相连，竟围出一整座山丘那么大的地方。她在这块地上建起了迦太基城，城邦后来发展成繁荣的商业强国。多年后，特洛伊王子埃涅阿斯遭遇海难漂到她的海岸，她收留款待。两人也因此相爱。
可是诸神早已告诉埃涅阿斯，他必须到意大利去建立一个新的民族，最后他还是扬帆离开，把她留在了岸边。被弃的狄多命人筑起一座高大的火葬柴堆。她攀上柴堆，拿起埃涅阿斯留下的剑刺向自己，火葬柴堆腾起的浓烟，被远去的埃涅阿斯船队望见。她临终所立下的诅咒，被后人记作了日后迦太基与罗马之间那场漫长战争的深远源头。

en: Among the Muisca people of the highlands of what is now Colombia, the king of Guatavita performed a remarkable ritual when he took the throne. He was rubbed with sticky resin and then covered all over with gold dust until his whole body shone. He was then carried out on a raft to the middle of Lake Guatavita, with offerings of gold and emeralds piled on the raft beside him.

There, at the center of the lake, he stepped into the water and washed the gold dust off into the lake, while his people threw the offerings in around him. The lake was understood to be sacred, and the gold was given to it as a gift to the gods. This ritual was practiced for centuries before the arrival of the Spanish.

When the Spanish heard of "the gilded one" — el dorado — they understood the story as proof that whole cities of gold lay hidden in the New World. Expedition after expedition pushed into the rainforests and mountains looking for the lost city. None of them ever found it. Lake Guatavita was eventually drained more than once in search of the treasure that lay on its floor, and El Dorado passed into language as the name for any dream of riches at the end of an impossible road.
zh: 在如今哥伦比亚高地的穆伊斯卡人中，瓜塔维塔的国王登基时要举行一场特殊的仪式。仪人先把粘性树脂涂遍他全身，再把他从头到脚撒上金粉，直到他通体金光熠熠。然后他被抬上一只木筏，划到瓜塔维塔湖的中央，木筏上还堆着供奉用的金器与绿宝石。
在湖心，他跳入水中，把身上的金粉一并洗入湖里；他的子民则把那些祭品一齐丢进他周围的湖水中。这座湖在他们眼中神圣无比，金银宝石便是献给湖中诸神的礼物。这套仪式在西班牙人到来之前已经流传了几个世纪。
西班牙人听到「那个被镀金的人」——el dorado——的传说后，便把它当作新大陆深处藏着整座金城的证据。一支又一支远征队深入雨林和山岭去寻找这座失落的城市，从来没有人真正找到它。瓜塔维塔湖也先后被人抽干过几次，希望在湖底找到那些祭品。后来「El Dorado」便成了一个语汇，专指那种在不可能的路尽头才有的财富之梦。

en: Among the Andean peoples, the great creator god Viracocha rose from Lake Titicaca in a time of darkness, before the present sun. He first made the sky and the earth, and then a race of giants of stone to live on the new earth. When these stone giants displeased him, he destroyed them with a great flood and turned them back into stone.

Then Viracocha made a new race of people, this time out of clay or, in some tellings, by painting them onto the rock. He shaped men and women of every nation, gave them their languages, their clothing, and their songs, and told them to spread out across the world. He commanded the sun, the moon, and the stars to rise from the lake to give them light.

After he had set the world in order, Viracocha himself walked across the land of the Andes, teaching the people farming, weaving, and the right way to live. He went down to the coast of the Pacific, and there he walked away across the surface of the sea, promising one day to return. He was remembered as the maker of everything, although later he often shared his honors with the sun god Inti at the heart of Inca religion.
zh: 在安第斯各民族的传说中，伟大的造物神维拉科查在尚无太阳的黑暗时代，从的的喀喀湖中升起。他先造出天空和大地，又在新造的大地上造出第一批石头巨人作为居民。后来这些石巨人惹他不快，他便降下一场大洪水将他们毁灭，把他们重新变回石头。
之后维拉科查再造一批人，这一次有的传说说是用泥土塑成，有的说他直接把人形画在岩石上。他塑出各民族的男男女女，赐予他们语言、衣裳和歌谣，命他们分散到世界各地。他又命太阳、月亮和群星从湖中升起，为这些人照明。
把世界安排妥当之后，维拉科查亲自走过安第斯高原，把农耕、纺织和处世之道教给人们。最后他下到太平洋海岸，在那里径直从海面上走开，承诺有一天会回来。后人把他记作万物的创造者，但在印加王朝盛期，他常与太阳神因蒂共同享有崇拜，居于印加宗教的核心。

en: In Inca tradition, the sun god Inti looked down on the dark and disordered world and decided to send help to humanity. He sent his son Manco Capac and his daughter Mama Ocllo up from the depths of Lake Titicaca with a golden staff. They were to walk across the land and look for a place where the staff would sink easily into the earth, for that place was meant to be the heart of a new kingdom.

The brother and sister walked north for a long time, testing the soil at many places. The staff would not sink anywhere they tried. At last they came to a green valley high in the mountains. There Manco Capac struck the staff into the ground, and it sank to its full length.

They stayed in that valley and gathered the people who lived nearby into a new community. Manco Capac taught the men to farm, irrigate, and build with stone, and Mama Ocllo taught the women to spin and weave. The city they founded at that spot became Cusco, the navel of the world to the Inca, and the seat of their empire for centuries to come.
zh: 印加传说中，太阳神因蒂俯瞰着黑暗混乱的人间，决定派人帮助人类。他从的的喀喀湖深处派出儿子曼科·卡帕克与女儿玛玛·奥克略，让他们带着一根金杖上到地面。他们要边走边试，找到一处金杖能轻易插进土里的地方，那里便是新王国的中心。
兄妹俩朝北走了很久，沿途多次试验。金杖在他们尝试过的每一处都插不下去。最后他们来到了一座高山中的青翠山谷。曼科·卡帕克把金杖向地上一击，整支金杖应声没入土中。
兄妹俩便留在这座山谷，把附近的部落汇聚成一个新的社群。曼科·卡帕克教男子耕作、灌溉、垒石筑屋，玛玛·奥克略教女子纺纱织布。他们立国的这处地方，就是后来的库斯科——印加人心目中的「世界肚脐」，此后数百年间一直是印加帝国的都城。

en: In the Maya book Popol Vuh, the hero twins Hunahpu and Xbalanque were called down to Xibalba, the underworld, by its lords. Their father and uncle had already gone there and been killed. The lords of Xibalba meant to do the same to the twins, and they set the brothers a series of cruel trials in dark houses — a house of cold, a house of jaguars, a house of bats, a house of fire, and a house of knives.

The twins used cleverness as much as strength. They tricked the bats, fooled the jaguars with bones, and survived the cold by sitting close to a hidden flame. In a great ball game against the lords of Xibalba, they played by night and by day, never losing. When one twin's head was cut off by a bat in the night house, they substituted a carved gourd and continued the game as if nothing had happened.

At last the twins let themselves be ground to powder and tossed into the river, knowing that the underworld lords could not really kill them. They returned five days later as wandering performers and tricked the lords into asking to be killed and reborn themselves. The lords were killed, but the twins did not bring them back. Xibalba's power was broken from then on, and Hunahpu and Xbalanque rose into the sky to become the sun and the moon.
zh: 玛雅《波波尔·乌》中讲述：英雄双子胡纳普与什巴兰克被冥界希瓦尔巴的众主召唤下界。两人的父亲和叔父早已去过那里，并被杀害。希瓦尔巴诸主也打算照样害死这对兄弟，便在一连串幽暗的屋子里给他们设下严酷考验——寒冷之屋、美洲虎之屋、蝙蝠之屋、火屋和刀屋。
双子靠机智不亚于靠勇力。他们骗过蝙蝠，用骨头骗过美洲虎，把自己挨着隐藏的火堆熬过了寒夜。他们与希瓦尔巴诸主对决球赛，无论白昼黑夜都不输一局。在蝙蝠屋中其中一人被夜里飞来的蝙蝠砍下了头，他们便雕了一只葫芦顶替，仿佛什么也没发生一样继续比赛。
最后双子任由自己被磨成粉投入河中，因为他们知道冥界诸主其实杀不了他们。五天之后，他们化作流浪艺人重返人间，使诈让希瓦尔巴诸主主动请求被「杀掉再复活」。诸主被杀，但双子并没有把他们带回来。从此希瓦尔巴的威权被打破，胡纳普与什巴兰克升上天空，化作了太阳与月亮。

en: Among the Iroquois of what is now eastern North America, the nations had long been at war with each other, with blood feuds and revenge killings between the Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, and Seneca. A prophet known as the Peacemaker came among them, carrying a message of a Great Law of Peace. He found in Hiawatha, a Mohawk speaker who had lost his daughters to violence, the voice that the nations would listen to.

The two of them traveled from village to village. At each council fire, Hiawatha spoke for the Peacemaker's vision: that the five nations should bury their weapons under a great white pine, share one council, and stand together as a longhouse with many fires. They had to win over the fierce sorcerer Tadodaho of the Onondaga, whose hair was thought to be tangled with snakes and whose mind was tangled with anger.

After long persuasion, Hiawatha combed Tadodaho's hair smooth and unbound his mind. Tadodaho accepted the Great Law, and the five nations became the Haudenosaunee, the People of the Longhouse — what later was called the Iroquois Confederacy. The Confederacy held the peace among its members for generations, and the story of Hiawatha and the Peacemaker remained the founding memory of how it had been made.
zh: 在如今北美东部的易洛魁诸族中，部落彼此长期争战，莫霍克、奥奈达、奥农达加、卡尤加和塞内卡之间血亲复仇连年不断。一位被人称为「和平使者」的先知出现在他们中间，传达「伟大和平之律」的讯息。他在一位名叫海华沙的莫霍克演说者身上找到了让各族肯听进去的声音——海华沙的女儿们正是死于这种血亲复仇。
两人一同从村庄走到村庄。在每一处议事火堆前，海华沙都替和平使者发言：五族应把武器埋在一棵巨大的白松树下，共聚一处议事火堆，犹如一座长屋之中燃着多团炉火，并肩而立。他们必须先说服奥农达加部落里那位凶悍的巫师塔多达霍——传说他的头发里缠着蛇，他的心思也因怒火而盘结。
经过反复劝说，海华沙替塔多达霍把头发梳直，也把他心中的怨结一并解开。塔多达霍接受了「伟大和平之律」，五族从此结成「豪德诺索尼」，意为「长屋之民」——即后世所说的易洛魁联盟。这一联盟在成员之间维持了多个世代的和平，海华沙与和平使者促成联盟的这段故事，也成了它最深的立国记忆。

en: Maui was a demigod of the Polynesian peoples, known for his cunning and his many feats. One day he went out fishing with his older brothers, who tried to leave him behind because they thought he was useless on the canoe. Maui hid in the bottom of the boat until they were far out at sea. When he revealed himself, they were too far from shore to turn back, and he insisted on joining the fishing.

Maui took out a magical fishhook made from the jawbone of his grandmother. He cast it into the deep water and pulled, and to his brothers' astonishment the line went taut as if it had caught the bottom of the sea itself. Maui chanted and hauled, and the brothers paddled with all their strength, and slowly a great mass rose from the depths.

What rose from the sea was not a fish but the land — the first of the islands of the Pacific. Maui warned his brothers not to walk on it until the proper rituals were done, but they could not wait. They began cutting and tearing pieces of the new land for themselves, and that is why the islands have their rough, broken shape today, with mountains, valleys, and ridges instead of one smooth surface. The story is told across Polynesia, with each island the catch of Maui's hook.
zh: 毛伊是波利尼西亚诸民族中的半神，以机智和无数英勇事迹著称。有一天他随兄长们出海打渔，兄长们嫌他在独木舟上没用，想把他留在岸上。毛伊偷偷藏在船底，直到他们划得远离海岸才现身。这时已经回不去了，他坚持要一起打渔。
毛伊取出一只用祖母颌骨做成的魔法鱼钩，把它沉入深海，奋力一拉。鱼线一下子紧绷起来，仿佛勾住了海底本身，把兄长们吓了一跳。毛伊念起咒歌不停拉拽，兄长们也全力划桨，一团庞大的物体慢慢从海底浮升上来。
从海中升起的不是鱼，而是陆地——太平洋上的第一座岛屿。毛伊提醒兄长们，要等仪式完成才能在新土上行走，他们却等不及，开始你抢一块、我撕一块地割起新地来。今日太平洋诸岛之所以有起伏的山脉、深谷与岭脊，而不是一整片平坦的陆地，便是因此。这则故事在波利尼西亚各处流传，每一座海岛都被说成是毛伊鱼钩的渔获。

en: In the early days, the people of the islands complained that the sun moved too quickly across the sky. The days were too short for them to fish, to cook, or to dry their tapa cloth. The hero Maui heard their complaint and decided to do something about it. He climbed up to the place from which the sun rose, taking with him long ropes woven from coconut fiber, and in some tellings from his sister's hair.

He stretched the ropes into a net of snares around the rising place. When the sun came up at its usual rate of speed, its rays were caught in the snares one after another. Maui sprang out and held the sun fast, beating it with a club.

The sun begged for its life. Maui made it swear an oath: from that day on it would travel more slowly across the sky, especially in the warm half of the year, so that the people would have time enough for the work of the day. The sun agreed, and Maui released it. The longer days that followed were taken as proof that he had really tamed it, and to the islanders he became the hero who had slowed the day for them.
zh: 古早的时候，岛上的人抱怨太阳行过天空走得太快，白天太短，他们既来不及打渔、生火做饭，也来不及晒干树皮布。英雄毛伊听到大家的怨言，决定动手解决。他带上椰子纤维编成的长绳——也有说是他姐姐头发编成的——攀上太阳升起的地方。
他把那些长绳张开，沿着日出的位置布下一张套圈大网。太阳照常以一贯的速度升起，光芒一道道被这些套圈卡住。毛伊一跃而出，把太阳死死按住，举棒猛打。
太阳苦苦求饶。毛伊要它立下誓言：从今往后必须在天空中走得慢一些，尤其在一年中的温暖时节，好让人们有足够的白昼时间干活。太阳答应了，毛伊才把它放走。之后的白日的确比过去更长，岛上的人便把这视为毛伊真的驯住太阳的证据，把他当作替众人放慢白天的英雄。

en: In Maori tradition, Ranginui the sky father and Papatuanuku the earth mother lay locked in a tight embrace at the beginning of the world. Their children lived between them in a narrow, dark, cramped space. The children grew tired of the darkness and discussed what to do.

Some wanted to kill their parents and force light into the world, but the war god Tumatauenga did not have his way. Tane, the god of forests and birds, proposed instead to push their parents apart. The brothers tried one by one and failed. When Tane's turn came, he lay on his back, set his shoulders against the earth and his feet against the sky, and pushed with all his strength.

Slowly Ranginui was forced upward, and Papatuanuku stayed below. Light flowed into the world for the first time, and the trees, plants, and birds that Tane fathered could grow on the open earth. The sky father still weeps for his wife from time to time, and his tears are the rain and the dew. The earth mother, in her sorrow, sometimes sighs upward, and her mists are the morning fog rising from the land.
zh: 毛利人的传说中，在世界之初，天父朗吉努伊与地母帕帕图阿努库紧紧相拥而卧。他们的儿女们就被夹在两人之间，挤在一片狭窄、黑暗、令人窒息的空间里。子女们慢慢厌倦了这种永远的黑暗，开始商量该怎么办。
有人主张把父母杀掉，让光从此涌进世界，但战神图玛塔乌恩加的主张并未占上风。森林与飞鸟之神塔内提出，不如把父母推开。兄弟们一个一个试，都没能撼动两人。轮到塔内时，他仰躺在地，肩抵大地，双脚顶住天空，用尽全力向上推。
天父朗吉努伊渐渐被顶向高处，地母帕帕图阿努库则留在了下方。光第一次涌入世界，塔内所生养的树木、花草和飞鸟，从此可以在敞开的大地上生长。天父至今偶尔仍为远离的妻子落泪，他的泪便是雨和露珠。地母在悲伤中也常向上叹息，她吐出的气息便是早晨从地面升起的薄雾。

en: In many traditions of Aboriginal Australia, the Rainbow Serpent is a great being who shaped the land during the Dreaming, the time when the world was being made. In the beginning the earth lay flat and dry. The Serpent came up out of the ground and pushed its way across the country, carving rivers, valleys, and gullies as it moved.

Wherever the Serpent stopped to rest, deep waterholes were left in the ground. Wherever it lifted itself into the sky after a storm, its long arching body appeared as the rainbow. Different peoples in different parts of Australia know it under different names, and tell different stories about its journeys, but its connection to fresh water, rain, and the shape of country is the same.

The Rainbow Serpent watches over those waterholes and the people whose country it has crossed. To approach a sacred waterhole carelessly, or to break the laws connected with it, is to risk waking the Serpent's anger. The story is one of the oldest continuously told stories in the world, repeated in many languages across the continent.
zh: 在澳大利亚原住民众多传统中，「彩虹蛇」是一个伟大的存在，它在「梦时代」——世界正在被塑造的时代——里塑造了这片大地。最初大地平坦而干燥。彩虹蛇从地里钻出来，缓缓穿过整个大陆，所到之处便雕出河流、峡谷和深沟。
彩虹蛇歇息的每一个地方，都会在地上留下一处深深的水潭。雨过天晴时，它从大地腾入天空，长长拱起的身躯就是天上的彩虹。澳洲不同地方的人对它有不同的称呼，对它的旅程也有各自不同的讲法，但它与淡水、雨水以及大地形貌之间的联系，却是各地共通的。
彩虹蛇守护着那些水潭，也守护着自己穿过的那片土地上的人。若有人不知敬畏地靠近圣水潭，或违背与之相连的禁忌，就有可能惹动它的怒火。这则故事是世界上仍在被持续讲述的最古老的故事之一，用各种语言在整片大陆上代代相传。

en: Pele, the goddess of fire and volcanoes, came to Hawaii from a far southern homeland after a quarrel with her elder sister, the sea goddess Namakaokahai. Where Pele tried to dig fire pits, her sister would send waves to put them out. Pele moved from one island to another, digging on Kauai, then Oahu, then Molokai and Maui, each time chased on by her sister's flood.

At last she reached the island of Hawaii, the largest, and dug her great pit on the slope of Mauna Loa, where the sea could not reach. She made her home in the crater of Kilauea. From there she shaped the islands with new flows of lava, each eruption adding more land at the southern end of the archipelago.

Pele's name became inseparable from the volcanoes themselves. Lava that flows down the mountain is her hair; the steam that rises from the cliffs is her breath. Other gods, lovers, and rivals appear in many stories about her, especially her sister Hi'iaka and the warrior Lohi'au, but at the center remains the goddess who carries the fire that is still building the islands today.
zh: 火与火山的女神佩蕾出身于一个遥远的南方家乡，因与姐姐——海洋女神娜玛卡奥卡海发生争执，逃到了夏威夷。她每挖出一处火坑，姐姐就涌起海浪把它浇灭。佩蕾只好一座岛一座岛地走，先到考艾岛，又到瓦胡岛、莫洛凯岛、毛伊岛，每次都被姐姐的海水追逼着继续往前。
最后她到达群岛中最大的夏威夷岛，在莫纳罗亚火山的山腰上挖出一座巨大的火坑，那是海水再也够不到的地方。她在基拉韦厄火山口安下家。从此她不断喷发熔岩，塑造群岛的形状，每一次喷发都让岛链南端多出一片新的土地。
佩蕾的名字从此与这些火山再难分开。沿山而下的熔岩流是她的长发，崖边升腾的水汽是她的气息。在许多关于她的故事里还出现了别的神祇、情人和对手，尤其是她的妹妹希伊阿卡与勇士罗希阿乌，但故事的中心始终是这位带着火焰、至今仍在塑造群岛的女神。

en: When Krishna was a boy in the village of Vrindavan, the deep pool in the river Yamuna was poisoned by Kaliya, a many-headed serpent who lived in it with his wives. The water there had become black, and birds that flew over the pool fell dead. The cowherds could no longer water their cattle, and the people grieved.

One day Krishna's playmates' ball fell into the poisoned pool. Krishna climbed a tree on the bank, dove into the water, and disturbed Kaliya's rest. The great serpent rose with all his hoods spread, wrapped his coils around the boy, and tried to crush him. Krishna grew larger and larger until Kaliya had to let him go.

Then Krishna leapt onto Kaliya's hoods and danced. He moved from one head to another, beating each one down with his heels in time to a music that only the gods could hear. Bleeding and exhausted, Kaliya finally surrendered. His wives begged Krishna to spare him. Krishna agreed on the condition that the serpent leave the Yamuna; Kaliya and his family slithered away to the sea, and the water of the river ran clean again.
zh: 黑天小时候住在牧村芬陀梵，亚穆纳河中有一处深潭，被住在那里的多头大蛇卡利亚和它的众妻毒得乌黑。潭水黑得不能饮，连飞过潭上的鸟雀都坠地而亡。牧人不敢再让牛群到那里饮水，村中愁云密布。
有一天黑天与同伴玩耍，球落进了那处毒潭。黑天爬到岸边的一棵树上，纵身跃入潭中，惊动了正在歇息的卡利亚。大蛇张开全部蛇头浮出水面，把孩子用蛇身紧紧缠住，想把他绞死。黑天却越涨越大，最后卡利亚不得不松开他。
黑天随即跃上卡利亚的众头之上跳起舞来。他从一头跳到另一头，每跨一步，脚跟都重重踏下，节奏暗合只有诸神才能听见的天乐。卡利亚被他踩得鲜血淋漓、精疲力竭，终于屈服。它的众妻苦苦哀求黑天饶命。黑天答应了，但要它离开亚穆纳河。卡利亚带着家眷游入大海，那条河的水从此重新清澈。

en: The gods had grown weak, and the demons had begun to defeat them. Vishnu advised the two sides to join forces just long enough to churn the great milky ocean and draw out the elixir of immortality, amrita, that lay hidden in its depths. They agreed to a truce. They used Mount Mandara as a churning pole and the cosmic serpent Vasuki as a rope wrapped around it; the demons took one end of the snake and the gods took the other.

When they began to churn, the mountain threatened to sink into the soft floor of the ocean. Vishnu took the form of a giant turtle and dove beneath the mountain to hold it up. As they churned, many wonders rose to the surface — the moon, a divine horse, a wishing tree, the goddess of fortune Lakshmi, and a deadly poison that threatened to destroy all creation, which Shiva drank and held in his throat to save the world.

At last the divine physician Dhanvantari rose from the ocean, holding a pot of amrita. The demons seized it first, but Vishnu, in the form of the beautiful Mohini, tricked them into letting her serve the drink. She gave the elixir only to the gods. By the time the demons realized the trick, the gods had become immortal and able to defeat them. The truce ended, and from that day on the gods kept their place at the head of creation.
zh: 诸神已经衰弱，阿修罗渐渐占了上风。毗湿奴出主意：让双方暂时联手搅动那片巨大的乳海，把藏在海底的不死甘露「阿姆利达」搅出来。两方答应休战。他们以曼陀罗山为搅杵，以宇宙巨蛇婆苏吉为绳缠在山上；阿修罗握住蛇的一端，众神握住另一端。
刚开始搅动，山就有沉入乳海软底的危险。毗湿奴化作一只巨大的乌龟潜到山下，把山顶住。搅动之间，海面上接连浮起一件件奇物——月亮、神驹、如愿树、吉祥女神拉克什米，还有足以毁灭万物的剧毒。剧毒之事是湿婆挺身将之喝下、把毒留在自己喉中，才保住了世界。
最后，神医旦文陀利从乳海中升起，手中托着一只盛着甘露的钵。阿修罗抢先夺到了它。毗湿奴便化作绝美的莫希尼，骗他们让自己来分酒。她只把甘露送给诸神。等阿修罗看出受骗，众神早已喝下甘露成了不死之身，再次将他们打败。休战至此结束，从那一天起，诸神在世界秩序里重新稳稳坐在头位。

en: Malin Kundang was the only son of a poor widow in a village on the coast of Sumatra. His mother raised him alone after his father had left to find work and never returned. When Malin grew up he asked his mother for permission to sail away on a trading ship, hoping to make his fortune. She wept, but let him go.

Years later, Malin returned in command of a great trading vessel, his clothes fine and his wife noble and beautiful. The old woman ran to the harbor and threw her arms around him in front of his crew. Ashamed of her ragged appearance before his new wife, Malin refused to acknowledge her. He pushed her aside and said she was no mother of his.

His mother stood on the dock and called upon God to judge him. As the ship sailed out, a great storm rose without warning. The ship was thrown back onto the rocks of the coast. Malin himself, in the moment he tried to repent, was turned to stone — and the rock formation said to be his shape can still be seen on the shore in West Sumatra, kneeling, with his hands raised toward the village.
zh: 马林·昆当是苏门答腊海边一个小村里穷寡妇的独子。父亲早年出海讨生活，再没有回来，母亲一人含辛茹苦把他拉扯大。马林长大后向母亲请求，让他随一艘商船出海闯荡，去搏一份家业。母亲流着泪应允了。
多年后，马林作为商船船长衣锦归来，穿着华丽的衣服，妻子也是名门贵女。老母听说儿子回来了，连忙赶到码头，当着满船人扑上去抱住他。马林见母亲衣衫褴褛，怕在新婚妻子面前丢脸，便不肯认她，反将她推开，说她不是自己的母亲。
母亲伫立在码头上，呼告苍天为她做主。商船刚出港，海上忽然刮起一场莫名的大风暴。船被风浪掀回岸边，撞在礁石上。马林想要在最后一刻悔过，整个人却已经化作了石头——苏门答腊西部的海岸至今还能看到一块石头，被认为正是他下跪、双手向村庄方向高举的样子。

en: Tam was the daughter of a kindly farmer, and Cam was her half-sister, the daughter of her cruel stepmother. After Tam's father died, the stepmother and Cam loaded all the work onto Tam and treated her badly. Once they sent the two girls to catch shrimp; Cam pretended she would lose her share and tricked Tam out of all the day's catch, leaving Tam only a single small fish. Tam kept the fish in the well as a friend.

When the stepmother and Cam found out about the fish, they killed and ate it. Tam wept, and a heavenly being came down and told her to gather the bones and bury them. From the bones came a beautiful set of clothes and golden slippers when she most needed them. A passing king found one of the slippers, fell in love with the girl who fit it, and married Tam, taking her away from the cruel household.

But Cam and the stepmother were not done. They lured Tam back, killed her, and put Cam in her place at the palace. Tam returned again and again — as a yellow bird, as a bamboo tree, as a fruit hidden in an old woman's house. In the end the king found her again in the old woman's hut, and Cam and her mother were punished. The tale is the Vietnamese cousin of Cinderella, with a longer struggle and a harder revenge.
zh: 塔姆是一位好心农夫的女儿，卡姆是她同父异母的妹妹，是她那位刻薄继母的女儿。父亲去世以后，继母把所有家务都压在塔姆头上，对她极为苛刻。一次继母让两人去捉小虾，卡姆假装自己的篮子空了，趁机把塔姆一天捉到的虾全骗了去，只给她留下一条小鱼。塔姆把那条鱼养在井里，当作朋友。
继母和卡姆得知井里的鱼后，把它捞出来吃掉了。塔姆痛哭，一位天上来的神灵下凡告诉她，把鱼骨收起来埋好。后来，每当塔姆最需要时，那些鱼骨就化出一身华美的衣裳和一双金鞋。一位巡游的国王捡到了其中一只金鞋，立誓要娶能穿上这只鞋的姑娘，最终找到塔姆，娶她为妻，把她从那个刻薄的家中接走。
可卡姆和继母并未就此罢休。她们设计骗塔姆回家，将她害死，让卡姆顶替她回到宫中。塔姆却一次次回到人间——先化作一只黄莺，又化作一棵青竹，再变成藏在一位老婆婆家里的一颗果子。国王最终在老婆婆的茅屋中再度认出她，卡姆和母亲也得到了应有的惩罚。这则故事是越南版的「灰姑娘」，斗争更漫长，复仇也更狠。

en: King Hung had a daughter named My Nuong, famous for her beauty. When she came of age he announced that he would give her in marriage. Two suitors arrived on the same day, both more than mortal. Son Tinh was the spirit of the mountain, who could raise hills and forests by a wave of his hand. Thuy Tinh was the spirit of the water, who could call up rivers and storms.

Unable to choose between them, the king said that whichever of the two could bring the proper bride gifts first would marry his daughter. The gifts were to include a nine-tusked elephant, a nine-spurred chicken, and a nine-maned horse. Son Tinh, the mountain spirit, arrived at dawn with everything ready. He was given My Nuong and led her away to his mountain.

Thuy Tinh came soon after, found that he had lost her, and was furious. He called up the waters of all the rivers to drown the mountain. Son Tinh raised the hills higher to keep ahead of the flood. The two spirits battled until the water finally fell back, but Thuy Tinh has not given up. The story says that every summer he returns to attack the mountain again, and that is why Vietnam still suffers floods at that season.
zh: 雄王有一个女儿叫媚娘，远近闻名地美丽。她成年之后，雄王宣布要为她择婿。同一天有两位求亲者前来，皆非凡人。山精「山神」一挥手就能令山林隆起；水精「水神」一抬手便可召来河水和风暴。
雄王难以取舍，便宣布：哪一位先按规矩送来聘礼，就把女儿许给谁。聘礼要包含九齿象、九距鸡和九鬃马。山神在天亮时分把全部聘礼整齐备好送到，雄王便把媚娘许给了山神，山神领着她回到自己的群山之中。
水神不久也赶到了，发现自己已经失了佳人，怒不可遏。他召动天下河川之水，要淹没群山。山神则把山岭一座座向上推高，让大水始终漫不到山顶。两位精灵激战许久，洪水才终于退去。可水神并不甘心，传说每年到了夏天，他都会再次回来攻打群山——越南每到那一时节频发洪涝，正源自这一段旧怨。

en: Tristan, a knight of King Mark of Cornwall, was sent across the sea to Ireland to fetch the princess Isolde, who had been promised in marriage to his king. On the voyage back, the two of them, by accident, drank a love potion that had been prepared for Mark and his bride. Bound to each other from that moment, they could not stop loving even after Isolde was married to Mark.

Their love continued in secret at the court of Cornwall. Spies tried to catch them, and twice they were nearly killed; once they fled into the forest and lived there for a long time. King Mark eventually discovered the truth but could not bring himself to destroy them. Tristan was sent into exile.

Far from Cornwall, Tristan married another woman named Isolde of the White Hands, but he never loved her. When he was mortally wounded, he sent for the first Isolde, asking the messenger to raise a white sail if she came and a black sail if she did not. His wife, in jealousy, told him the sail was black. Tristan died of grief, and when the first Isolde at last reached him she lay down beside him and died as well. The tale of their love became one of the great stories of medieval Europe.
zh: 特里斯坦是康沃尔国王马克麾下的骑士，奉命渡海前往爱尔兰，去把已经许配给国王的伊索德公主接回来。归途中，两人误饮了一只本来准备给马克王和新娘喝的爱情药酒。自那一刻起，两人的心被无形的力量绑在一起，即使伊索德嫁给了马克王，他们也无法不再相爱。
他们的恋情在康沃尔宫廷中暗暗延续。侍臣四处侦察想抓他们的把柄，两人几度险被处死；一次他们干脆逃进森林，过了很长一段山林生活。马克王最终知道了真相，却下不了狠心要他们的命，只把特里斯坦放逐他乡。
特里斯坦远走他国，娶了另一个名叫「白手伊索德」的女子，但他从未真正爱过她。后来他身负致命重伤，便派人去请第一位伊索德，嘱咐使者：若她肯来，就在归航的船上挂白帆；若不肯，就挂黑帆。他的妻子出于妒嫉，告诉他帆是黑色的。特里斯坦闻言绝望而死。第一位伊索德赶到时见他已逝，倒在他身旁也随之去世。这一对恋人的故事，从此成为中世纪欧洲最广为流传的爱情传奇之一。

en: King Lir of the Tuatha De Danann had four beloved children — three sons and a daughter — by his first wife. After she died, he married her sister Aoife, and Aoife grew jealous of how much Lir loved his children. One day she took them to a lake, and there she struck them with a druid's wand and turned them into four white swans. She gave them one mercy: they would keep their human voices and their human minds.

Their punishment was to spend nine hundred years as swans — three hundred on the lake where they had been changed, three hundred on a stormy sea, and three hundred in another desolate water. They sang the loveliest music in Ireland from those waters, but they were forbidden to come ashore. Their father grieved and could not undo the spell.

At the end of the long nine hundred years, the swans came back to the shore. By then the old gods had gone, and Ireland had received the Christian faith. A holy man heard their music and welcomed them. As they took human form again, they were already very old, and they died at once after the bell of the chapel rang over them. He buried the four of them together in one grave, where they had finally found rest.
zh: 图哈德达南族的国王利尔与原配生有四个心爱的儿女——三子一女。原配去世后，他续娶了她的妹妹埃菲。埃菲见利尔对孩子们爱得入骨，妒火渐盛。某日她带着四个孩子到湖边，挥起一根德鲁伊魔杖把他们变成了四只白天鹅。她只留下一点恩典：他们保有人的声音和人的心智。
他们注定要做九百年的天鹅——前三百年留在被变身的那座湖上，再三百年漂泊在风浪汹涌的海上，最后三百年又困在另一片荒凉的水域。他们在湖海上唱出爱尔兰最动人的歌声，却被禁止上岸。利尔伤痛不已，却无力解咒。
漫长的九百年终于走到尽头，四只天鹅回到岸边。那时旧神早已远去，爱尔兰已迎来了基督的信仰。一位圣者听见他们的歌声，便把他们迎接过来。等他们重新化回人形时，已经苍老至极，礼拜堂的钟声在他们头顶响起时，他们立刻一齐逝去。圣者把四人合葬于同一座坟茔，他们终于得享安息。

en: When the Connacht queen Medb invaded Ulster to seize the great brown bull of Cooley, the warriors of Ulster lay under a curse that left them sick and helpless. Only Cu Chulainn, a young hero who was not under the curse, was left to defend the country. He rode out alone and met the invading army at a series of fords on the border.

By the rules of single combat that Medb's army respected, Cu Chulainn could face one warrior at a time at the ford. Day after day he fought and killed each champion the queen sent against him. He went without proper sleep, and his wounds were dressed each night by his charioteer.

When Medb sent his old friend and foster-brother Ferdiad against him, the two of them fought for days at the ford, weeping when they rested and praising each other's courage. In the end Cu Chulainn killed Ferdiad with a secret weapon, the Gae Bolg, and held him in his arms as he died. By the time the Ulster warriors recovered from the curse, Cu Chulainn was nearly dead himself, but he had held the army of Connacht at the fords long enough to save his country.
zh: 康诺特女王梅芙率军入侵阿尔斯特，要夺取库利的褐色大公牛。阿尔斯特的勇士此时正陷于一道古老的诅咒之中，浑身衰弱不能动弹。整片国土只剩下年轻英雄库胡林尚不在诅咒之列，能挺身出战。他独自骑马驰出，到边境一连串浅滩前迎击大军。
按梅芙军中所遵的单挑规矩，库胡林每次只用面对一名勇士。日复一日，他在浅滩上接连击杀女王派出的每一位将领。他几乎不能好好歇息，每晚由车夫替他重新包扎伤口。
梅芙最后派出库胡林的童年挚友、义兄弗迪亚德。两人在浅滩上鏖战数日，间歇时彼此泪流满面，互相称赞对方的勇武。最后库胡林用一件名为「迦伊伯尔加」的秘藏神器把弗迪亚德刺死，把他抱在怀里咽气。等阿尔斯特勇士们终于从诅咒中恢复时，库胡林本人也几乎垂死，但他已在浅滩前死死拖住了康诺特大军，保住了自己的国土。

en: When Fionn mac Cumhaill was still a boy, he was sent to study with the old druid and poet Finnegas, who lived on the bank of the river Boyne. Finnegas had spent years trying to catch the Salmon of Wisdom, a salmon that had eaten the nine hazelnuts of knowledge from the trees beside a sacred pool. Whoever ate the salmon first would gain all the wisdom of the world.

At last Finnegas caught the salmon. He gave it to the boy to cook for him and warned him not to taste a single bite. Fionn turned the fish over the fire. A blister rose on the salmon's skin, and Fionn, without thinking, pressed it with his thumb to flatten it. His thumb was burned, and he stuck it into his mouth to ease the pain.

When Fionn brought the cooked salmon to his master, Finnegas saw the change in his eyes. The wisdom of the salmon had passed into the boy with that single touch. Finnegas, after a moment, gave him the rest of the fish to eat himself. From then on, whenever Fionn needed knowledge of something hidden, he had only to put his thumb in his mouth and bite, and the answer would come to him.
zh: 芬恩·麦克库尔还是少年时，被送去跟随住在博因河畔的老德鲁伊兼诗人芬尼戈斯学艺。芬尼戈斯已花了多年工夫，想钓到「智慧鲑鱼」——那条鲑鱼吃下了圣潭旁九棵榛树上九颗结着智慧的榛果。谁第一个尝到这条鱼的肉，谁就能得到天下所有的智慧。
终于有一天，芬尼戈斯钓上了那条鱼。他把鱼交给芬恩去烤，再三叮嘱他一口也不能尝。芬恩在火上翻动鱼身，鱼皮上忽然鼓起一个泡，他下意识地伸出拇指把它按平。拇指被烫到，他疼得把拇指含进嘴里吮了一下。
芬恩把烤好的鱼端给师父时，芬尼戈斯一看他的眼神就知道有变。智慧鲑鱼的本领，已经随着那一下烫伤进了少年的体内。芬尼戈斯沉默片刻，便把余下的鱼让他自己吃完。从此以后，芬恩每次想知道隐秘之事，只要把拇指放进嘴里一咬，答案便会自然浮现在他心中。

en: In the early days of Krakow, a dragon was said to live in a cave at the foot of Wawel Hill, by the river Vistula. It came out at night to carry off sheep, cattle, and sometimes young women from the surrounding country. King Krak called for someone to kill it, offering his daughter and his throne as a reward. Knight after knight tried and was burned to death in the dragon's flame.

At last a young shoemaker's apprentice named Dratewka — or in some tellings the wise Skuba — came forward with a humbler plan. He took the carcass of a sheep, slit it open, and stuffed it full of sulfur and pitch. He sewed it up to look like a healthy animal again and set it on the path the dragon used to come out to feed.

The dragon ate the prepared sheep whole, as was its habit. The sulfur soon began to burn its insides, and a terrible thirst came over it. It rushed down to the Vistula and drank, and drank, and went on drinking until at last it burst. The shoemaker was given the king's daughter and a place in the city's memory, and Wawel Hill kept the dragon's cave as a story passed on by every guide of Krakow.
zh: 克拉科夫城刚兴起的时候，传说瓦维尔山脚、维斯瓦河旁的一处洞穴里住着一条恶龙。它夜里出来掳走附近的羊、牛，有时连年轻女子也一并带走。国王克拉克下令谁能除掉这条龙，就把女儿和王位一并赏给他。一位又一位骑士前去挑战，都被龙吐出的烈焰烧死。
最后，一个名叫德拉特夫卡的年轻鞋匠学徒（也有版本说是聪明的斯库巴）站了出来，他的法子并不那么英雄。他取来一只死羊，将羊腹剖开，把硫磺和沥青塞满羊身，再把皮缝起来，让它看上去像一只健康的羊，丢在恶龙惯常出洞觅食的小路上。
恶龙照旧把那只「肥羊」整只吞下。硫磺很快在它腹中烧起来，剧渴让它无法忍受。它冲下山到维斯瓦河边大口饮水，一直喝，一直喝，最终肚子胀裂而死。鞋匠如愿娶到国王的女儿，并在这座城市的记忆中占了一席之地。瓦维尔山下那处龙洞，至今仍由克拉科夫的导游们一代代讲述着这个故事。

en: When Vasilisa's mother was dying, she gave her daughter a small doll and told her always to keep it close and never to show it to anyone. After her mother died, Vasilisa's father married a cruel widow with two daughters of her own. The stepmother and stepsisters loaded all the housework on Vasilisa. One evening they put out every fire in the house and sent her into the dark forest to fetch fire from Baba Yaga.

Vasilisa walked through the forest with her doll in her pocket. She came to a hut on chicken legs surrounded by a fence of skulls with glowing eyes. Baba Yaga arrived in her mortar and said the girl could have her fire only if she did the impossible chores set for her — sorting heaps of grain, cleaning the whole house, cooking for the witch. Each night Vasilisa shared her bread with the doll, and the doll did the work for her while she slept.

When Baba Yaga saw the chores done day after day, she asked how it was possible. Vasilisa answered that her mother's blessing helped her. The witch threw her out, saying she would have no blessing in her house, but gave her a skull with burning eyes on a stick to take home as fire. The skull's gaze burned the stepmother and her daughters to ashes when she arrived. With the help of her doll, Vasilisa lived on free and was eventually taken into the Tsar's household as his bride.
zh: 瓦西丽莎的母亲临终前送给女儿一个小布偶，叮嘱她要随身带着，永远不许示人。母亲死后，父亲又娶了一位狠毒的寡妇，对方还带着两个女儿。继母和继姐把所有家务都压在瓦西丽莎身上。一天傍晚，她们把家中所有的火都灭掉，逼她到漆黑的森林里去向芭芭雅嘎讨火。
瓦西丽莎揣着小布偶走进林子，看到一座长着鸡脚的小屋，四周围着一圈眼孔会发光的骷髅篱笆。芭芭雅嘎乘着石臼飞回来，告诉她想要火，就得替自己做完那些做不完的活——分拣谷物堆、把整座屋子打扫干净、为巫婆烹饪。每晚瓦西丽莎都把面包分给小布偶，小布偶便在她睡着时替她把活计完成。
芭芭雅嘎日复一日看着该做的活全都做完了，便问她是怎么做到的。瓦西丽莎说，是母亲的祝福在帮她。巫婆听了，立刻把她赶出门，说自己屋里容不下「带着祝福的人」，还递给她一支顶着燃烧眼睛骷髅的火把作为火种。瓦西丽莎回到家，那骷髅的目光把继母和继姐一齐烧成了灰烬。靠着小布偶的陪伴，瓦西丽莎自此自由地活下去，后来还被沙皇接进宫中做了王后。

en: Koschei the Deathless was a cruel old wizard in Russian folktale who could not be killed by ordinary means. His soul did not live inside his body. It was hidden inside a needle, which was inside an egg, which was inside a duck, which was inside a hare, which was locked in an iron chest buried under a great oak tree on the island of Buyan. As long as the needle was safe, Koschei could not die.

Koschei carried off a young princess and locked her in his tower, far from her husband. The hero — a prince, a young man, or in some versions Ivan Tsarevich — set out to find her. On his way he helped a wolf, an eagle, and a fish, who in return helped him reach Koschei's island and dig up the chest. The hare burst out of the chest, but the wolf caught it; the duck flew up out of the hare, but the eagle struck it down; the egg fell into the sea, but the fish brought it back.

The hero broke the egg, took out the needle, and snapped it in two. Far away in his tower, Koschei felt his death come at last and fell. The princess was freed and went home with her rescuer. The tale stayed in Russian memory as one of the great proofs that even a being who hides his soul cannot finally hide from those willing to chase him to the edge of the world.
zh: 「不死的科谢伊」是俄罗斯民间故事中一位狠毒的老巫师，普通办法无法杀死他。他的灵魂并不在自己身上，而是藏在一根针里，针又藏在一只蛋里，蛋在一只鸭子腹中，鸭子又在一只野兔的肚子里，兔子锁在一只铁箱中，铁箱埋在布扬岛上一棵巨大橡树下。只要那根针完好无损，科谢伊就死不了。
科谢伊把一位年轻公主掳走，关进他高远塔楼，让她远离丈夫。前去搭救她的英雄——王子、青年、有版本里则是伊万·察列维奇——一路上分别帮助了一只狼、一只鹰和一条鱼。三只动物为了报恩，又分别帮他到达科谢伊的岛屿、刨出那只铁箱。野兔从箱中蹿出，狼一口咬住；鸭子从兔腹中飞出，鹰俯冲将它击落；蛋落入海中，鱼又把蛋衔了回来。
英雄打破鸡蛋，取出那根针，咔嚓一声折成两段。远处塔楼里的科谢伊立时感到大限来到，应声倒下。公主被解救出来，与救她的英雄回到家中。这则故事在俄罗斯民间的记忆里反复被讲述，提醒人们：哪怕一个把灵魂藏起来的存在，也终究躲不过那些愿意追到世界尽头的人。

en: A tsar planted a garden of apple trees that bore golden apples, and one night the apples began to disappear. Watchmen could not stay awake to catch the thief. At last the tsar sent his three sons one by one to guard the tree. The two older princes slept through the night. Only the youngest, Ivan, stayed awake and saw the thief — the Firebird, with feathers that lit up the whole orchard.

He tried to seize the bird and managed to catch only a single glowing feather. The tsar sent the three brothers out into the world to find the rest of the bird. Ivan, helped by a gray wolf he met on the road, traveled through distant kingdoms. He nearly captured the Firebird at one court, then was sent to fetch a horse with a golden mane, and from there to win a beautiful princess, each time saved from disaster by the wolf's advice.

On the way home, his older brothers met him, killed him in his sleep, and took the bird, the horse, and the princess back to the tsar themselves. The wolf revived Ivan with water of life. He returned to court, exposed his brothers, and married the princess. The Firebird stayed at the tsar's court — proof, the storytellers said, that the brightest things in the world are not always to be kept, but they leave a feather to remember them by.
zh: 一位沙皇在园中栽下结金苹果的果树，可某天夜里金苹果开始一颗颗失踪。守夜的人怎么也熬不住瞌睡。最后沙皇让三个儿子轮流守果树。前两位王子都在夜里睡着了，唯独最小的伊凡撑过了一整夜，看到了真正的偷者——身上羽毛能照亮整座果园的火鸟。
他想抓住那只鸟，最终只扯下一根发光的羽毛。沙皇便派三兄弟分头出门去找完整的火鸟。伊凡在途中得到一只灰狼相助，走遍许多遥远的国度。他差点在一个王廷里抓住火鸟，又被派去取一匹金鬃马，再被派去赢得一位美丽公主，每一次都靠灰狼的提点才化险为夷。
在归途上，两位兄长追上熟睡的伊凡，把他杀死，把火鸟、骏马和公主一并带回宫中冒充功劳。灰狼用「生命之水」救活伊凡。伊凡回到宫中揭穿兄长，与公主成婚。火鸟则留在了沙皇的宫廷——讲故事的人说，这正是世间最明亮之物未必能据为己有的证明，但它至少会落下一根羽毛，让人记得它来过。

en: A stepmother in a Russian winter forced her husband to take his own daughter, a kind and patient girl, out into the freezing forest and leave her there to die. The man, helpless against his wife's anger, did as he was told. The girl sat down under a fir tree as the cold deepened.

Morozko, Father Frost himself, came striding through the trees, snapping his fingers and making the air still colder. He asked the girl, "Are you warm, my dear?" Though she was nearly freezing, she answered politely, "Yes, grandfather, I am warm." Three times he asked, each time freezing the air harder, and three times she answered with the same gentleness. Morozko, moved by her grace, wrapped her in furs, filled her sled with treasure, and sent her home.

When she returned alive and richly dressed, the stepmother decided that her own rude daughter should go and earn the same reward. Father Frost asked her in turn, "Are you warm, my dear?" The girl answered each time with rudeness and complaint. By the third question Morozko had grown weary of her, and he let the cold take her. The father came back from the forest with only her body. The first daughter kept the treasure, and the tale was told ever after as proof that gentleness even in cold lasts longer than greed.
zh: 俄罗斯的一个寒冬里，一位继母逼丈夫把自己温顺懂事的亲女儿赶到冰天雪地的森林深处，让她冻死。丈夫斗不过妻子的怒火，只好照办。少女在严寒中坐到了一棵杉树底下。
霜老人莫罗兹科——也就是「冰霜父亲」——大步穿过林间，弹响指间冷气，让空气变得更寒冷。他问少女：「孩子，你暖和吗？」少女虽冻得几乎僵硬，仍轻声答道：「暖和，祖父。」莫罗兹科一连问了三遍，每一遍空气都更冷，她每一遍都以同样的温顺回答。莫罗兹科被她的从容打动，给她披上皮裘，把雪橇装满珍宝，送她回了家。
少女活着回家，还带回满满的礼物。继母于是逼自己那位粗鲁的女儿也去森林里讨一份赏赐。霜老人也照样问她：「孩子，你暖和吗？」她每一次都以抱怨和粗话回应。问到第三遍时，莫罗兹科已对她不耐，便让寒气将她带走。父亲再从森林里回来时，只带回了她的尸体。前一位女儿守住了那份馈赠。这则故事此后一直被讲下去，提醒人们：寒冷中的温柔比贪婪走得更远。

en: Long ago, when the Lakota people were hungry, two scouts went out across the plains looking for game. They saw a young woman walking toward them through the grass, dressed in white. One of the scouts, struck by her beauty, thought of taking her for himself. He stepped toward her and was instantly enveloped in a strange cloud; when it cleared, only his bones lay on the ground. The other scout knew at once that she was holy.

The young woman told the surviving scout to go home and prepare the village to receive her. She came into the camp the next day carrying a bundle. She unwrapped it and gave the people the sacred pipe, the chanunpa. She showed them how to fill it with tobacco, how to light it, and how to offer the smoke to the four directions, to the sky, and to the earth. She taught them seven sacred rites that would guide their life as a people.

When she had finished, she walked back out across the prairie. As the people watched, she stopped, rolled over on the ground, and rose as a white buffalo calf. Then she changed colors — black, red, yellow — and at last disappeared from sight. Buffalo herds returned to the plains soon after, and the pipe she had given remained at the center of Lakota spiritual life for generations to come.
zh: 古时候，拉科塔人陷入饥荒，两名侦察者出去到大草原上寻找猎物。他们看到一位身穿白衣的年轻女子穿过草丛走来。其中一名侦察者被她的美貌打动，起了占有之心，朝她走过去。瞬间一团奇异的烟雾把他包住，烟散去后地上只剩一堆骨头。另一名侦察者立刻明白，她是神圣的。
这位女子吩咐活着的那位侦察者回村，让大家做好迎接她的准备。第二天她走进部落营地，怀里抱着一只包裹。她打开包裹，把神圣的烟斗「查努帕」交给拉科塔人，教他们如何装入烟草、如何点燃、如何向东南西北、向天向地献上烟气。她又传授他们七项神圣仪式，作为整个民族日后生活的指引。
讲完之后，她沿着原路走回草原。族人目送她远去，她忽然停下身，在草地上打了个滚，再起来时已化作一头小白野牛。接着她又变换颜色——黑、红、黄——最后从众人视野中消失。不久草原上的野牛群便重新回来。她留下的那只烟斗，世代相传，始终居于拉科塔精神生活的核心。

en: La Llorona, "the weeping woman," is a ghost said to wander at night by rivers and waterways in Mexico and across Latin America. In one common version, she was a beautiful young woman named Maria who married a wealthy man and bore him two children. Over time her husband grew cold to her and spent his attention elsewhere. In a fit of jealousy and rage, she drowned her own children in the river.

When she came to her senses and understood what she had done, she ran along the bank screaming, and at last drowned herself as well. But she was not allowed to rest. She was condemned to walk the earth, dressed in white and weeping, calling out for her lost children.

Parents in many towns warn their children not to go near rivers, lakes, or canals at night, because La Llorona may mistake them for her own and try to take them. People claim to have heard her cry from a distance — and to feel her grow nearer the louder she sounds. Her story stretches back to colonial Mexico and beyond, and continues to be told as a warning, a ghost story, and a song that mothers still know by heart.
zh: 「哭泣的女人」拉略罗娜是一个流传于墨西哥及整个拉美地区的鬼魂，相传她在夜里游荡于河岸与水道之间。常见的版本里，她原本是一位名叫玛丽亚的美丽女子，嫁给一位富有的丈夫，并为他生下了两个孩子。后来丈夫渐渐对她冷淡，把心思放到了别处。她在一场嫉妒与狂怒之中，竟亲手把两个孩子溺死在河中。
等她回过神来意识到自己做了什么时，她沿着河岸尖声哭号，最后自己也投河自尽。可她得不到安息，被判罚要在世间永远徘徊，身披白衣，啼哭不停，呼唤着失去的孩子。
许多小镇的父母告诫孩子，夜里不要靠近河流、湖泊或水沟，怕拉略罗娜把他们当作自己的孩子，从此把他们带走。有人说自己曾远远听到她的哭声——而且哭声越大反而越近。她的故事可一直追溯到西班牙殖民时期甚至更早，至今仍以一道警告、一段鬼故事和一首许多母亲熟记的歌谣的形式被讲述着。

en: Sundiata Keita was born to the king of the small kingdom of Mali and his second wife, a buffalo-woman from a distant land. As a child Sundiata could not walk, and his half-brothers and rivals at court mocked him. His mother bore the insults until at last she begged him to stand for her sake. Sundiata gripped an iron rod and pulled himself upright; the rod bent under his hands, and from that day he walked.

After his father's death, his rivals drove Sundiata, his mother, and his sister into exile. They lived for many years at other courts, where Sundiata grew into a great warrior and a careful judge of people. Meanwhile, the sorcerer-king Sumanguru of the Sosso conquered Mali and ruled it harshly. Refugees and griots traveled to find Sundiata and begged him to come back.

Sundiata gathered allies from many small kingdoms and at the battle of Kirina defeated Sumanguru, whose magic failed against him. He returned home as the founder of the Mali Empire, with twelve subject kings beneath him. He set out the Kouroukan Fouga, an oath of laws among his peoples, and is remembered in West Africa as the lion king whose long childhood weakness was the seed of his later greatness.
zh: 松迪亚塔·凯塔生于马里小国国王与第二位王后——来自远方的「水牛女」——之间。少年时代他不能走路，宫中的同父异母兄弟和敌对势力都暗中嘲笑他。他的母亲一直忍受讥讽，最后忍无可忍，恳求儿子站起来给她做主。松迪亚塔握住一根铁棒一把撑起身子，铁棒在他手下弯了下去，从那一天起他能行走了。
父王去世后，敌对一派把松迪亚塔、他的母亲与妹妹一齐赶出国境。他们在外国宫廷里辗转多年，松迪亚塔在那段岁月中长成了一位出色的战士，也练就了识人之眼。同一时期，索索的巫王苏曼古鲁征服了马里，对故土施以暴政。逃亡的难民与口传史诗的格里奥们辗转找到松迪亚塔，恳请他回去。
松迪亚塔联合多个小国的盟军，在基里纳之战中击败了苏曼古鲁——后者倚仗的巫术在他面前完全失灵。他回到故土，成为马里帝国的开国之君，麾下统辖十二个属国之王。他在「库鲁坎富加宪章」里立下民族间的盟誓与法度，被西非世代记为「狮王」——少年时的长久衰弱，反而成了他日后伟大的根。

en: An old childless couple prayed at a shrine for a baby of any size at all. They were granted a son who, when he was born, was no taller than a thumb. They named him Issun-boshi, "one-inch boy." Though he never grew taller, he was clever and brave. When he came of age, he set out for the capital with a sewing needle as his sword and a rice bowl as his boat.

He floated down the river in his bowl, using a chopstick as an oar, and at last reached the city. There he found work in the household of a noble lord. The lord's daughter took a liking to him because of his courage and wit. One day, while he was traveling with her, two ogres jumped out at them on a lonely road.

Issun-boshi rushed forward with his needle. One of the ogres swallowed him whole, but he stabbed the inside of the monster's stomach until the ogre vomited him out and fled. The other dropped a magic mallet as it ran. With each shake of the mallet Issun-boshi grew larger, until he stood at full human height. He went on to marry the lord's daughter and live as her husband at the court.
zh: 一对无子的老夫妇到神社祈求，说哪怕只有指节那么大的孩子也好。后来真的得到一个男婴，出生时不过拇指大小。他们给他取名「一寸法师」。他虽然始终未再长大，却聪慧勇敢。长大成人后，他以一根缝衣针为佩剑、一只饭碗为小船，独自启程上京。
他坐在饭碗里顺河漂流，用筷子当桨，终于到了京城。他在一位贵族家中谋得差事，主人的女儿欣赏他的胆识与机智。某日他陪小姐出门，行至一处偏僻的山路，突然冲出两个鬼怪。
一寸法师举针冲上前。一头鬼怪一口把他吞下，他便在鬼肚里用针猛刺，把鬼怪刺得呕出他来，落荒而逃。另一头鬼怪逃跑时遗落了一只「打出小槌」。每摇一次小槌，一寸法师的身体便大上一截，直到他变成一位寻常身量的成年男子。他后来娶了主人的女儿，从此以丈夫的身份留在那座宅邸里。

en: Kintaro, the "Golden Boy," was raised in the forests of Mount Ashigara by his mother, a woman who had fled the troubles of the capital. From his earliest years he was unusually strong, and his only playmates were the animals of the mountain. He wrestled bears and was always the winner; he could uproot small trees with his bare hands.

He spent his days roaming the slopes with a red-and-white badge on his chest and a great axe, leading the animals as if they were his band. He helped them across rivers, lifted boulders off injured deer, and judged their disputes. The animals called him their master, though he was only a child.

One day a passing samurai, the warrior Sakata Kintoki, saw the boy throw a giant carp out of a stream with one arm. He recognized at once that the child was meant for greater things. He brought him to the capital, where Kintaro entered the service of the lord Minamoto no Yorimitsu and became one of his famous Four Heavenly Kings. From the wilderness child grew one of Japan's best-known warriors.
zh: 「金太郎」自幼由从京城避乱出来的母亲抚养，长在足柄山的群山林间。他从小就力大无穷，山中的野兽便是他唯一的玩伴。他与熊摔跤总能取胜，又能徒手把小树连根拔起。
他每天背着一柄大斧，胸前挂着红白相间的腰布，在山坡上四处奔走，把山中野兽统领得像自家伙伴。他扶它们过河，把压在受伤梅花鹿身上的巨石搬开，又替它们裁断纷争。野兽们都把这个不过孩童年纪的人称作主人。
有一天，一位过路武士——坂田金时——看见这少年单臂将一条大鲤从溪中甩上岸，立刻明白他绝非池中物。武士把他带回京城，将他引荐给源赖光门下。金太郎从此追随源赖光，成为其麾下著名的「四天王」之一。一个山中长大的孩子，由此长成日本最为人传诵的勇士之一。

en: A kind old man kept a tame sparrow that he loved as his own child. While he was out, his bad-tempered wife caught the sparrow eating starch she had prepared for laundry. In her rage she cut out its tongue and let it fly away. When the old man came home and learned what had happened, he was heartbroken and set off into the mountains to find his friend.

He searched for a long time and at last came to a small inn deep in the woods, kept by the sparrow itself, now restored among its own kind. The sparrow welcomed him warmly, fed him, and entertained him with dancing. When he was leaving, the sparrow offered him a choice between two baskets, one large and one small. He chose the small one, saying he was an old man and could not carry much.

When he opened the small basket at home, he found it full of gold, silks, and treasures. His wife, hearing this, ran herself into the mountains to demand a basket as well. She found the inn and bullied the sparrow into letting her have the larger basket. On her way home, growing impatient, she opened it on the road — and out poured snakes, hornets, and ghosts. She fled in terror and was never the same again, while the kind old man lived comfortably for the rest of his days.
zh: 一位心地善良的老爷爷养了一只如同亲生孩子般疼爱的麻雀。某天他不在家，脾气暴躁的老婆婆撞见麻雀偷吃她为洗衣调好的浆糊，便一怒之下剪掉了麻雀的舌头，把它赶走。老爷爷回家后听说原委，伤心至极，独自上山去寻这位「老朋友」。
他翻山越岭走了很久，终于在林子深处看到一家小客栈，原来正是那只回到同类间、又得到照应的麻雀开的。麻雀热情迎接他，又是端饭又是为他献舞。临别时，麻雀让他在两只筐子中选一只——一大一小，作为送给他的礼物。他笑说自己年迈，挑了那只小的。
回家打开筐子一看，里面装满了金银、丝绸和各样珍宝。老婆婆听说后，自己也急匆匆赶到山里，逼麻雀把大的那只送给她。她背着大筐回家，路上等不及，半途就将筐打开——蛇、毒蜂和鬼魅一齐扑了出来。她吓得仓皇逃回家，从此再也没回过神，而善良的老爷爷则衣食无忧地度过了余生。

en: A poor tinker, passing through a temple yard, saw a tanuki — a raccoon dog — caught in a trap. He paid a few coins to free it. Later the tanuki, in gratitude, came to the man's hut, transformed itself into a fine tea-kettle, and told him to sell it to a rich man for a good price. The tinker did as he was told.

The new owner placed the kettle on the fire to make tea, and at the first touch of heat the kettle suddenly grew a tanuki's head, four legs, and a bushy tail. It leapt down from the fire and ran around the room, frightening everyone. The rich man tried to throw it away, but no one would take it.

The tinker bought back his strange kettle for almost nothing. With the tanuki's help, he turned it into a traveling show: the half-kettle, half-tanuki danced on a tightrope, balanced on its tail, and performed tricks for crowds. The little show became famous, and the tinker grew rich. At last he placed the tanuki-kettle at the same temple where he had first saved it, where it was honored as a treasure.
zh: 一位穷补锅匠路过一座寺庙的院子，看见一只貉子（俗称狸）被陷阱卡住。他掏出几枚铜钱把它救了下来。后来那只貉子为了报恩，跑到补锅匠的茅屋里，变作一只精致的茶釜，让他把这只茶釜高价卖给富翁。补锅匠照办了。
新主人把茶釜架在火上烧水煮茶，茶釜一受热，竟突然冒出貉的头、四条腿和一条毛茸茸的尾巴。它从火上一跃而下，绕屋乱跑，把众人吓得四散。富翁想把它扔掉，却没人敢要。
补锅匠几乎不花钱就把这只奇怪的茶釜买了回来。在貉子的配合下，他把它带上街头献艺：这只半茶釜半貉子的小怪物，在绳上跳舞、用尾巴顶立、玩各种把戏，引来一群又一群观众。这套小节目很快出了名，补锅匠也由此富起来。最后他把这只「貉茶釜」安放回当初救它的那座寺庙，作为一件珍宝供奉。

en: In late-Heian Japan, the retired emperor Toba took into his palace a beautiful young woman named Tamamo-no-Mae. She was famous for her learning as well as her beauty; she seemed to know the answer to every question. The emperor came to love her above all his other companions. After a time, however, his health began to fail without any clear cause.

The court astrologer Abe no Yasuchika was called in. By his arts he saw that Tamamo-no-Mae was no human woman but a nine-tailed fox spirit who had taken human shape, and that her presence was draining the emperor's life. When she heard that she had been discovered, she fled the palace at once and disappeared into the wilderness.

Warriors were sent to hunt her down. They pursued her across the country to the moor at Nasu, where they finally cornered and killed her. Even after her death her spirit was said to have entered a great stone there, the Sessho-seki, the "killing stone," which gave off poisonous fumes and killed birds that flew over it. A wandering priest later prayed at the stone until her spirit was at last laid to rest.
zh: 平安时代末，退位天皇鸟羽法皇把一位名叫玉藻前的美貌女子收入宫中。她不仅貌美，学识也极广博，似乎天下没有问题难得倒她。鸟羽法皇对她日益宠爱，把所有伴侣都比了下去。可不久之后，法皇身体莫名衰弱起来，找不出病因。
宫廷阴阳师安倍泰亲奉召入宫。他以阴阳之术看出，玉藻前并非凡人，而是一只化为人形的九尾妖狐，是她在不知不觉间夺去了法皇的精气。妖狐听到自己已被识破，立刻离开宫廷，遁入山野。
武士奉命四出搜寻她。他们一路追到那须野，终于在那里把她围住并将她诛杀。但据说她死后怨气仍未消散，附入了野地里的一块巨石——「杀生石」。那块石头从此散发毒气，连飞过其上的鸟雀都会坠落而亡。后来一位云游僧人在石前一再诵经超度，她的怨灵才终于得以安息。

en: Hong Gildong was born to a high nobleman of Joseon Korea and a serving woman. By the strict laws of his time he could not call his own father "father" or his elder brother "brother" — the child of a concubine was barred from office and even from family rank. Gildong was unusually intelligent and ambitious, and the contempt he met at home cut him deeply.

He left his father's house and took to the mountains, where he learned martial arts and the arts of disguise and transformation. He gathered around him a band of poor men, deserters, and outcasts, and turned them into a disciplined band of outlaws. They raided corrupt officials and rich monasteries and shared the gain with the poor. The court could not catch him; some said he could appear in eight different places at once.

After many years of robbing the powerful in Korea, Gildong sailed with his men to an island called Yul-do far in the southern sea. There he founded a small kingdom of his own, where status of birth did not matter and laws were fair. He was remembered in Korea as a hero of those who had been shut out by birth — and the tale of his life became one of the earliest novels written in the Korean alphabet.
zh: 洪吉童出生于朝鲜王朝一位大臣与他家中一名婢女之间。按当时严苛的礼法，他不能叫亲生父亲为「父亲」，也不能称兄长为「兄」——庶出之子被剥夺了仕途，甚至连家中正规身份都没有。吉童天资聪颖、心怀壮志，家中所受的轻慢让他刻骨铭心。
他离开父家，遁入山中，习武、习易容与变化之术。他在山中聚集起一帮贫民、逃兵和被遗弃之人，把他们调教成一支纪律严明的绿林队伍。他们专劫贪官污吏与富贵寺院，把所得分给贫民。朝廷怎么也抓不到他，有人说他能同时出现在八个不同的地方。
在朝鲜国内劫富济贫多年之后，吉童与众部属扬帆出海，到达南方海上一座名为「栗岛」的岛屿，在那里建起了一个属于自己的小国。岛上不再看重出身的高低，律法也力求公正。他在朝鲜历代被记作那些「因出身而被关在门外」的人的英雄——他的故事，也成为以韩文文字写下的最早的小说之一。

en: Ilya Muromets was a peasant's son from the village of Murom who lay sick on the family stove from birth, unable to walk or to use his arms, for the first thirty-three years of his life. One day three old pilgrims came to the house and asked for water. Unable to refuse the holy men, Ilya stirred himself and discovered that he could move. He brought them water; they gave him a cup to drink, and his strength flowed back as if he had been ill for no time at all.

The pilgrims told him that great deeds awaited him as a defender of the Russian land. He rose, plowed his father's whole field in a single day, and then went to Kiev to serve Prince Vladimir. On the way he met the great robber Nightingale, whose whistle could level forests, captured him alive, and brought him to court bound to his saddle.

From then on Ilya rode with the other bogatyrs at the prince's side, guarding the frontiers of Rus from steppe raiders and giants. He grew old in the saddle, refused to abandon his peasant origin, and quarreled honestly with the prince when he had to. In the great Russian epic cycle of the byliny, no warrior is more loved than Ilya Muromets, the slow-starting hero who became the strongest defender of his land.
zh: 伊利亚·穆罗梅茨是穆罗姆村一位农夫的儿子，从出生起就病卧在自家炉床上，整整三十三年里手脚都不能动。某天三位云游圣徒来到他家讨水。他不忍拒绝圣人，硬撑着试了一下，竟发现自己居然能动。他给圣徒们端来水，他们让他自己也喝了一杯，他的体力恍如从未生过病一般涌回。
圣徒们告诉他，他将作为俄罗斯大地的保卫者，肩负伟大事业。他起身后，一日之间把父亲整片田地犁完，随后启程前往基辅，要为弗拉基米尔大公效力。途中他遇到能以一声啸把森林吹倒的大盗「夜莺贼」，把他活捉，捆在自己马鞍上带回了王宫。
从此伊利亚与众壮士一同骑马护卫大公左右，戍守罗斯边境，抵御草原游骑和巨人。他在马背上慢慢老去，从不掩饰自己的农家出身，与大公意见不合时也敢直言相争。在俄罗斯著名的史诗「壮士歌」体系里，没有哪位英雄比这位「起步极慢，却最终成为最强守护者」的伊利亚·穆罗梅茨更受爱戴。

en: Nasreddin Hodja, a wise fool of Anatolia, lived by his wit and his proverbs and rode everywhere on a small donkey. The stories about him pass back and forth across the Muslim world and laugh at greed, pride, and the foolishness of judges. In one of the most famous, Nasreddin and his son were traveling with their donkey from one village to the next.

First the boy rode while Nasreddin walked, and bystanders said the boy was lazy and disrespectful to his old father. So they switched, and now bystanders said Nasreddin was cruel to make a child walk in the dust. Then they both rode, and people said they were cruel to the donkey. Then they both walked beside the donkey, and people laughed at them for not using the animal at all.

At last Nasreddin and his son picked up the donkey and tried to carry it across a bridge. The animal struggled, kicked, and fell into the river. Nasreddin sighed and turned to his son: "That is what comes," he said, "of trying to please everyone." The lesson — and the donkey — became proverbs that have outlived the empires of his time.
zh: 纳斯尔丁霍加是安纳托利亚一位「智者兼笑话人」式的人物，靠机智与谚语在世上行走，到处都骑着一头小毛驴。他的故事在整个伊斯兰世界互相流传，讽刺贪婪、骄傲与糊涂法官。其中最有名的一则，是纳斯尔丁带着儿子和那头小毛驴，要从一个村子走到下一个村子。
起先是儿子骑驴，纳斯尔丁步行，路人嘀咕这孩子懒，对老父亲不敬。于是两人换位，纳斯尔丁骑驴让儿子走，又有人说他狠心，让孩子在尘土里赶路。两人一同骑上驴，又有人说他们把驴压坏了。两人都下来牵着驴走，又有人笑他们好端端的牲口竟不会用。
最后纳斯尔丁与儿子干脆把驴抬起来，要扛着它过桥。驴一阵踢腾，反倒掉进了河里。纳斯尔丁叹了口气，对儿子说：「这就是想让所有人都满意的下场。」这一句话与那头落水的驴，从此化作越过他那一时代各大帝国的谚语，一直被人讲到今天。

en: Jupiter and Mercury, traveling through Phrygia in human disguise, came to a hill town and asked at each door for shelter for the night. House after house turned them away. At the very edge of the town, a small thatched cottage opened its door to them. There lived an old couple named Baucis and Philemon, who had grown old together in that hut and were very poor.

They hurried to set out their best — old wine, a little bread, cheese, vegetables, and the dish of bacon they had saved. They noticed that the wine jug stayed full no matter how much they poured. Frightened, they realized their guests were gods and offered to kill their only goose for them. The gods stopped them, said the time of the town was over, and led the couple up the hill to look back.

From the top they saw the town drowned in water as punishment for its hardness, while only their hut remained, now changed into a temple of marble. The gods asked Baucis and Philemon what they wished. They asked to serve in the temple together and to die at the same moment, so that neither would have to bury the other. The gods granted both wishes. In their old age the two were turned into trees, one oak and one linden, standing side by side at the temple gate.
zh: 朱庇特和墨丘利化作凡人，穿过弗里吉亚来到一座山间小镇，挨家挨户敲门求住一晚。一户又一户人家都把他们拒之门外。直到镇子的最边缘，一间破旧的茅屋向他们敞开了门。屋里住着一对名叫鲍喀斯和腓利门的老夫妇，他们在这间小屋里相伴老去，家境十分贫寒。
两人连忙摆出家里最好的——存着的老酒、一点面包、奶酪、青菜，还有舍不得吃的一小块腌肉。倒酒的过程中，他们发现酒罐无论怎么倒都倒不空。两人惊觉客人乃是神明，便要把家中唯一的一只鹅杀了献神。神拦住他们，告诉他们这座镇子的时日已尽，并把两人领上山顶回望。
从山顶望去，整座小镇都被大水淹没，作为对它无情的惩罚，唯独他们的茅屋还在，而且变成了一座大理石的神殿。神明问鲍喀斯和腓利门有什么愿望。他们说，只求两人能一同在这座神殿里侍奉，同一刻一起去世，免得任何一方为另一方送葬。神明都答应了。后来在他们年迈之时，两人化作了两棵树，一橡一菩提，并肩立在神殿门旁。

en: The hero Siegfried, in the medieval German Nibelungenlied, won fame by killing a dragon and bathing in its blood, which made his skin tough as horn — except for one spot on his back where a linden leaf had fallen between his shoulders. He also seized the great treasure of the Nibelungs, with the help of an enchanted cloak of invisibility. He came to the Burgundian court and married the princess Kriemhild.

But court politics, jealousy, and an old quarrel between Kriemhild and her sister-in-law Brunhild brought disaster. Brunhild was insulted, and her champion Hagen, sworn man of the king, learned from Kriemhild the secret of her husband's single weak spot. While Siegfried was bending to drink at a spring on a hunt, Hagen drove a spear into his back at that very place.

Hagen then took the Nibelung treasure and sank it in the Rhine, so that no one else could profit from it. Kriemhild's grief turned to long revenge. Years later she lured Hagen and the Burgundian lords to the court of the Huns, where in a great final battle nearly all of them, and she with them, perished. The hoard remained at the bottom of the Rhine; the heroes were remembered as men whose pride and broken oaths had pulled down the great houses of the German epic.
zh: 在中世纪德语史诗《尼伯龙根之歌》中，英雄齐格弗里德因斩杀一条恶龙而扬名。他用龙血洗身，皮肤从此坚硬如角——只有两肩之间一处被掉落的一片菩提叶遮住，没沾上龙血。他又借助一件能隐身的斗篷夺得了尼伯龙族的巨额宝藏。后来他来到勃艮第宫廷，娶了克里姆希尔德公主。
宫廷里的派系、嫉妒，加上克里姆希尔德与嫂嫂布伦希尔德之间的旧怨，最终酿成大祸。布伦希尔德被当众羞辱，她那位忠心于王的家臣哈根，从克里姆希尔德口中套出了齐格弗里德唯一弱处的秘密。一次狩猎中，齐格弗里德俯身在泉边饮水，哈根从背后一矛刺入那一处。
随后哈根夺走尼伯龙宝藏，把它沉入莱茵河底，不让任何人再受益。克里姆希尔德的哀痛化作绵长的复仇。多年后她将哈根与勃艮第众贵族诱至匈人之王的宫廷，在一场决战中，他们几乎全部丧命，她自己也在其中。那批宝藏从此长眠莱茵河底。这些英雄留给后人的，是骄傲与背誓如何把日耳曼史诗中的一整批大族一齐拉下深渊的记忆。

en: Doctor Faust, in the old German story, was a learned man who had mastered every kind of human study — philosophy, law, medicine, theology — and still found himself empty. He turned to magic in search of deeper knowledge. By his arts he summoned the spirit Mephistopheles and made a bargain with him: Mephistopheles would serve Faust for twenty-four years on earth, giving him whatever knowledge and pleasure he wished, and at the end of that time Faust's soul would belong to Hell.

Faust signed the contract in his own blood. Mephistopheles took him through the world. He saw princes and popes, traveled by magic horse and dragon, conjured up the spirit of Helen of Troy as his lover, and used the spirit to play tricks on the great and the foolish alike. He grew rich in learning and in experience, but the contract's deadline drew steadily closer.

On the final night, in terror, Faust prayed and called for help, but the bargain was sealed. At midnight a great storm broke over the house, and in the morning his students found his torn body in his study. Some versions of the story end there; in Goethe's later retelling, Faust is saved at the last moment through love and striving. Either way, the figure of Faust passed into European thought as the warning of what a man might give up for knowledge without limits.
zh: 古老德意志故事里的浮士德博士，本是位通晓人间各门学问的学者——哲学、法学、医学、神学，他无一不精，却仍觉空虚。他转向魔法，去寻找更深一层的知识。借助所学，他召出魔鬼梅菲斯托菲勒斯，与之立约：梅菲斯托菲勒斯在尘世间为浮士德服侍二十四年，赐给他想要的一切知识与享乐；二十四年之后，浮士德的灵魂归属地狱。
浮士德以自己的血签下契约。梅菲斯托菲勒斯领他周游世界。他见过王公、教皇，骑着魔法的马与龙四处游历，召出特洛伊海伦的幽魂作情人，借这位幽魂的形象戏弄贵胄与愚人。他在学问和阅历上日益富有，可契约的期限也在一日日逼近。
最后一夜，浮士德惶恐之中祈祷求救，可契约早已不可更改。半夜一场狂暴风雨笼罩屋舍，第二天清晨学生们在书房里发现他被撕碎的尸体。一部分版本的故事便止于此；在歌德后来的版本里，浮士德则在最后一刻，因爱与不息的进取而得救。无论结局如何，「浮士德」这一形象都进入了欧洲思想，成为一个反复出现的警示——一个人究竟肯为无限的知识，付出多少代价。

en: Shango was the fourth king of Oyo, a powerful Yoruba kingdom in what is now Nigeria. He was famous for his fierce courage in war and his hot temper. In one telling, he experimented with a charm meant to call down lightning, and a bolt struck his own palace and killed several of his family. Some of his people blamed him and turned against him.

Ashamed and grieving, Shango left the palace and went out into the forest. He did not die in any ordinary way. The stories say that he climbed a tree and disappeared into the sky, or that he hanged himself and was carried away by the gods. Either way, he was no longer counted among living kings, and his enemies thought they had finally seen the end of him.

But then thunder began to crash whenever his name was spoken with contempt, and lightning struck the houses of those who had betrayed him. The Yoruba understood that Shango had not died but had become an orisha, a divine force, the god of thunder, fire, and justice. He is honored at altars by a double-headed axe and red beads, and his worship traveled across the Atlantic with enslaved peoples to Cuba, Brazil, and beyond.
zh: 尚戈是约鲁巴强国奥约的第四位国王，国土在如今的尼日利亚。他以战场上的勇悍和火爆的性子闻名。一种说法是，他试用一道意在召来雷电的法术，结果一道闪电反劈在自家王宫里，烧死了他的几位至亲。部分子民因此把怒气转到他身上，与他离心。
尚戈又惭又痛，离开王宫走进森林。他并未以常人方式死去。传说他爬上一棵树后径直消失在天空之中，也有说他自缢之后被诸神带走。无论哪种讲法，他不再列于在世国王之中，他的敌人也以为终于熬到了他的下场。
可不久之后，凡是有人轻慢地提起他的名字，雷声便随之炸响；当年背叛他的人，家中接连被雷击中。约鲁巴人于是明白：尚戈并未真死，而是化作了一位「奥里沙」——神圣的力量，主管雷、火与正义之神。他的祭坛以双刃斧与红色串珠为标记，他的崇拜随着被贩卖的黑奴漂洋过海，传到了古巴、巴西及更远的地方。

en: Makeda was the queen of Sheba — a kingdom whose memory is shared by Ethiopia and Yemen. Hearing of the great wisdom of King Solomon of Israel, she set out across the deserts with a long caravan loaded with gold, spices, and rare stones. She wished to test him with hard questions of her own.

Solomon welcomed her at Jerusalem and answered every question she put to him. He showed her the splendor of his court and his temple, and he gave her in return any gift she asked. According to the Ethiopian tradition recorded in the Kebra Nagast, before she left she conceived a son by him.

On her way home she gave birth to a boy named Menelik. When he had grown, he traveled to Jerusalem to meet his father, and Solomon honored him as his son. The Ethiopian tradition says that Menelik brought the Ark of the Covenant back with him to Ethiopia, and that the line of kings he founded — claiming descent from Solomon and Makeda — ruled until the twentieth century. Solomon and Sheba thus stood at the source of an entire national story.
zh: 玛凯达是示巴国的女王——示巴国的记忆同时属于今天的埃塞俄比亚与也门。她听闻以色列国王所罗门以智慧名满天下，便率领一支驮着黄金、香料和珍宝的长长商队，穿过沙漠前去拜访。她想用自己的难题亲自考一考他。
所罗门在耶路撒冷迎接了她，对她提出的每一个问题都给出回答。他让她参观自己宫廷与圣殿的壮丽，又答应她临行前可以索要任何礼物。按埃塞俄比亚《国王的荣耀》中所记，她离开之前与所罗门生下了一个儿子。
归途中她产下一名男婴，取名孟尼利克。男婴长大后远赴耶路撒冷与生父相见，所罗门以儿子之礼相待。埃塞俄比亚传统又说，孟尼利克回国时把约柜带回了埃塞俄比亚，他所开创的王统——自称源自所罗门与玛凯达——一直延续到二十世纪。所罗门与示巴女王，由此成为整段民族叙事的源头。

en: In the Persian Shahnameh, the great hero Rostam stopped one night at the city of Samangan during his travels. There he met the princess Tahmineh, who came to him by night, and the two were quickly wed and parted. Before he left, Rostam gave her an arm-clasp; she was to give it to their child if she bore him a son.

Tahmineh gave birth to a boy and named him Sohrab. Sohrab grew to enormous size and strength, hearing only that his father was the greatest hero of Iran but not certain who he was. When he became a young warrior of Turan, he rode at the head of an army into Iranian territory, hoping to find Rostam and join with him to set things right.

Iranian and Turanian armies met. Rostam, sent for as champion, did not recognize his son. The two of them fought in single combat for three days. On the third day Rostam threw the young warrior down and drove a dagger into his side. As Sohrab lay dying he showed Rostam the arm-clasp on his arm. Rostam recognized the gift he had once given Tahmineh, and understood, far too late, that he had killed his own son.
zh: 波斯史诗《列王纪》中，大英雄罗斯塔姆一次在远行途中，夜宿萨曼甘城。当夜公主塔赫米娜前来相见，两人匆匆结亲，又匆匆分别。临别时罗斯塔姆把一只臂环交给她，叮嘱：如果生下的是儿子，便把这只臂环交给他。
塔赫米娜后来生下一个男孩，取名苏赫拉布。苏赫拉布体魄魁梧、力大无穷，只听说自己的父亲是伊朗最大的英雄，却不曾确知是谁。他成年后在图兰为将，率军进入伊朗境内，本是想找到罗斯塔姆，与他一同匡正局势。
伊朗与图兰大军相对，罗斯塔姆被召作主将出战，却不认得这是自己的儿子。父子在阵前单挑了三天。第三天罗斯塔姆把这位年轻勇士摔倒在地，将匕首刺入他身侧。苏赫拉布临死前撩起手臂，向罗斯塔姆露出那只臂环。罗斯塔姆看到自己当年送给塔赫米娜的信物，方才明白——可惜已经太迟——自己亲手杀死了亲生的儿子。

en: Garuda, the great eagle who would later become Vishnu's mount, was born from an egg of the sage Kashyapa. His mother Vinata had lost a wager to her sister Kadru, mother of the serpents, and was made a slave in their house. The serpents told Garuda that they would free his mother only if he brought them the amrita, the elixir of immortality kept in heaven by the gods.

Garuda flew up to the abode of the gods. Indra and the other gods fought him, but his strength was too great. The gods had set the amrita inside a turning wheel of blades, guarded by two giant serpents and surrounded by a curtain of flames. Garuda swallowed water from many rivers to put out the flames, shrank himself to slip through the wheel, blinded the serpents with dust, and seized the elixir.

On the way down he met Vishnu, who blessed him with eternal life as a friend rather than as an enemy, and Indra, who struck a deal: Garuda would carry the amrita to the serpents to free his mother, but Indra could steal it back before they drank. Garuda set the cup on sharp grass and called the serpents to bathe before drinking; while they were gone, Indra carried the amrita home. The serpents licked the grass in their haste and only got their tongues split. Garuda's mother was freed, and the serpents and the eagle have been enemies ever since.
zh: 迦楼罗是日后被毗湿奴选作坐骑的神鸟之王，出生于圣者迦叶波的一枚蛋。他的母亲毗那陀与姐姐妙翼——蛇族之母——打赌输了，被罚到对方家中为奴。蛇族告诉迦楼罗：除非他到天上去把诸神珍藏的不死甘露「阿姆利达」夺来送给他们，否则不会放他母亲离去。
迦楼罗飞上天界。因陀罗和众神纷纷出战，可他的力量之大让他们都拦不住。诸神把甘露藏在一座飞转的刀刃之轮中，由两条巨蛇守着，再以一圈火帘环护。迦楼罗喝下众河之水，把火帘浇灭，又把自己缩小钻过刀轮，用尘土迷住两蛇的眼睛，把甘露夺到了手。
归途中他遇见毗湿奴，毗湿奴乐意以朋友而非敌人的身份赐他不死之身；又遇到因陀罗，与他立下约定：迦楼罗可以把甘露送到蛇族那里以解救母亲，但蛇族喝下之前因陀罗可以再夺回来。迦楼罗把甘露放在锋利的茅草上，让蛇族先去沐浴再来享用；蛇族沐浴之时，因陀罗便把甘露收回了天界。蛇群急中扑回草上去舔，舌头反被茅草划成两半。迦楼罗的母亲就此得救，鹰与蛇之间的仇怨从此结下，再也不曾消除。

en: Savitri was the daughter of a king, and she had her father's permission to choose her own husband. Out of all the men she saw, she chose Satyavan, a noble young man living in the forest with his blind exiled father. A holy sage warned her that Satyavan was fated to die in exactly one year. Savitri refused to choose anyone else and married him anyway.

On the morning of the day predicted for his death, Savitri took a vow and went with Satyavan into the forest, where he was cutting wood. He grew weak and lay down with his head on her lap. Yama, the lord of death, came at that moment to take his soul, and Savitri rose and followed Yama as he walked away with it.

Yama told her to turn back, but she answered every objection with such wise words that he granted her one wish after another — wealth, the restoration of her father-in-law's sight and kingdom, sons of her own — as long as she did not ask for her husband's life. With her third wish she asked for many sons, knowing that she had only one husband to give them. Yama saw that he had been outwitted by her devotion, returned Satyavan's soul, and walked on alone. Satyavan and Savitri walked back from the forest together.
zh: 萨维特丽是一位国王的女儿，得到父亲许可，可以自己选择丈夫。她看遍了见过的所有男子，最终选中萨提梵——一位与流放、失明的父亲一同住在山林里的高贵青年。一位圣者警告她：萨提梵命中注定恰在一年之后死去。萨维特丽不肯改选他人，仍嫁给了他。
在被预言的死亡之日的清晨，萨维特丽立下苦行誓言，陪丈夫一起进林砍柴。萨提梵在林中忽然力衰，把头枕在她膝上倒下。死神阎摩正在此刻前来取他的灵魂。萨维特丽站起来，跟着提着丈夫魂魄离去的阎摩。
阎摩劝她回去，她却用极其睿智的言辞回应他的每一句劝阻。阎摩感佩之下，一愿接一愿地满足她——财富、公公复明、公公复位、她自己将来要有许多儿子——条件只有一个：不可直接求丈夫的命。她第三次许愿要许多儿子，又故意补充自己只有这一位丈夫。阎摩这才发现自己被她的贞节与智慧将了一军，只好把萨提梵的灵魂归还，独自走开。萨提梵与萨维特丽手挽手，从林中一同走了回来。

en: Long ago, the ancestors of King Bhagiratha had been burnt to ashes by the curse of a sage. Their souls could not find rest because no proper water had touched their bones. Bhagiratha vowed to bring down the Ganges, which then flowed only in heaven, so that its water could wash over the ashes and free his ancestors' souls.

He performed long, severe austerities, year after year. At last he won the favor of the goddess Ganga, who agreed to descend to the earth. But her flow was so heavy that, falling directly onto the ground, she would have shattered the world. Bhagiratha then turned to the great god Shiva and begged him to break her fall.

Shiva stood on the high Himalaya and let Ganga land in his matted hair. There she wandered for years in his locks before he released her in small streams. Bhagiratha walked ahead of the river, leading it across the plains of India and out to the sea, until at last her waters reached the ashes of his ancestors and they were freed. The river has been called the Ganges ever since, and the place where she meets the sea, Ganga Sagar, is sacred to him.
zh: 古时候，跋吉罗陀王的祖先因触怒一位圣者，被诅咒烧成了一堆灰烬。他们的灵魂迟迟得不到安息，因为没有合适的水洒到那堆骸骨上。跋吉罗陀立誓要把当时还只在天上流淌的恒河迎下凡间，让河水冲过那些骨灰，让祖先得到解脱。
他常年苦修，年复一年绝不间断，终于打动了恒河女神，她答应降临人间。可恒河水量极大，如果径直从天空坠落，会把整片大地震碎。跋吉罗陀又转而恳求大神湿婆出面，替他承住这一击。
湿婆立于高耸的喜马拉雅山上，让恒河水落入自己盘结的发髻之中。她在他的发缕里游荡了很多年，他才把她以一支支细流缓缓释放出来。跋吉罗陀走在水流前头，引着河水穿过印度平原直奔大海，最终河水真的流到了祖先骨灰之处，将他们解脱。从此那条河便以「恒河」之名传到今日，而她入海的地方——恒河三角洲的恒河海口——也被奉为属于跋吉罗陀的圣地。
