en: King Ogu and his queen had six daughters and prayed for a son. When a seventh daughter was born, the king was so angry that he ordered the baby thrown into the sea. She was placed in a small jeweled box and set adrift, but a sea-dragon king found her and an old couple raised her on a far shore. They named her Bari, "the abandoned one."

Years later, both the king and the queen fell deathly ill. Their other six daughters refused to undertake the dangerous journey to fetch the medicinal water from the western paradise that could heal them. Only Bari, when she was at last brought back to the palace and told the truth about her birth, agreed to go.

She crossed mountains, deserts, and the river of the dead, performed hard labor for the keeper of the medicinal water for nine years, and bore him seven sons. At last she was given the water and returned. The king and queen had already died, but with the water and her chants she brought them back to life. Bari refused to remain at the palace; she became instead a guide for the souls of the dead, and she is honored to this day in Korean shaman ritual as the first muist priest, the one who leads the spirits home.
zh: 古时候的吴格国王与王后接连生了六个女儿，仍盼着能有一个儿子。等到第七位女儿出生时，国王怒不可遏，下令把这个婴儿扔进大海。她被装在一只缀玉的小箱中漂在水上，幸而被海中龙王所救，并由一对住在远方海岸的老夫妇收养。他们给她取名「巴里」，意为「被丢下的孩子」。
多年之后，国王与王后双双病重将死。他们另外六个女儿，谁也不愿替父母去往西方乐土取那能救命的灵水。最后只有被接回宫中、得知自己身世的巴里，自愿启程上路。
她翻山越岭，渡过沙漠和亡者之河，又在守护灵水的神祇手下整整劳作九年，并为他生下七个儿子。最终她得到灵水，捧着返回宫中。父母虽然早已断气，她用灵水与诵唱将他们救活。巴里却不肯留在宫中享受尊位，而选择从此成为亡灵的向导。直到今天，韩国的萨满仪式中仍尊奉她为第一位巫者——那位领着亡魂回家的女子。

en: Sedna was a young Inuit woman who refused every suitor her father brought to her. At last a stranger arrived from across the sea promising her a warm house and good food. She agreed and went with him, but his promises were false; he was in fact a bird-spirit in human shape, and she lived on a bare cliff. Her father sailed across the water and took her back in his kayak.

The bird-spirit pursued them with a great storm. To save his own life, the father threw Sedna over the side of the kayak. She clung to the edge with her fingers, and he cut them off, joint by joint. As her fingers fell into the sea, they became seals, walruses, and whales — the sea mammals on which the Inuit depend.

Sedna herself sank to the bottom of the sea and became its ruler. Her hair could not be combed because she had no fingers to do it. When her hair was matted with the sins and broken promises of human beings above, the sea animals were tangled in her hair and could not be hunted. Shamans had to travel down to her, comb her hair clean of human wrongs, and only then would the animals be released again to feed the people.
zh: 塞德娜原本是因纽特人中的一名年轻女子，对父亲为她物色的求亲者一个不答应。终于有一天，一位从海那边来的陌生男子前来求婚，许诺会给她温暖的家与丰足的食物。她答应嫁过去，却发现一切都是谎言——那男子其实是化作人形的鸟灵，她被关在光秃秃的悬崖上过活。她的父亲驾着皮艇渡海，把她接了回来。
鸟灵随后召来一场巨大的风暴追上他们。父亲为保性命，把塞德娜从皮艇边推了下去。她用手指紧抠艇缘不肯放手，父亲举起刀，把她的手指一节一节地砍断。手指坠入海中之后，分别化成了海豹、海象与鲸鱼——因纽特人赖以生存的海中生灵。
塞德娜自己也沉入海底，从此成为海洋之主。她没有手指，无法梳理头发。每当上界人间的罪过和违誓越积越多时，她的头发就会被这些恶事缠成乱团，海中生灵便被困在她的乱发中、无法被人捕获。萨满便要下到海底替她梳头、把人间的罪恶一缕缕从她发间剔除，海兽才会重新被她放出来，供族人猎食。

en: Among the peoples of the Pacific Northwest coast, light at the beginning of the world was held in three boxes by an old chief who lived at the head of a river. He had captured the sun, the moon, and the stars and kept them for himself, and the world was in darkness. Raven, the trickster, decided to steal them.

Raven transformed himself into a tiny hemlock needle and dropped into the drinking water of the chief's daughter. She swallowed him and became pregnant. He was born as her child in the chief's house and grew up as the old chief's beloved grandson. He cried until his grandfather opened the box of stars to amuse him; once it was open, Raven scattered the stars through the smokehole into the sky. He cried again until the moon was opened, and scattered it the same way.

Finally, at the last box, Raven seized the sun in his beak and burst out through the smokehole, changing back to his bird form as he flew. As he carried the sun across the sky, fire blackened his white feathers; that is why Raven is black today. He set the sun in its place in the heavens, and from then on the people of the world had day and night, summer and winter, and the light by which to live.
zh: 在太平洋西北沿海的诸民族传说中，世界刚开始时，光被一位住在大河上游的老酋长锁在三只匣子里。他把太阳、月亮和群星都收作己有，整个世界一片漆黑。骗子英雄渡鸦决心去偷回光明。
渡鸦把自己变成一根细小的铁杉针，掉进了老酋长女儿喝的水里。她吞下水后怀了孕，把渡鸦生为自己的孩子。渡鸦在酋长家中长大，被外祖父极其疼爱。他先是哭闹不止，外祖父无奈打开装星辰的匣子哄他；匣子一开，渡鸦立刻把里面的星星全部从烟囱口扔上夜空。他又哭着要月亮，月亮一开匣，也被他照样撒上了天。
最后只剩盛着太阳的那只匣子。渡鸦一把把太阳衔在嘴里，从烟囱口冲了出去，飞行中重新变回鸟形。他衔着太阳穿过天空，火焰把他原本雪白的羽毛烧成了焦黑——这便是渡鸦至今全身漆黑的原因。他把太阳挂上天空的固定位置，从那以后，世界上的人才有了昼与夜、夏与冬，有了赖以生活的光。

en: In a Scottish border ballad, the young Janet went to the wood of Carterhaugh, which everyone said belonged to a knight called Tam Lin. She picked a rose at the well, and Tam Lin appeared. They became lovers in the wood, and she returned home pregnant. When she went back to him, she discovered that he had once been a mortal man, but had been taken by the Queen of the Fairies and was now bound to her court.

Tam Lin told Janet that the fairies rode out at Halloween, and that he would be among them. To win him back, she would have to stand at a crossroads and pull him off his horse as he passed. Then she would have to hold on to him no matter what he became, for the fairies would turn him into many shapes in her arms.

On Halloween night Janet did as he said. As she held him, Tam Lin turned in her arms into a snake, then a bear, then a red-hot iron bar that burned her, then into many other terrors. She did not let go. At the last he became a naked man, and she wrapped him in her green cloak. The Queen of the Fairies cursed her aloud but had to ride away without him, and Tam Lin walked back to the living world with Janet at his side.
zh: 苏格兰边境的一首民谣里，少女珍妮特独自去了卡特霍林——人人都说那片林子归一位名叫塔姆林的骑士所有。她在井边摘下一朵玫瑰，塔姆林便现身。两人在林中相恋，珍妮特回家时已经怀了身孕。她再回林中找他时，才得知他原本是凡人，被仙后掳走，如今为仙后所役使。
塔姆林告诉珍妮特，每年万圣节夜里，仙女队伍会从林中走过，他就在其中。要把他救回来，她必须在十字路口拦下他的马，将他拉下来，然后无论他变成什么模样都要紧紧抱住——仙后会让他在她怀中变成种种东西。
万圣节那夜珍妮特依言而行。她紧抱着塔姆林，他在她臂弯里先变成一条蛇，再变成一头熊，又变成一根烧红的烙铁烫她，又变成种种可怕模样。她始终没有松手。最后他变作一个赤身的男子，她连忙把自己的绿斗篷披在他身上。仙后当场恶毒咒骂，却也只能空手离开，塔姆林由此挣脱仙国，在珍妮特身旁走回了人间。

en: Among the Muisca people of the high plain of Bogota, the bearded old wise man Bochica had long ago come from the east to teach them the arts of weaving, farming, and law. He went away after teaching them, but he was remembered as a benefactor. In a later age, a being named Chibchacum, angry at the people, sent the river Funza out across the plain in a flood that drowned the fields and houses.
The Muisca had no way to drive the water back. They cried out to Bochica for help. He appeared at the edge of the high plain near the place now called Tequendama.

Bochica struck the rim of the plateau with his golden staff, and the rock split open in front of him. The river poured down through the new gap in a great waterfall, and the floodwaters on the plain drained away. The fields became dry again, and the Muisca could rebuild. The Tequendama Falls, plunging from the edge of the savanna of Bogota, were remembered as the cut that Bochica had once made to save his people.
zh: 在波哥大高原上的穆伊斯卡人中，远古时一位有须的白发智者博奇卡曾从东方来到他们之中，教他们纺织、农耕和律法。他传授完之后离去，但被族人记为大恩人。后世有一位名为奇布查孔的神，因对人间不满，让丰萨河漫过整片高原，洪水淹没了田地与村庄。
穆伊斯卡人毫无办法把大水逼回。他们呼喊博奇卡前来相助。博奇卡出现在高原的边缘——那里就是今日所说的特肯达马附近。
博奇卡手持金杖，朝高原的边沿用力一击，岩石应声裂开一道缺口。汹涌的河水从这道新开的口子上飞流直下，化作壮丽的瀑布，高原上的洪水顺势退尽，田地重新晒干，穆伊斯卡人得以重整家园。后人便把波哥大草原边奔流而下的特肯达马大瀑布，记作博奇卡当年为救子民而劈开的那道缺口。

en: In a Thai folktale, a poor couple had twelve daughters. Unable to feed them all, they took them deep into the forest one day and left them there. The twelve sisters wandered until they came to the palace of an ogress, who pretended to be a kindly woman and offered them shelter. The sisters discovered her true nature in time and slipped away while she slept.

They reached the city of a king, who took them all as his wives. The ogress, still hungry for revenge, came to court disguised as a beautiful woman and bewitched the king. She told him that to be cured of his sudden illness he must put out the eyes of his twelve wives and feed them to her. The king did this and locked the twelve blind sisters in a cave.

The youngest sister gave birth in the cave to a son, Phra Rothasen, who grew up clever and brave. He eventually traveled to the ogress's palace, won the heart of her own daughter, and through her help recovered the medicine that could heal his mother and aunts. He returned to the cave, restored their sight, and overthrew the ogress at last. The king repented, and the twelve sisters were brought back as queens.
zh: 泰国一则民间故事中，一对穷夫妇生有十二个女儿。养不活全家的他们某天把女儿们带进林子深处丢下。十二姐妹一路漂泊，来到一座女妖的宫殿。女妖装作慈祥的妇人收留她们，姐妹们及时察觉了她的本相，趁她熟睡时悄悄逃了出来。
她们逃到一个国王的都城，国王把十二姐妹一并娶为妃。女妖怀恨在心，又化作绝美的女子前往宫廷，把国王迷惑了。她对国王说，要医治他突然发作的怪病，就必须挖出十二妃的眼睛喂她。国王照办，把十二个失明的姐妹关进一处山洞。
最小的妹妹在洞中产下一子，取名「帕罗陀森」，长大后聪明又勇敢。后来他设法到了女妖的宫殿，赢得了女妖亲生女儿的心，并借她之手取得能让母亲和姨母们复明的药。他回到山洞，让十二位母亲与姨母重见光明，又终于击败了女妖。国王悔过，把十二姐妹接回宫中重新做了王后。

en: In the Babylonian Enuma Elish, the first beings were the salt-water Tiamat and the sweet-water Apsu, whose mingled waters were the only thing in existence. From them came a long line of younger gods, whose noise and movement disturbed Apsu. Apsu plotted to destroy his children, but the wise god Ea killed him first. Tiamat, enraged, made a host of monsters and went to war against the gods.

The younger gods feared her and could find no champion to stand against her, until Marduk stepped forward. He demanded that, if he won, he be made the chief of all the gods. The gods agreed. Marduk armed himself with bow, mace, lightning, and the four winds, and rode out in a storm chariot to meet Tiamat.

When Tiamat opened her mouth to swallow him, Marduk drove the winds into her belly so that she could not close it, then shot an arrow through her heart. He split her body in two and used one half to make the dome of the sky and the other to make the earth. From the blood of one of her commanders, mixed with clay, he made human beings to do the work of the gods. So Marduk became the head of the Babylonian pantheon and the maker of the ordered world.
zh: 在巴比伦的《埃努玛·埃利什》中，最初的存在是咸水之神提阿玛特与淡水之神阿普苏，他们交融的水域便是世间唯一的一切。后来从他们之间生出一支接一支的年轻神祇，这些年轻神的喧闹与躁动让阿普苏不胜其烦。阿普苏便密谋要除掉自己的子嗣，但智慧之神埃阿抢先一步把他杀死。提阿玛特怒不可遏，造出大批怪物，向众神宣战。
年轻众神惧怕她，找不出一位敢出阵迎敌的勇士，直到马尔杜克挺身而出。他提出条件：若他取胜，便要被立为众神之首。诸神答应了。马尔杜克备好弓、铜锤、闪电与四方之风，驾起风暴战车迎向提阿玛特。
提阿玛特张开大口想吞下他，马尔杜克便驱风灌入她的腹中，使她口不能合，再挽弓一箭穿心。他把她的尸身剖为两半，一半作天穹，一半作大地。又从她麾下一位将领的血与泥土相和，造出人类来替诸神担起劳役。马尔杜克由此成为巴比伦众神之首，也成为这一被安排妥当的世界的塑造者。

en: Sinbad was a merchant of Baghdad who, having lost his fortune, signed on with a trading ship to make it again. On his first voyage he and his shipmates landed on what seemed to be a green island in the open sea. They built fires and cooked food on it. The "island" suddenly heaved and sank — it had been the back of an enormous whale that had lain still long enough for plants and earth to grow on it.

Sinbad was thrown into the water and managed to cling to a piece of wood. He drifted for days until he reached a coast, where he was found by men breaking horses for a king. They presented him to the king, who took to him because he could tell of distant lands. After a time, by chance, his old ship's crew passed through that port, recognized him, and gave back the goods they had thought lost with him.

He returned home rich. But the lure of the sea was strong, and Sinbad set out again — to the roc bird's nest, to the valley of diamonds, to the old man of the sea who clung to his shoulders, to the cyclops who ate his companions, and to other dangers across seven voyages in all. Each time he came back wiser and richer, and his stories became one of the great threads in the Arabian Nights.
zh: 辛巴达原是巴格达的一位商人，因生意失败把家底赔光，便搭上一艘商船想再挣回家业。第一次航行途中，船到了茫茫海面上一座看似郁郁葱葱的小岛。船员们在岛上生火做饭。那「岛」忽然一抬一沉——原来是一头巨大无比的鲸，因为长年静卧海面，背上才积起泥土草木。
辛巴达被甩进海中，幸而抓住一块木板。他在海上漂了几天后到达一片海岸，被几个替国王驯马的人发现。他们把他引荐给国王，国王喜欢听他讲远方的见闻，便把他留在身边。后来巧合中，他原本那艘商船恰巧停靠到这处港口，老船友认出了他，把当初当作他遗物收存的货物悉数归还。
他衣锦还乡。然而海上的诱惑太强，辛巴达又一次次出航——到大鹏鸟的巢、到钻石之谷、遇见趴在他肩上不肯下来的「海上老人」、被独眼巨人吃掉同伴，等等，共有七次航行。每一次他都更加见多识广，也更富有。这些故事，后来汇成了《一千零一夜》中最为人传诵的一脉。

en: Among the Zulu, the first being was Unkulunkulu, "the great-great-one," who came up out of the reeds of a marsh at the dawn of the world. Different beings came up after him from the same reed-bed — men, women, animals, and the things of the natural world — until there was a full creation. Unkulunkulu taught the first people their customs: how to use fire, how to marry, how to herd cattle, how to honor the dead.

Wishing to send a message to humanity that they would not have to die, Unkulunkulu chose a chameleon and told it to carry the news. The chameleon, slow as it is, moved at its own careful pace toward the people. Some time later Unkulunkulu changed his mind and sent the lizard with the opposite message — that humans must die.

The faster lizard reached the people first and delivered its message. By the time the chameleon arrived with the older promise, it was too late. Death had already entered the world. The Zulu remember the story when they see a chameleon on a branch, walking as slowly as ever, and they remember that the world might have been different if the first messenger had only hurried.
zh: 祖鲁人的传说中，最初的存在是「乌恩库伦库鲁」，意为「最伟大的祖先」。世界初晓时，他从一片沼地的芦苇丛中走出。其他存在也接连从同一片芦苇丛中走出——男子、女子、各样动物和万物——直到天地之间各类生灵齐备。乌恩库伦库鲁教第一批人各种规矩：如何用火、如何嫁娶、如何牧牛、如何敬奉亡者。
他想给人类捎去一条消息，说他们本不必死。他选中变色龙作信使，让它带着这则消息上路。变色龙天性慢吞吞，按自己一贯小心翼翼的步伐朝人群走去。过了一段时间，乌恩库伦库鲁却改变了主意，又派出一只蜥蜴去送相反的口信——告诉人类他们必须死。
蜥蜴跑得快，先到了人间，把它捎的话说了出来。等变色龙带着先前那则关于不死的承诺赶到时，已经太迟。死亡已经走进了世界。祖鲁人每当看到变色龙在树枝上仍以一贯的慢节奏爬行时，便会想起这个故事——若当年第一个信使肯走快一点，世道说不定就完全不同。

en: Nyame is the sky god of the Akan people of Ghana. In the time of beginnings he was much closer to the earth, and people could speak to him directly. Folktales say that there was once a woman who pounded her fufu so high with her pestle that the pestle struck Nyame in the eye. He withdrew further into the sky in displeasure, and from that time the heavens were placed far above human reach.

All the stories of the world were once Nyame's stories. The spider Anansi asked him to give them to humanity. Nyame agreed, on the condition that Anansi first bring him four difficult prizes: the python that swallows people, the leopard with teeth like spears, the hornets that sting in clouds, and a hidden fairy of the forest. The price seemed impossible, and Nyame thought he would never have to pay.

Anansi tricked all four into capture, one by one, using flattery and cleverness rather than strength. He brought them up to the sky to Nyame, who could not deny that the trickster had paid the price in full. Nyame handed the stories down, and from then on they were known as the Anansesem, the stories of Anansi, and travelled wherever the Akan people went.
zh: 尼亚美是加纳阿坎人崇拜的天神。世界初始时，他与大地相距甚近，凡人可以直接和他说话。民间故事里说，曾有一位妇人捣木薯团时杵举得太高，竟撞到了尼亚美的眼睛。他一气之下退向更高的天际，从此天穹便被安置在凡人够不到的高处。
世上所有的故事，原本都归尼亚美所有。蜘蛛阿南西去求尼亚美，希望把这些故事赐给人类。尼亚美答应了，但提出条件：阿南西必须先献上四件难取之物——会吞人的巨蟒、牙如长枪的豹、成群叮人的胡蜂，以及躲在林中的森林精灵。这价钱看来是不可能办到的，尼亚美自信永远不必兑现。
阿南西却凭谀辞与机智、不靠蛮力，一个一个地把四物诱入陷阱，逐一献上天界。尼亚美无法否认这骗子英雄已经付清了代价。他从天上把故事一并交了下来。这些故事从此被称作「阿南西的故事」——「阿南塞塞姆」，随着阿坎人迁徙之处，一路流传开去。

en: Long ago, when the earth was still empty, the first man Kintu lived alone with one cow. The sky god Ggulu had a daughter named Nambi, who looked down from heaven and saw Kintu, and wanted to come down and live with him. Her father did not approve of the lonely man on the earth and set Kintu a series of tests to prove his worth.

He gave him a single meal big enough for a hundred people and told him to eat it all alone; he made him pick out his cow from a vast herd; he made him bring tools and resources that should have been impossible to find in a day. With Nambi's secret help and his own cleverness, Kintu passed every test. Ggulu finally consented to the marriage and sent the couple back to the earth.

He warned them not to look back on the way down, lest Nambi's malicious brother Walumbe — "Death" — follow them. They forgot one of Nambi's chickens and turned back to fetch it. Walumbe slipped down after them and followed them to the earth. From him came sickness and dying, which until then had not lived among humans. Kintu and Nambi became the ancestors of the Baganda people of Uganda, but Walumbe stayed with them, and stays with their descendants still.
zh: 很久以前，大地仍空空荡荡，第一个男人金图独自带着一头母牛生活。天神格鲁鲁有个女儿名叫楠比，她从天上望见金图，喜欢上这位孤单的男子，想下到人间和他一起生活。她的父亲不大看得上这位独居凡人，便给金图出了一连串考验，看他配不配。
他端给金图够一百人吃的一顿饭，让他独自吃完；又让他从庞大的牛群里把自己那头母牛认出来；还让他在一天之内找来本来根本拿不到的工具和材料。借楠比暗中相助以及自己机敏的应对，金图把考验一一通过。格鲁鲁这才答应婚事，让两人一同回到人间。
他叮嘱两人，下界途中不许回头，以免楠比那位心怀恶意的兄弟瓦伦贝——「死亡」——跟着下来。两人忘了带楠比的一只鸡，半路转身去取。瓦伦贝便趁机溜下，一路跟到了人间。从那以后，疾病与死亡才开始在人间出现，本来这些都不曾住在人间。金图与楠比此后成了乌干达布干达人的祖先，但瓦伦贝也留在了他们身边，至今仍与他们的后人同在。

en: In many tellings across the Native peoples of western North America, fire was once kept on a far mountain by three fire-beings who would not share it. People down in the valleys froze in winter and ate cold food. Coyote, the trickster, decided to steal fire for them, and he gathered a chain of helpers along the path between the mountain and the village.

He went up the mountain, hid himself, and waited. When the fire-beings dozed, Coyote snatched a brand of fire and ran. The fire-beings woke and chased him down the slope. Coyote ran as fast as he could, and when he could run no further he flung the fire to Squirrel. Squirrel ran with it on his back — and the fire burned a black curl into his tail, which squirrels carry to this day.

Squirrel passed the fire to Chipmunk, whose striped back was scorched by the flame; to Frog, whose tail was burned off as the fire-beings caught his tail in their hands; and Frog spat the fire into Wood, who swallowed it. From then on, fire lived inside wood, and people learned how to draw it out with two dry sticks. Coyote sat down to watch them cook their first warm meal, and saw that he had given a real gift to the world.
zh: 在北美西部许多原住民的讲法里，远古时火只藏在远方一座山顶上，由三位火神严密把守，从不肯分给凡人。山谷里的人冬天挨冻，平时只能吃凉食。骗子英雄郊狼决心替他们去把火盗来。他事先沿着山到村子之间的山路布下一条由其他动物组成的接力队。
他独自摸上山顶，躲在暗处守候。趁火神打盹时，郊狼飞快地抢下一根燃着的火把，撒腿就跑。火神惊醒，沿山追下来。郊狼一路狂奔，跑不动时把火把扔给了松鼠。松鼠驮着火把跑——火苗在它的尾巴上烫出了一道黑色卷曲的纹路，至今松鼠尾巴上仍带着那道痕。
松鼠把火传给花栗鼠，花栗鼠的脊背被火苗烫出几道条纹；又传给青蛙，青蛙的尾巴被追近的火神抓住一截，被烫掉了。青蛙把火吐进木头里，木头一口将它吞下。从此火便藏在了木头中，人们也学会了用两根干木相互摩擦把它请出来。郊狼坐下来看着众人吃上第一顿热饭，知道自己确实送给了世界一份真正的礼物。

en: After the world had been destroyed and made again, the gods decided that a new race of human beings must live on the earth. The task of fetching the bones of the former race fell to the Feathered Serpent Quetzalcoatl. He went down to Mictlan, the underworld, and asked its lord Mictlantecuhtli for the bones, so that he could carry them back and remake humanity.

Mictlantecuhtli agreed, on a condition: Quetzalcoatl must first walk around the underworld blowing a conch shell. The shell had no holes, so Quetzalcoatl called on worms to bore holes and bees to make it sound. The lord of the dead then said the bones could be taken — but in secret he ordered a trap dug across Quetzalcoatl's path.

Quetzalcoatl gathered the bones and ran for the upper world. The ground opened, he fell into the pit, and the bones spilled and broke into many sizes. He picked the pieces back up and carried them to the place where the other gods were waiting. He gave his own blood over the broken bones, and from them living people rose into the new world — but because the bones had been broken into different lengths, humans have come in all sizes ever since.
zh: 前一个世界被毁灭、世界被重新造就之后，诸神决定要让新一代的人类在大地上生活。寻回旧人类骸骨的任务交给了羽蛇神魁札尔科亚特尔。他下到冥界米克特兰，向冥界之主米克特兰特库特里索取那些骨头，好把它们带回去重造人类。
米克特兰特库特里答应了，但提出条件：魁札尔科亚特尔必须先一边绕行冥界，一边吹响海螺号角。那只海螺原本没有任何孔洞，于是魁札尔科亚特尔请来虫子在螺上钻孔、又让蜜蜂在里面发声。冥界之主表面应承让他取走骨头，暗中却命人沿路挖下一道陷阱。
魁札尔科亚特尔把骨头抱在怀里，朝上界飞奔。大地忽然裂开，他坠入坑中，骨头散落出来，摔成了大小不一的碎片。他重新把这些碎骨拾起，带回众神等候之处。他用自己的血浇在断骨上，新一代活着的人类便从中升起来到新的世界——但因为骨头当初摔成了不同长度，从此人类的身量也就有了高有矮。

en: Lac Long Quan was the dragon lord of the rivers and seas, descended from the dragon line. Au Co was a beautiful fairy of the mountains. They met, fell in love, and married, and Au Co gave birth to a great pouch containing one hundred eggs, from which one hundred sons were born. The family grew up together for a time on the coast.

But Lac Long Quan, a being of water, missed the sea, and Au Co, a being of the mountains, missed her highlands. They agreed at last that they could not share one home. They divided their hundred sons between them. Au Co took fifty children up into the mountains; Lac Long Quan took fifty down to the coast.

From the eldest son of those who stayed in the mountains came the first of the Hung kings, the legendary founders of the Vietnamese state. From the rest of the brothers came the different peoples and clans of the country. The Vietnamese remember themselves as the "children of dragon and fairy," tracing their ancestry to this divided family that filled the rivers and the highlands alike.
zh: 雒龙君是江海里的龙王，出身于龙族一脉。妪姬是山中一位美丽的仙女。两人相遇相爱，结为夫妻。妪姬生下一只装着一百个蛋的胎包，从蛋中孵出一百个儿子。一家人一时之间在海边生活在一起。
可雒龙君本是水族，怀念大海；妪姬本是山灵，怀念高山。两人终于无法共住一处，便约定分家。他们把一百个儿子分成两半：妪姬带五十个孩子上山，雒龙君带五十个孩子下海。
留在山中的兄长一脉，便有了越南神话中的雄王——传说中越南国家最早的开国之君。其余兄弟分散开来，成了越南各地不同的部落与氏族。越南人自称为「龙与仙之子」，便是把自己的祖系追到这个把江河与高山一并填满的、注定分离的家庭。

en: During the reign of the sixth Hung king, foreign invaders from the north attacked Vietnam. The king sent messengers throughout the country calling for any man who could drive them out. In a village outside the capital lived a peasant boy who, although three years old, had not yet spoken a single word. When the messenger's cry reached him, he suddenly sat up and said, "Send the messenger to me."

He asked the king for an iron horse, an iron staff, and iron armor. As the smiths worked, he began to eat — and to grow. Bowl after bowl, basket after basket of rice were brought to him, and with every meal he grew taller and stronger. By the time the iron horse was finished he had become a giant.

He put on the armor, mounted the iron horse — which breathed fire — and rode out at the head of the army. He charged into the invading host and broke it. When his iron staff snapped in battle, he tore up bamboo from the roadside and used it as a weapon. After the enemy was driven out, he rode his iron horse up Mount Soc, took off his armor, and rose into the sky on horseback, never seen again. He is honored as one of the four immortal saints of Vietnam.
zh: 雄王六世时，北方异族入侵越南。国王派人到各处张榜，征召能驱敌的勇士。京城外有一个小村庄，村中有个农家孩子，已经三岁了还从未开口说过一句话。听到使者宣旨的声音传来，他忽然坐起身，说：「让使者过来见我。」
他对国王请求了三样东西：一匹铁马、一根铁棒、一副铁甲。铁匠们打造这三件兵器的当口，他开始吃饭——并迅速生长。一碗又一碗、一筐又一筐地喂下饭去，每吃一顿他就长高一截、力气也增一截。等铁马铸成之时，他已长成一位巨人。
他披上铁甲，跨上那匹能喷火的铁马，率军杀向敌阵。他一冲就把侵略大军杀得溃散。铁棒在战斗中被打断，他便就近从路边拔起整丛竹子当作兵器。等敌人被赶出国境，他骑着铁马登上朔山，脱下铠甲，连人带马腾空升入天空，从此再无踪迹。越南人将他奉为「越南四不死圣」之一。

en: Thuy Kieu was a beautiful and gifted young woman of a respectable Vietnamese family, in love with a young scholar named Kim Trong. While Kim was away on family business, Kieu's father was falsely accused and threatened with ruin. To save him, Kieu sold herself in marriage to a stranger, after asking her younger sister to keep her promise to Kim in her place.

The man who bought her turned out to be a procurer. Kieu was sold into a brothel and tried to escape by killing herself, without success. Over many years she passed through one hard fortune after another — sold from house to house, briefly the wife of a generous outlaw chief named Tu Hai who was then betrayed and killed, made a slave again, and finally driven to throw herself into a river.

She was rescued by a Buddhist nun and lived for a time in a temple, broken but at peace. By chance her family found her at last. Kim Trong had married her younger sister as Kieu had asked, but he was still loyal to Kieu in his heart. The two were reunited, and Kieu, now too old and too marked by suffering for marriage, chose to live with him as a sister and a friend. Nguyen Du's long poem about her became Vietnam's national literary classic.
zh: 翠翘是一位出身体面之家、才貌双全的越南少女，与年轻的书生金重相爱。金重因家事远行未归时，翠翘的父亲被人诬告，眼看就要家破人亡。翠翘为了救父，托妹妹替自己守住与金重的婚约，便把自己卖给了一个陌生人作妾。
买下她的人原来是个皮条客。翠翘被卖入妓院，曾试图自尽未遂。此后多年她颠沛流离、屡遭厄运——被一家家青楼倒卖、短暂地嫁给豪侠义贼徐海做妻而徐海后又被设计杀害、再次被沦为奴隶，最后绝望中投江自尽。
她被一位佛门尼姑救起，在寺中住了一段时间，身心虽受创，至少得以暂时安顿。后来机缘巧合中，家人终于找到了她。金重当年依翠翘之托娶了她的妹妹，心中却始终未忘她。两人重逢之后，翠翘已年长又身世憔悴，不愿再以妻子的身份相处，便选择以姐妹与挚友之礼留在他身旁。阮攸笔下这部长篇诗体小说《金云翘传》，从此成为越南民族的文学经典。

en: After the death of the patriarch Krok, the Czech tribes were ruled by his three wise daughters. The youngest, Libuse, had the gift of prophecy. From her hilltop at Vysehrad above the Vltava she judged disputes among the people. In time some of the men of the tribes complained that they would not be ruled by a woman, and asked her to choose a husband to be their prince.

Libuse told them to send her white horse out alone, harnessed but riderless. Wherever the horse stopped of its own will, they would find their future ruler at a plowman's work. The horse walked across the country and stopped before a peasant named Premysl, plowing his field with two oxen. The men brought him back to Vysehrad, and Libuse married him; from them descended the Premyslid line of Czech princes.

Standing above the Vltava with her court, Libuse fell into a prophetic trance and looked out across the forest. She saw a city rising on a hill where a man was hewing the doorsill — "prah" — of his house. "I see a great city," she said, "whose glory will reach the stars." Her people went to the spot she pointed to and founded the city there. From that hewn doorsill, the story says, came the name of Prague.
zh: 捷克部落的族长克罗克死后，他三位睿智的女儿继承了治理之职。其中最小的利布谢有预言的能力。她在伏尔塔瓦河畔高地维谢赫拉德上为族人裁断纠纷。日子久了，部族里有些男子抱怨不愿被一位女子统治，要她另选一位丈夫，做他们的王侯。
利布谢便让他们把她的白马单独放出去，给它套上鞍具，却不许任何人骑。无论那马自行走到哪里停下，他们就会在那里找到一位正在耕地的「未来的君主」。白马走过乡野，停在一位名叫普热米斯尔的农夫面前——他正用一对公牛犁地。族人把他迎回维谢赫拉德，利布谢与他成婚；捷克的普热米斯利德王朝便自他们一脉延续而下。
某日她在朝堂之上俯瞰伏尔塔瓦河，忽然进入预言的恍惚之态，远望林海。她看到一座城市正从一座山丘上升起，而一名工匠正在劈削自家屋舍的门槛——「prah」。她说：「我看到一座伟大的城市，它的荣耀必上达星辰。」族人按照她所指之处前去筑城，定名于那块门槛之上。布拉格（Praha）之名，传说便从那一道被劈开的门槛而来。

en: On a high cliff above a narrow bend of the Rhine, where the current grew dangerous, German legend placed the Lorelei. She was a beautiful young woman who, in some tellings, had thrown herself from the rock after being betrayed by her lover. After her death she returned as a spirit and sat on the cliff at sunset, combing her long golden hair and singing.

Boatmen passing through the bend below would hear her song carried on the water. They looked up to find the source of the music and could not look away. While their eyes were on her, their boats were caught by the swift current and the hidden rocks of that stretch of river.

Many sailors were said to have drowned at that bend, drawn there by the Lorelei's voice. The rock above the river still bears her name today, and Heinrich Heine's poem about her became one of the most loved verses in German. The story passes the warning down — that some forms of beauty, especially those answered with carelessness, cost a man his life.
zh: 莱茵河有一段狭窄而水流湍急的拐弯，岸边一座高崖上，德国传说安放了「罗蕾莱」这个名字。她原本是一位美丽的少女，按一种说法，她因被情人背叛而从崖上一跃而下。死后她化作精灵重回这处崖顶，在夕阳里坐着梳长长的金发，唱起歌来。
船夫驶过下方的拐弯时，会听到歌声沿水面传来。他们抬头去找歌声的来处，便再也移不开视线。眼睛被她吸住时，船却被这一段急流和暗礁夺去了方向。
据说有许多船夫就在那个拐弯处溺亡，正是被罗蕾莱的歌声引去的。河上方那座岩崖至今仍以她为名，海涅的那首咏叹罗蕾莱的诗，也成了德语中最为人爱诵的诗篇之一。这段传说把警示一代代传下来——有些美，尤其是被人轻率回应的美，要拿一条命去偿。

en: In the Amazon basin, the people tell of Iara, a river spirit who lives in the deep pools of the great rivers. In one common version she was once a beautiful young woman, a skilled warrior whose brothers grew jealous of their father's favor toward her. They tried to kill her, and she killed them in defense; in shame, her father had her thrown into the river. The fish there carried her down to safety, and she was changed into a being half-woman, half-fish.

From then on Iara sat on river stones at dusk, combing her long dark hair and singing softly across the water. Men who came down to fish or to bathe heard the song and were drawn toward it. Those who looked into her eyes could not turn away.

She would lead the bewitched man into the deep pool with her and pull him down beneath the water. He was rarely seen again, or, if he returned, he came back broken in mind. Fishermen on the Amazon and its tributaries still warn one another not to linger by the water at twilight, because Iara has not forgotten her songs.
zh: 亚马逊流域的人们讲到伊阿拉——一位住在大河深潭里的水灵。常见的一种说法是，她原本是一位美丽的少女，又是一名出色的女战士，因父亲偏爱她而招致兄长们的妒忌。兄长们想杀她，她出于自卫反将他们杀死。父亲为此蒙羞，便命人把她丢进河里。河中鱼群把她带向河底救下，她从此化作半女半鱼的水中精灵。
从那以后，伊阿拉每到黄昏便坐在河石上，梳着乌黑的长发，对着水面轻轻唱歌。下河打渔或沐浴的男子听见歌声，就会被一步步引过去。看进她眼睛的人，再也无法把目光移开。
她会把被迷住的男子一同拉入深潭，把他拽到水底。他多半再也不会被人看到；即便活着回来，也已神思错乱。亚马逊河及其支流上的渔人至今仍互相告诫，黄昏时分不要在水边逗留过久，因为伊阿拉并没有忘记她的那些歌。

en: Saci is a small mischievous spirit known across Brazil, especially in the countryside. He is dark-skinned, one-legged, wears a red cap, and always has a pipe between his lips. He moves around in a small whirlwind of dust that suddenly springs up on a dry road. Where people see a tiny twister whip through, they say Saci has just passed through.

He is famous for small annoyances rather than great evil. He tangles the manes of horses overnight, sours pots of milk, hides house keys, lets cattle out of their pens, and chases travelers along the road just for fun. Anyone who has lost something to him learns to look in unlikely places.

He can be caught, but only with effort. To trap him you throw a knotted cord into his whirlwind, or capture him inside a bottle and stopper it with a cork marked with a cross. If you take his red cap, he must grant you a wish to get it back. Children in Brazil still know to scatter the little whirlwinds that race past them on a country road, in case the trickster is inside.
zh: 萨西是巴西各地、尤其乡间广为人知的一个调皮小妖。他皮肤黝黑，只有一条腿，头上一顶红色小帽，嘴里永远叼着一只烟斗。他总裹在一阵突然在干路上窜起的小旋风里出没。人们见到田野小路上忽然旋起一团小旋风掠过，就说是萨西刚走过。
他出名的是各种小骚扰，并不算什么大恶。他在夜里把马鬃打成乱结，让奶罐里的牛奶发酸，把家中的钥匙藏起来，把牛从圈里放出去，又会出于好玩沿着小路追在行人后面。被他作弄丢了东西的人，往往得到些意想不到的地方才找得回来。
要抓他也不是不行，只是要费点工夫。可以朝那阵小旋风里扔一根打了结的细绳，或者把他诱入瓶中，再用画着十字的木塞把瓶口塞上。若再夺下他的红帽子，他必须答应你一个愿望才能把帽子讨回去。巴西的孩子至今仍懂得，乡间小路上掠过的小旋风，要拿手把它打散——里面说不定就坐着这位骗子。

en: Among the Tupi-Guarani peoples of Brazil, Curupira is the guardian of the forest. He is a small being, often shown with red hair and a bright body, but his most striking feature is that his feet are turned backward, so that his footprints in the leaf litter point the opposite way from where he is going. Anyone who tries to track him by his prints walks farther and farther away from him.

Curupira protects the animals from hunters who kill more than they need or who waste what they kill. He punishes loggers who fell trees without reason and travelers who do not honor the forest. He confuses them by changing the paths, by imitating the calls of game, and by leading them in circles until they collapse.

Hunters who respect the forest leave him gifts of tobacco or honey on a stump at the edge of the trees, and they are allowed to take what they need. Those who do not learn to fear the small footprints that point the wrong way are sometimes never seen again. In modern Brazil, Curupira has been adopted as a symbol of forest conservation as well as a figure of older fear.
zh: 在巴西的图皮-瓜拉尼诸民族中，库鲁皮拉是森林的守护者。他个头不高，多数版本中红发明亮，最显眼的是他的双脚倒生，脚趾朝后——所以他在林间落叶上留下的脚印，方向与他实际行走的方向完全相反。任何想按他脚印追踪他的人，只会越走越远，再也追不上他。
库鲁皮拉守护林中野兽，专门惩罚那些猎杀超过所需、或浪费猎物的猎人。他也对付那些无故砍伐林木的人，以及不知敬畏森林的过路者。他会变换林中小路、模仿猎物的叫声、引人在林中不停打转，直到迷路者精疲力竭倒下。
懂得敬重森林的猎人会在林缘的树桩上留下烟草或蜂蜜作为供奉，他便容许他们带走所需的猎物。那些不学着害怕「脚印走向相反方向」的人，有时就再也走不出林子了。在今天的巴西，库鲁皮拉既被当作森林保护的象征，也仍保留着旧时那种让人敬畏的形象。

en: Among the Mapuche of southern Chile, two great serpents are said to have shaped the islands of Chiloe and the surrounding coast. Caicai-Vilu was a sea-serpent who ruled the deep waters. Trentren-Vilu lived on the land in the hills. Caicai was angered, the story says, by humans who had grown careless and disrespectful, and he called the sea up against the coast in a great flood.

As the waters rose, Trentren-Vilu rose with them. The land serpent lifted the hills higher and higher and called on people, animals, and birds to climb up onto her back. Those who reached her were saved as the water rose around them. Some who could not climb were turned into seals, fish, or sea birds so that they could survive in the new sea.

The two serpents fought as the flood climbed, until at last Caicai-Vilu's strength gave out and the water began to fall. Where Trentren had pushed the land up the highest, the new mountains and islands of Chiloe remained. The Mapuche say that the broken coastline of southern Chile, with its many islands, is the shape Caicai and Trentren left behind when their battle finally ended.
zh: 智利南部的马普切人中，传说有两条巨蛇塑造了今日的奇洛埃岛和附近海岸。凯凯威吕是海中大蛇，统辖着深水。特伦特伦威吕则住在陆上的山岭里。传说凯凯不满人类日益放肆无礼，召动大海向岸边涌来，掀起一场吞没大陆的洪水。
海水上涨时，特伦特伦威吕也一同腾起。这条陆地大蛇把山岭一层一层向上顶高，召唤人、走兽和飞鸟爬到她背上避难。爬上来的得以保全。来不及爬上去的，被她变成海豹、鱼或海鸟，让他们能在新的海里继续活下去。
两条大蛇在洪水持续上涨时激烈搏斗，最终凯凯威吕力竭，水势开始退去。特伦特伦把陆地顶得最高的地方，便留下了今天奇洛埃岛上那些起伏的山岭与岛屿。马普切人说，智利南部支离破碎的海岸线、那一串大小不一的岛屿，正是凯凯与特伦特伦那场大战最后停下时留下的形状。

en: Among the peoples of the Andes, Pachamama is the earth mother. She is not a distant deity but a present being who lives in the soil and the mountains, in fields and crops, in animals and stones. Everything that grows belongs to her, and farmers, herders, and travelers all owe her thanks before they take from her.

The most familiar ritual to her is the challa, the libation. Before drinking, a person tips the first sip of chicha or wine onto the ground and says, "Para la Pachamama." Before eating in the open, the first piece of food is set aside on the earth for her. Before sowing or harvesting, a small ceremony — coca leaves, sweets, llama fat, or wine — is offered at a small hole dug in the soil.

In August, when the cold has bitten deepest and the planting is about to begin, families across the Andes celebrate the great month of Pachamama. They dig an offering pit, place gifts inside, and ask her to give back fertility to fields, herds, and families. The practice continued under colonization and Christianity, and continues today; for many, the earth itself is still listening for the words and the small gifts.
zh: 安第斯各民族中，「帕查玛玛」是大地母亲。她并非高高在上的神，而是切实就住在大地里的存在——住在土壤、山岭、田野、作物、牲畜与石头之间。一切生长之物都属于她；农人、牧人、过路者，向她取用之前都要先道一声谢。
向她致意最常见的仪式叫「恰拉」，即奠酒。喝酒前，人们会把第一口奇恰玉米酒或葡萄酒洒到地上，口中念一句「献给帕查玛玛」。野外用餐时，第一块食物会先放到地上献给她。播种或收割前要先举行一个小仪式：在土里挖一个小坑，献上古柯叶、糖果、羊驼脂或酒。
每到八月——高地最冷、即将开播的那个月——安第斯各家各户都过「帕查玛玛大月」。他们挖出一个供奉的坑，把礼物一一放进去，请她把丰饶重新还给田地、牲畜和家人。这一套习俗在殖民和基督教传入之后仍未消失，至今依旧延续下来；对许多人来说，大地本身仍在倾听那些话语与那些小小的供物。

en: Pele, the fire goddess of Hawaii, dreamed of a handsome young chief named Lohi'au of the island of Kaua'i. She sent her youngest sister Hi'iaka to fetch him for her. Pele told Hi'iaka to bring him back in forty days, no more, and on no account to fall in love with him. Hi'iaka agreed, asking only that Pele guard the lehua grove that Hi'iaka loved while she was away.

Hi'iaka traveled across the islands and met many trials — angry sorceresses, monsters, and rough country. She arrived at Kaua'i to find that Lohi'au was already dead. She caught his soul in time, restored him to his body, and began the long journey back. As they crossed each island, days slipped past beyond what Pele had allowed.

Pele, growing angry, burned the lehua grove she had promised to guard and killed Hi'iaka's dearest friend Hopoe in the lava flow. When Hi'iaka finally brought Lohi'au home to the crater of Kilauea, she saw the scorched land and lost the last of her patience. She embraced Lohi'au in front of Pele. Pele in fury sent fire and killed him a second time; Hi'iaka brought him back again. After much grief and divine quarrel, Pele finally released them. The story is the long heart of the Pele cycle still told across Hawaii.
zh: 夏威夷的火山女神佩蕾在梦中见到考爱岛上一位俊美的年轻酋长罗希阿乌，便派最小的妹妹希伊阿卡替自己去把他接来。佩蕾要她在四十天内带人回来，一天也不可多，并郑重叮嘱她绝不能爱上他。希伊阿卡答应了，只请佩蕾在自己外出期间替她照看那片心爱的列瓦花林。
希伊阿卡一路跨过群岛，遇到种种考验——脾性恶劣的女巫、各种妖怪、险恶的山野。她抵达考爱岛时，罗希阿乌已经死了。她赶在魂魄散去之前把它收住，重新让它回到躯体里，又开始漫长的归途。每越过一个岛，时间都比佩蕾允许的多滑过几日。
佩蕾渐渐怒火中烧，烧光了自己答应替妹妹看守的那片列瓦花林，又用熔岩夺走了希伊阿卡最亲密的朋友霍波埃的性命。希伊阿卡终于把罗希阿乌带回基拉韦厄火山口，看到那片焦土，再也忍不下去。她当着佩蕾的面拥抱了罗希阿乌。佩蕾盛怒之下喷出烈火，再次让他丧命；希伊阿卡又一次把他复活。在长久的悲伤与神祇间的争执之后，佩蕾终于松手让他们离去。这段故事，是夏威夷各处至今仍在传讲的「佩蕾叙事圈」最绵长的核心。

en: Dobrynya Nikitich was one of the great bogatyrs of Kievan Rus, a warrior of the court of Prince Vladimir. He was famous as a strong man and a skilled diplomat, fluent in many languages. One day, against his mother's warning, he rode out to swim in the Puchai river. While he was in the water, the dragon Zmey Gorynych — a many-headed flying serpent — descended on him from the air.

Dobrynya, unarmed, used what was nearest to hand. He scooped up handfuls of mud and threw them in the dragon's eyes, then climbed back to shore, grabbed a Greek hat full of sand he had left there, and used it as a weapon. He beat the dragon down until it begged for its life. They struck a peace agreement: Zmey would no longer carry off the people of Rus, and Dobrynya would not kill him.

Zmey soon broke the agreement and carried off Prince Vladimir's niece. Dobrynya rode out a second time to a cave in the mountains where the dragon hid. There they fought for three days. He killed all of the dragon's heads, sealed up the cave, and brought the princess and many other captives back to Kiev. The story stayed at the heart of the Russian byliny as one of the clearest images of duty pursued to the end.
zh: 多勃雷尼亚·尼基季奇是基辅罗斯的大壮士之一，是弗拉基米尔大公麾下的勇士。他既以武勇闻名，也以善于辞令著称，通晓多种语言。一天，他不听母亲劝阻，骑马去普恰伊河中游泳。正在水中时，多头飞翔的蛇龙「戈雷尼奇蛇龙」从天而降扑向他。
手无寸铁的多勃雷尼亚就地取材。他抓起一把把河泥砸进蛇龙的眼睛，再爬上岸，拿起留在岸边、内装满沙的希腊式头盔当作武器。他将蛇龙打得节节败退，蛇龙苦苦求饶。两人立下契约：蛇龙从此不再掳走罗斯人民，多勃雷尼亚也不取其性命。
可没过多久，蛇龙就毁约了，竟将弗拉基米尔大公的侄女掳走。多勃雷尼亚再次出征，追到山中那座蛇龙藏身的洞穴。两者在洞前激战三日。他斩下蛇龙所有的头颅，封住洞口，把公主和被掳的众多百姓一并带回基辅。这段故事从此居于俄罗斯「壮士歌」体系的核心，作为「将职责追到底」的一幅最分明的图像被代代传讲。

en: In Slavic folklore, the rusalki are the spirits of young women who died near or in water — most often drowned, sometimes by suicide, sometimes murdered. They live in rivers, lakes, and ponds, and come out most freely in early summer, in the week between green meadows and high summer, called Rusalka Week.

On those nights they sit on the banks combing their long hair, dance in circles in the meadows, and swing on the lower branches of trees. They are beautiful, but they are not at peace. A young man who comes alone to the water or to the woods at the wrong hour may be drawn into their dance.

Once a rusalka has taken hold of a man's hand, she does not let go. In some tellings she tickles him to death; in others she pulls him into the water. The women of the village hung herbs and flowers at doorways during Rusalka Week and went out to the fields together rather than alone, so as not to be caught either by the rusalki's grief or by their longing for company.
zh: 斯拉夫民间故事中，「鲁萨尔卡」是那些死在水边或水中年轻女子的幽灵——大多是溺亡，有的是自尽，有的是被人害死的。她们住在江河、湖泊、池塘里，最容易现身的时节，是初夏从葱绿草甸走向盛夏的那一周，被称为「鲁萨尔卡周」。
在那一周的夜晚，她们坐在岸边梳着自己的长发，在草地上围成圈跳舞，也常坐在树的低枝上荡来荡去。她们美丽动人，却并不安宁。若有年轻男子在不该出现的时辰独自走到水边或林中，便有可能被卷进她们的舞圈。
鲁萨尔卡一旦抓住一个男子的手，便不会再松开。一些讲法里她会一直挠他直到他笑死；另一些讲法里她会把他拽下水。乡间妇女在「鲁萨尔卡周」会在门口挂上香草和野花，并互相结伴下田，不敢独行，以免被她们的哀怨或想找伴的渴望盯上。

en: In Slavic tradition, every great forest has its leshy — a spirit of the woods who is also their guardian. He is most often described as a tall man, sometimes giant, sometimes able to shrink to the size of a leaf, dressed in skins and leaves, with no shadow and no belt. When he walks through his own forest he is hard to see; when he steps out into the field he is the size of the grass.

He watches over the wolves and the wild game, the trees and the streams. He is not exactly evil, but he is not human, and he is jealous of those who come into the woods without proper respect. He can lead travelers in circles, turn paths around, imitate familiar voices, and make a person walk for hours past landmarks they thought they knew.

If you are lost because of the leshy, the cure is to take your clothing off and put it back on inside out, or to switch your left and right shoes. Once you are no longer the person he tricked, the path becomes clear again. Hunters who wished to take game from his woods left him a little bread and salt on a tree stump first, and asked his permission to pass through what was his.
zh: 斯拉夫传统中，每一片大森林都有自己的「列希」——既是森林的精灵，也是它的守护者。他多被描绘为一个高大的男子，有时如巨人般，有时又能缩到一片树叶大小，身上披着兽皮和树叶，没有影子，腰间也不束带。在自家森林里行走时他几乎隐身；走出林子到田野上时，他便只有草高。
他守护狼群与各种猎物，也守护树木和小溪。他并不算邪恶，但毕竟不是人，对于不知敬畏便闯入林中的人极为不满。他可以让人在林中绕圈、把小径来回打转、模仿熟悉的声音呼唤，又能让人一连几个小时反复经过自以为熟识的地标。
如果你是被列希弄迷了路，对付的办法是把衣服全部脱下再反着穿回，或者把左右两只鞋互换。等你不再是「他刚才作弄的那个人」时，路便会自己清晰起来。猎人若想从他的森林里捕猎，会先在一处树桩上留点面包和盐，请他准许自己穿过他的领地。

en: In Thailand, the story of Mae Nak Phra Khanong is one of the most loved ghost tales. Nak was a young woman who lived in the village of Phra Khanong with her husband Mak. While she was pregnant, Mak was conscripted into the army. Nak died in childbirth while he was away, but her love for him was so strong that she stayed behind in their house with the baby, as if nothing had changed.

When Mak returned from the war, he found his wife and child waiting for him. They lived together happily for some time before he began to notice strange things. One day he watched her drop a lime through the floor of their stilt house and stretch her arm impossibly long to pick it up. He understood at last that she was a ghost.

He fled to a temple, and Nak's spirit, furious at being abandoned, terrorized the village. Several monks tried and failed to subdue her. At last a powerful monk, in some versions the future Somdet To, sealed her spirit into a piece of her own skull bone, which he wore tied to his belt. Mae Nak's shrine still stands in modern Bangkok, where many people come to thank her or to ask favors of her.
zh: 在泰国，「帕卡农的湄娜」是流传最广、最深入人心的鬼故事之一。湄娜是帕卡农村里的一位年轻女子，与丈夫马克成婚。怀孕期间，马克被征召入伍。她在丈夫不在家时难产去世，可她对丈夫的眷恋实在太深，便带着孩子留在原本的家中，仿佛一切都未曾改变。
马克退伍归来时，发现妻子和孩子还在等他。一家三口又过了一段安乐日子，他才渐渐察觉异样。某天他看到她从竹架房的木板缝里掉下一个酸柚，她的手臂竟拉长到不合常理的长度去把它捡起来。他这才明白，她其实是鬼。
马克吓得逃进寺庙，被丢下的湄娜灵魂震怒，在村里到处作祟。几位高僧出手都未能镇住她。最后一位法力极高的师父——一些版本里是后来的颂德托大师——把她的灵魂封入她自己的一块头骨之中，并把这块骨头随身系在腰间。今天曼谷仍有「湄娜祠堂」，无数信众前来酬谢她、或向她讨愿。

en: Gesar was sent down to earth from heaven to set the world right at a time when demons, wicked kings, and disorder filled every region. He was born to a poor mother in the kingdom of Ling, on the high plateau of Tibet. His childhood was hidden and humble; people who knew him saw only a strange and ragged boy, not a king.

When he came of age, Gesar entered a great horse race that would decide the kingship of Ling. Against far better-equipped rivals, he won and was crowned. From the throne of Ling he led campaigns in every direction. He defeated the demon king of the north, the giant of the east, the wicked king Lutsen of the south, and the rulers of many other realms whose rule had become harsh.

His epic — recited still by traveling bards on the high plateau — is the longest in the world, with many tens of thousands of verses describing his battles, his wisdom, and his rides. The cycle says that Gesar finally rose back to heaven, with his queen and his horse, but that he is still kept in reserve to return if the world should again grow as dark as it had been before.
zh: 格萨尔王是从天界被派下凡间的：彼时人间妖魔横行、暴君遍地，秩序一片混乱，他奉命到人间扫荡乱象。他生于青藏高原的岭国，母亲家境清贫。他的童年隐藏而平凡，认识他的人只看到一个邋里邋遢的怪小孩，并不觉得他是什么国王。
他成年后参加了一场关乎岭国王位归属的赛马大会。面对装备远胜自己的对手，他凭实力胜出，并被加冕为王。登上岭国王位之后，他向四方出征：先后击败了北方的妖魔之主、东方的巨人、南方的暴君鲁赞，以及其他暴政横行的诸国之主。
关于他的史诗——至今仍在高原上由游吟艺人继续讲唱——是世界上篇幅最长的一部，几十万行诗句记载了他的征战、智谋与骑行。整套史诗最后说，格萨尔与王后和神驹一同重返天界，但他仍被天上留作一份后备：若日后人间再陷入旧时那般的黑暗，他还可以再下凡来。

en: Amina was a princess of the Hausa city-state of Zazzau, in what is now northern Nigeria. From an early age she trained in horsemanship and weapons rather than in the courtly arts expected of a princess. After her mother's death she inherited the throne, and at the time her city was one of several Hausa centers contesting for influence over the trade routes of the central Sudan.

Amina chose to lead her armies in person. She extended Zazzau's power south and west, fighting campaign after campaign and adding new territory each season. She is said to have refused to marry any one man as her king, taking instead a single warrior at each conquered town for one night and having him executed at dawn so that no rival could rise from her bed.

Behind her armies she ordered earthen walls thrown up around the towns she took. The remnants of these long earthen ramparts, called "Amina's walls," were still standing across northern Nigeria centuries later. She is remembered as the queen who turned a trading city into a small military empire, riding always at the head of her own host.
zh: 阿米娜是豪萨城邦扎扎乌的公主，扎扎乌位于今天尼日利亚北部。她从小不学宫廷淑女功课，反而苦练骑射与兵器。母亲去世后她继承王位，此时扎扎乌正与几个豪萨城邦在中央苏丹商路上明争暗斗。
阿米娜亲自统军出征。她将扎扎乌的势力一路向南、向西扩展，连年用兵，一季又一季吞并新地。传说她拒绝以任何一人为夫为王，而是每攻下一座城便挑一名当地勇士同度一夜，天明便将他处死，以免有人借「她的丈夫」之名挑战王权。
她在军队之后命人在所攻占的城镇周围筑起厚重的土墙。那些蜿蜒数百里的土垣——被后世称作「阿米娜之墙」——直到几个世纪后仍立在尼日利亚北部。她被记作那个把一座商业城市改造成小型军事帝国、永远亲率部属冲在阵前的女王。

en: In the Yoruba city of Ile-Ife, the people were repeatedly raided by an enemy known as the Ugbo, who came out of the forest dressed in raffia palm so that their attackers looked like spirits. The townspeople were too frightened to fight. The queen Moremi made a vow at the river Esimirin: if it would help her save her city, she would offer back her dearest possession.

She allowed herself to be captured in the next raid. Taken to the Ugbo court, she so impressed their king with her beauty and wit that he made her his wife. While she lived among them, she learned the secret of their raffia disguises and how they could be set on fire. She escaped at last and brought the knowledge back to Ile-Ife.

The next time the Ugbo attacked, the people of Ile-Ife were waiting with torches. The raffia disguises caught fire, and the attackers were routed. Moremi returned to the river to fulfill her vow. The river demanded her only son, Oluorogbo. She gave him up, with great grief, and her people mourned with her. Moremi is honored to this day in Ile-Ife as the queen who had bought her city's freedom at the price of her own child.
zh: 约鲁巴名城伊费屡遭一支叫「乌格波」的敌人侵扰。乌格波人从林中出兵，浑身披着拉菲亚棕榈纤维，看起来活像神灵，伊费城民吓得不敢抵抗。王后莫雷米来到埃西米林河畔立誓：若河神助她保住故土，她愿献上自己最珍爱之物作为回报。
她故意让自己在下一次袭击中被俘。被掳到乌格波宫中后，她以美貌与机智深深打动了那里的国王，被立为妃。她借此打入对方内部，摸清了那种拉菲亚伪装的来历，也得知它最怕的就是火。她终于逃出，把这些情报带回伊费。
乌格波人再次袭击时，伊费城民早已手持火把等着他们。一披身的拉菲亚棕榈一点就着，敌军溃不成军。莫雷米回到河边履行旧誓。河神所要的，恰恰是她的独子奥卢罗格博。她忍痛把儿子献了出来，全城与她一同哀悼。直到今日，伊费的人仍以隆重祭祀来记住这位用自己的孩子，为城邦换来自由的王后。

en: Mami Wata, "Mother Water," is a water spirit known across West and Central Africa and, through the slave trade, across the African diaspora. She is usually pictured as a beautiful long-haired woman, sometimes half-fish, often with a snake wound around her. She lives in the deep waters, fresh and salt, and she is rich beyond measure.

Those she favors may receive sudden wealth, beauty, or success in business. But Mami Wata gives nothing without taking. The man or woman she takes for a lover must keep silent about her, and often must remain faithful to her alone, never marrying a mortal. Those who break her terms are ruined as suddenly as they were raised.

She is honored at altars laid with mirrors, perfumes, sweet drinks, and white cloth. Believers among traders, fishermen, and city-dwellers offer her gifts before any new venture, and dancers in her cult go into trance and speak with her voice. From the markets of Lagos and Cotonou to the candomble houses of Brazil, the image of the long-haired water woman with the serpent has traveled wherever the deep water has carried it.
zh: 「玛米瓦塔」即「水之母」，是流传于西非、中非乃至随贩奴航线遍布非洲离散社群的水之精灵。她常被描绘为一位长发飘飘的美丽女子，有时下半身是鱼，身上常缠着一条蛇。她住在淡水或咸水的深处，财宝无尽。
受她垂青的人，可能会突然得到财富、美貌或事业上的成功。但玛米瓦塔从不白白给予——她会同时取走某些东西。被她相中作为情人的男女，必须对她守口如瓶，往往还必须只忠于她一人，不再与凡间另娶另嫁。一旦违背与她的约定，那些被她抬起的人，就会如他们被抬起时一样骤然跌落。
她的祭坛上常摆着镜子、香水、甜饮和白布。在商人、渔民和都市信众中，许多人在动手一桩新事业前先向她献礼。她的祭仪舞者在出神之中以她的声音说话。从拉各斯、科托努的市集，到巴西的「坎东布雷」会堂，这位「身缠蛇的长发水女」的形象，随着深水漂流到哪里，便在哪里继续被供奉。

en: Among the Zulu and Xhosa peoples of South Africa, the tokoloshe is a small, hairy, mischievous creature said to slip into houses at night. He is no taller than a child but very strong, and he can become invisible by swallowing a pebble. He likes to terrorize sleepers, especially women, and he plays small spiteful tricks on a household.

He is said to be sent into a house by a witch who has a grudge against the people there. Once inside, the tokoloshe may strangle the sleepers, sit on their chests, or simply break their dishes and steal small things. In old beds his short height was the only protection against him; the cure was to put the bed up on bricks so that he could not reach to harm anyone asleep on it.

Even today some homes in southern Africa keep their beds raised off the floor for the same reason, whether or not the people who live there still believe in the small night creature. The tokoloshe has remained a shared image of how easily one's home might be entered after dark, and of the small unseen things that can press on a chest from below.
zh: 在南非的祖鲁和科萨人中，「托科洛希」是一种小小的、毛茸茸的调皮怪物，传说夜里会溜进人家。他不比小孩高，却力气惊人，吞下一块小卵石便能隐身。他喜欢吓睡梦中的人，尤其爱吓妇人，又会对一户人家干些小小的坏事。
据说托科洛希是被某个怀恨那户人家的巫师差遣进屋的。进屋之后，他可能会掐住熟睡者的脖子，或坐到他们胸口上压得他们透不过气，也可能只是打碎碗碟、偷走些小物件。在传统床铺上，他唯一够不到的是抬高的床——补救之法便是用砖把床脚一一垫高，让他无论如何都搆不到床上的人。
时至今日，南非许多家庭仍习惯把床抬离地面，无论住在那里的人是否真信夜里这小怪物。托科洛希成了一个共同的形象，提醒人们：夜里家门多么容易被悄悄推开，也提醒人们那种从床底压上胸口的、看不见的小重量。

en: In the folklore of southern Africa, the grootslang — Afrikaans for "great snake" — is said to live in a deep cave in the Richtersveld of South Africa. It is described as a huge creature with the head and trunk of an elephant and the body of a serpent, and it is enormously old.

The story is that long ago the gods made a single creature so powerful that they regretted it almost at once. To correct their mistake, they cut the first grootslangs in two, making elephants from one half and serpents from the other. A few of the original beings escaped, and one of them retreated into the cave that still bears the name.

The grootslang is said to hoard diamonds in its cave. Travelers who venture into its valley risk being dragged down into the pool inside the cave; some accounts say it will spare a traveler in exchange for a large enough offering of jewels. The tale is one of those that has grown out of the meeting of indigenous Khoi-San stories, Dutch settler folklore, and the diamond country of the desert.
zh: 南部非洲民间故事中，「格鲁特斯朗」——南非荷语意为「大蛇」——据说住在南非里克特斯费尔德的一处深洞之中。它被描述为一种庞然巨物，长着大象的头与象鼻，蛇身长得看不到尽头，年纪极其古老。
传说远古时诸神造出了一种过于强大的生灵，刚一造出就后悔了。为了补救，他们把最初的几条格鲁特斯朗拦腰斩成两段，用其中一半做出了今日的大象，用另一半做出了普通的蛇。但仍有几条原始格鲁特斯朗逃脱开来，其中一条退入今天那处仍以它为名的洞穴里。
据说格鲁特斯朗在洞中藏着大量钻石。胆敢闯进它山谷的人，有被它拖入洞中水潭里的风险；也有说法称，只要献上足够多的宝石作礼物，它会放过来人。这个传说，是科伊-桑人原住民故事、荷裔殖民者民间想象与沙漠钻石带的现实，三股力量交汇而长出来的一段奇谈。

en: Among the Dogon of Mali, the Nommo are the first beings created by the high god Amma. They are amphibious, born in pairs, with smooth bodies suited to water. From the start they were responsible for putting the cosmos in order. The first attempt at creation went wrong; one of the original beings broke the pattern, and Amma had to start again.

Amma sent down a great ark in which the Nommo descended to the earth, carrying with them the seeds of all plants and animals and the patterns of human society. Some Dogon accounts describe the ark as turning in the sky like a great whirlwind, coming down to a particular spot on the earth where the people then settled.

From the Nommo came water for the rivers, the right way of speaking, the calendar, blacksmithing, weaving, and all the other arts the Dogon practice. Their pairs are remembered in the doubled images of Dogon sculpture and the doubled forms of their granaries. The Dogon say that one day the Nommo will return, and the rituals and dances they keep are partly a way of preparing the world for that return.
zh: 马里的多贡人中，「诺莫」是天神安玛所造的第一批存在。他们身躯光滑，两栖适水，一对对成双而生。世界一开始的安排就交托给他们。最初的创世尝试出了岔子，其中一位原初存在打破了既定模式，安玛只好把整套创世从头再来。
安玛后来又派下一艘巨大的方舟，诺莫乘坐方舟降临人间，舟中带着所有植物与动物的种子，以及人类社会该有的种种秩序。多贡的一些讲法说，那艘方舟在空中如巨大的旋风一样旋转着落下，落到了大地上一个特定的地点，多贡人此后便在那里定居。
河流的水、合宜的言谈、历法、铁匠、纺织以及多贡人所行的种种技艺，皆出自诺莫之手。他们「成双」的形象，留在多贡雕像的双联造型与谷仓的对偶结构之中。多贡人说，诺莫终有一天会回来；他们流传的仪式与舞蹈，正是为这一份归来在准备世界的样子。

en: The Manas epic is the great song-cycle of the Kyrgyz people, central to their identity for many centuries. It tells of Manas, a warrior-leader born to a clan that had been driven from its ancestral lands by enemies and forced into exile. From an early age Manas was huge, strong, and remembered the wrong done to his people.

As he grew up he gathered around him forty companions — the chorobashi — and at last began the long campaigns to reunite the scattered Kyrgyz clans. He fought the Kalmyks and the Manchu. He traveled across mountains and rivers, took fortresses, made alliances and broke false ones, and led his people back to their old country.

The epic continues in further parts with his son Semetey and his grandson Seitek, so that the full Manas trilogy follows three generations of leadership and struggle. Recited by special bards called manaschi, who go into a kind of trance to perform it, the Manas is one of the longest oral epics in the world. It remained a living core of Kyrgyz memory through centuries of empire, exile, and recovery.
zh: 《玛纳斯》是吉尔吉斯人的伟大史诗，多个世纪里一直处于这一民族认同的中心。它讲的是英雄玛纳斯——他所在的部落被敌人逐出祖居之地，沦为流亡之民。玛纳斯自小就魁梧强壮。他从未忘记族人所受的屈辱。
长大后他身边聚拢了「四十勇士」，并开始漫长的征战，把散落各地的吉尔吉斯各支部落重新聚合在一起。他与卡尔梅克人和满洲诸部交战，翻越山岭，渡过江河，攻取要塞，结过盟也撕过盟，最终把族人带回故土。
这部史诗后面还有他儿子萨梅泰、孙子谢伊泰克两部，合称《玛纳斯三部曲》，跨越三代领袖与他们各自的奋斗。专门吟诵这部史诗的「玛纳斯齐」会进入近乎出神的状态来演唱。它是世界上最长的口传史诗之一，在数个世纪的帝国统治、流亡与重新立足之中，始终活在吉尔吉斯人的记忆深处。

en: Ekeko is a small smiling figure honored in the Bolivian and southern Peruvian highlands as the bringer of abundance. He is shown as a short, round man, often with a moustache. He wears a poncho and a knit cap and carries many tiny bundles tied to his body — miniature sacks of food, money, tools, and household goods.

Each year in La Paz on the 24th of January, the great Alasitas festival is held in his honor. People go to the festival and buy miniatures of what they wish to have during the coming year — small dollar bills, tiny diplomas, model cars, model houses, small bags of grain. The miniatures are blessed and given to Ekeko, in the belief that he will help bring the real things into the buyer's life by the year's end.

His worship goes back to pre-Inca times in the region. It survived under both Inca and Spanish rule by absorbing pieces of each. Today Ekeko stands in homes, on shop counters, and at market stalls across the Andes, accepting cigarettes lit for him and small sips of alcohol — a domestic god of prosperity in a region that has always known the unevenness of harvests.
zh: 埃克科是玻利维亚和秘鲁南部高原上备受敬奉的「丰饶之神」。他被造成一个矮矮胖胖的笑面男子形象，多有八字胡。他身穿斗篷、戴一顶针织帽，全身上下挂满了无数微缩物品的小包——一小袋食物、一小叠钞票、各色工具、各种日用品。
每年 1 月 24 日，拉巴斯都会举行盛大的「阿拉西塔斯节」专门敬奉他。人们到节场上购买自己来年想要拥有之物的迷你版——小面额美元、小毕业证、玩具车、玩具房、小袋谷物。这些微缩物被祈祝过后献给埃克科，信众相信他会在年底之前把真实的对应之物带进自己的生活。
他的信仰可追溯到这一带前印加时期的传统。这一传统在印加与西班牙两番统治之下都靠吸收对方一部分元素而存活下来。今天的埃克科被放在安第斯各地的家中桌上、店面柜上和集市摊位上，接受信众替他点上的香烟、献上的几口小酒——这位在一个收成永远难料的地区里的家庭式丰饶之神。

en: Pecos Bill, in American tall tale, was a baby who fell off the back of his family's wagon as they crossed the Pecos River. Coyotes found him and raised him as one of their own. He grew up running on all fours and howling at the moon, until years later a passing cowboy convinced him that he was a man and rode him back to civilization.

Once among humans, Bill set out to be the greatest cowboy who had ever lived. He used a rattlesnake as a lasso and rode a mountain lion as his horse, until he caught a wild stallion he called Widow-Maker that no other man could ride. He invented many of the cowboy's tricks, including, the stories say, the lasso itself.

When a tornado came across the plains, Pecos Bill roped it and rode it across three states, leaving the Grand Canyon scratched into the earth where he had dragged a hoof. He fell in love with Slue-Foot Sue, who tried to ride Widow-Maker on her wedding day, was bounced up into the sky on her bustle, and bounced until she had to be cut down. Bill's adventures lived on in the bunkhouses of the cattle drives and the pages of pulp magazines.
zh: 美国西部「夸张故事」里的佩科斯·比尔，是一个婴儿时从家中马车上掉下来、滚入佩科斯河边的小孩。一窝郊狼把他拾回去，当作自家小郊狼养大。他从小四脚奔跑，对月嗥叫，过了许多年才被一个路过的牛仔说服自己其实是人，被他带回了人类社会。
回到人间后，比尔立志要做有史以来最厉害的牛仔。他以响尾蛇为套索，骑山狮代马，直到后来驯服了一匹野种公马，自取其名「寡妇制造者」——别人谁也驾驭不了它。牛仔之间许多看家本领，包括套索的发明，传说都是他鼓捣出来的。
有一回平原上起了一场龙卷风，比尔把它套住，骑着它横扫过三个州；他坐骑的一只蹄子拖在地上，就拖出了一整条大峡谷。他爱上了「滑脚苏」，她在两人婚礼那天非要骑寡妇制造者不可，被弹到天上，靠着裙撑一弹一弹，最后还得人把她从空中切下来。比尔的故事在赶牛人的工棚里、廉价小说的书页上世代流传。

en: Among the Wabanaki peoples of the northeast woodlands and coast, Gluskap is the great culture hero, a giant who shaped the land in the time of beginnings. After he had finished much of his work, he wished to cross to an island in the open sea. There was no canoe large enough for him.

Gluskap called to the whales for help. A great whale rose, lifted her head from the water, and offered to carry him on her back. He climbed on, and she set off. As they swam, she warned him that if she felt the bottom under her belly with her flippers, she might be left stranded. She asked him to call out if he saw the bottom growing too near.

Gluskap did as she asked, but for fun he kept his mouth shut just a little too long, and the whale ran herself up on a beach with him on her back. She was so big she could not get herself back into deep water. Gluskap, laughing, put his hands beneath her, lifted her like a man might lift a salmon, and slid her back into the sea. After that he and the whales remained friends, and people of the Wabanaki coast still know who held the seas open for them.
zh: 在东北部林海与海岸的瓦巴纳基诸民族中，格卢斯卡普是伟大的「文化英雄」——一位在创世之初塑造大地的巨人。他基本完成手头的工作之后，想去外海一座岛上看看，可是没有哪艘独木舟载得动他这么大的身躯。
格卢斯卡普呼唤海中诸鲸来帮忙。一头巨大的母鲸浮出水面，把头从浪中抬起，让他坐到自己背上。他爬上去，母鲸驮着他出发了。游着游着她叮嘱他：万一她的鳍碰到了海底，她就有搁浅的危险，让他看到水底太浅时一定要立刻喊她。
格卢斯卡普起初照办，但他贪玩，故意憋着一句话不说，结果母鲸驮着他直接冲上了沙滩。她身躯太大，自己回不到深水里去。格卢斯卡普哈哈大笑，把手伸到她身下，像拎一条大鲑鱼似地把这位巨鲸抬起来，再轻轻送回深水之中。从此他与海中鲸群结成朋友，海岸边的瓦巴纳基人也至今记得，是谁替他们撑开了这片大海。

en: The mermaid of Warsaw, the Syrenka, is said to have been the sister of the Little Mermaid of Copenhagen. The two sisters set out together from the Baltic Sea, but at the mouth of the Vistula one decided to swim up the great river. She came at last to a sand bank near the small fishing settlement that would later become Warsaw, and she liked the place so much that she stayed.

She lived among the reeds and was friends with the local fishermen, who often heard her singing in the evenings. They left her offerings of bread and fish, and she warned them about storms. One day, however, a rich merchant heard about her and decided to catch her and sell her in his shop. He trapped her in a net and locked her in a wooden cage on his property.

The fishermen of the river broke down the merchant's gate at night and set the mermaid free. From then on, the story says, she swore to protect the city. She is shown today on Warsaw's coat of arms with a sword in one hand and a shield in the other, half-woman, half-fish, ready to guard the place that once defended her.
zh: 华沙的美人鱼「西伦卡」据说是哥本哈根那位小美人鱼的姐妹。两姐妹一同从波罗的海出发，在维斯瓦河入海口分手，其中一位决定逆流而上，沿大河北上。她最终游到一处沙洲附近——那里有一座小渔村，日后便发展为华沙。她喜欢这地方，索性留了下来。
她住在芦苇荡中，与当地渔民结为朋友，傍晚时常听见她在水边唱歌。渔民们留下面包和鱼献给她，她则提前告诉他们风暴将至。后来有位富商听说了这件事，决定把她抓住卖到店里展出。他用网把她捕住，关在自家院里的一只木笼里。
河上的渔民们当夜冲进富商家中，砸开大门把美人鱼救出。传说从那以后，她立誓要永远守护这座城市。今日华沙的市徽上便有她的形象——半女半鱼，一手持剑、一手持盾，随时准备护卫这片当年曾保住她的土地。

en: Pan Twardowski was a Polish nobleman and learned magician of the sixteenth century. Like Faust in Germany, he made a bargain with the devil, signing in his own blood: in exchange for great magical knowledge and power, his soul would belong to the devil after his death. But Twardowski insisted that the devil could only collect him in the city of Rome, where Twardowski had no intention of ever going.

He served at the court of King Sigismund Augustus and was said to have summoned the spirit of the king's dead wife Barbara so that the grieving monarch could see her again. He worked in Krakow as an alchemist and astrologer. For years he avoided Rome. The devil, growing impatient, finally tricked him: he lured him to an inn called "Rome" in a small Polish village.

Inside the inn, the devil revealed himself and dragged Twardowski toward the door to take him away. Twardowski began desperately to sing a hymn to the Virgin Mary. His soul was saved from Hell — but the devil could not let go entirely, and lifted him into the sky on the way down. Twardowski was caught between, hanging in the air. He landed on the moon. Polish stories say he is still there, and the cock that he kept as a pet calls down to him from the moon's edge to this day.
zh: 特瓦尔多夫斯基先生是十六世纪波兰的一位贵族，精通各类学问与魔法。他与德国的浮士德相仿，与魔鬼立约，用自己的血签字：以无穷的法力与知识换取自己死后灵魂归魔鬼。但他给契约加了一道附则——魔鬼只能在罗马城里收他的灵魂，而他打定主意终生不去罗马。
他出仕齐格蒙特·奥古斯都国王的宫廷，据说曾召出国王亡妻芭芭拉的幽魂，让悲痛的君主再见她一面。他在克拉科夫从事炼金与占星，多年里始终远离罗马。魔鬼终于失去耐心，设了一计：把他诱到波兰乡间一家名叫「罗马」的小客栈。
进入那家客栈后，魔鬼现出原形，拉住他便要把他带走。特瓦尔多夫斯基拼命唱起一首献给圣母玛利亚的圣咏。他的灵魂得以不堕地狱——可魔鬼也不肯完全放手，把他向天上一抛，他便挂在了空中。最后他落在了月亮上。波兰民间故事说，他至今仍住在月亮上，他从前养的那只公鸡，则一直从月亮的边缘冲他打鸣。

en: The road through the Schollenen Gorge in the Swiss Alps was one of the hardest in Europe to keep open. The river Reuss runs through a deep cleft, and every bridge the locals built over it was washed away. At last, in despair, an old story says, the chief mason cried out, "Let the devil build us a bridge!"

The devil appeared at once and offered to build a bridge that would stand. In return, he demanded the soul of the first to cross it. The villagers agreed. The devil built a fine stone arch in a single night. Then he sat on the far side waiting for the first soul.

The villagers were too clever for him. They drove a young goat across the bridge first. The devil, furious at being cheated, ran off and tried to fetch a great stone to throw at the bridge in revenge. Along the way he met an old woman who marked the stone with a cross, and he could not lift it again. He dropped the stone in disgust, and it lies along the road below the gorge to this day. The pass remained open through his bridge for centuries.
zh: 瑞士阿尔卑斯山中穿过舍伦嫩峡谷的小路，是欧洲最难维持通畅的道路之一。罗伊斯河在峡谷里飞流而下，当地人在此搭起的每一座桥都被冲毁。古老传说说，最后那位石匠绝望之下大喊一声：「让魔鬼来给我们造桥吧！」
魔鬼当即现身，答应造一座永不被冲垮的桥。条件是：第一个过桥的灵魂归他。村民们答应了。魔鬼一夜之间立起一座漂亮的石拱，然后坐在桥的另一端等第一个过桥的人。
村民们却比他更狡猾。他们把一只小山羊先赶过了桥。魔鬼气得跳脚，扭头去搬一块巨石想砸毁这座桥泄愤。半路上他遇到一位老妇人，老妇人在石头上画了个十字，他再也搬不动了。他怒而把石头丢在路旁，那块巨石至今仍躺在峡谷下方的山道边。这条要道便靠他造的那座桥，几个世纪里始终畅通无阻。

en: In Slavic folklore, the vodyanoy is the spirit of a deep pond, mill-pond, or river hole. He is usually shown as a fat, slimy old man with a long green beard, sometimes with frog-like features, who lives at the bottom of the water. He keeps a household of drowned souls and grumbles when his peace is disturbed.

Millers, fishermen, and bee-keepers — those whose work depends on water — used to make small offerings to him: a pinch of tobacco, a glass of vodka, occasionally a sacrificial animal at the building of a new mill. In return the vodyanoy would not turn the wheel too hard, not steal the catch, not flood the meadow. A miller who neglected him risked the broken dam, the empty net, the drowned worker.

He was said to drag careless swimmers down to his underwater house. Children who ignored their parents and went to the water at night were the easiest catch. He stays in the deeper folk memory as the figure that explains why the smooth surface of the millpond at dusk is also the place where someone never comes home.
zh: 在斯拉夫民间故事中，「水怪沃迪亚诺伊」是深潭、磨坊池或河中深穴的精灵。他多被描绘成一个又肥又黏的老头，长着一把长长的绿胡子，有时还有蛙类的特征，住在水底。他在水下养着一群溺亡的灵魂，一旦清静被搅扰便会忿忿不平。
磨坊主、渔夫和养蜂人——所有靠水吃饭的人——常常向他献小礼：一小撮烟草、一小杯伏特加，磨坊新立时还会献上一只祭祀的牲畜。作为回报，沃迪亚诺伊不会把水轮转得太狠，不会偷走渔获，也不会让水漫到草甸。若磨坊主对他怠慢，他便可能毁掉水坝、空走渔网，或淹死一名工人。
据说他还会把不留意的泳者拉下水底，带回自己的水下宅第。不听父母劝阻、夜里偷偷下水的孩子，是他最容易捉到的猎物。在更深的民间记忆里，他正是用来解释——为什么黄昏时磨坊池那平静无波的水面，同时也常是某人再也回不了家的地方。

en: In Romanian folklore, the strigoi is a restless dead person who returns from the grave to harm the living. Some are born strigoi — children born with a caul, with a tail, or as the seventh child of one sex in a family — but most become strigoi after death. A man who lived violently, who died without proper funeral rites, or who was the victim of a curse might rise up out of his grave at night.

The strigoi returns to its own family first. It comes back to the house where it lived, drinks from the well, sits at the table, and slowly wastes the life of those who knew it. The household grows sick and weak without obvious cause; cattle die in the byre; women miscarry. The neighbors begin to whisper about the new grave outside the village.

The cure, in the old practice, was severe. The body was dug up, and if it was found undecayed, with fresh blood at the mouth, it was identified as strigoi. Its heart was cut out, sometimes burned, sometimes pierced with iron, and the ashes given to the family to drink. Then the household recovered. The stories of strigoi crossed the Carpathians into the wider European imagination, and helped to give shape to the modern vampire.
zh: 罗马尼亚民间故事中，「斯特里戈伊」是不肯安息的亡者，会从坟墓中归来害活人。其中一部分是「生而为斯特里戈伊」——生时带有胎膜、长有尾巴，或在家中作为同性别的第七个孩子出生——但大多数是死后才变成斯特里戈伊的。生前行事凶暴、死时未行妥当葬仪，或受过诅咒之人，便可能在夜里从坟中爬起来。
斯特里戈伊首先回到的是自家。他重回他曾住过的屋子，从家里的井中喝水，坐到饭桌前，慢慢消耗每一位他认识之人的生命。家中接连莫名生病、衰弱，圈里的牲口接连倒毙，妇人接连小产。村中邻里也开始低声议论那座新立在村外的坟。
旧时的「解咒」之法极为残酷：把尸体重新挖出，如果发现仍未腐烂、嘴角带血，便被认作斯特里戈伊。家人会把它的心脏取出来，或焚烧，或以铁钉钉穿，再把骨灰让家中之人冲水喝下。此后病弱者才陆续恢复。斯特里戈伊的故事越过喀尔巴阡山，进入更广阔的欧洲想象，是塑造今日「吸血鬼」形象的源头之一。

en: Sang Kancil, the mouse-deer, is the most beloved trickster of Malay and Indonesian folktale. He is small and graceful, with no strength against the larger animals of the forest, but his wit always saves him. In the most famous of his stories he wishes to cross a wide river but a crocodile waits in the water and would happily eat him.

Sang Kancil calls out to the crocodile: "The king of the forest has ordered me to count all the crocodiles in this river so that they can be given gifts." The crocodile, flattered, calls his brothers and cousins to line up across the river. Sang Kancil hops from the head of one crocodile to the next, counting aloud — one, two, three, four — until he steps off on the far bank.

Only then does he tell the crocodiles that there is no king, no count, and no gift. He bounds away into the trees, leaving them angry in the water. In other tales he tricks a tiger into being trapped under a fallen log, fools a snake into letting him pass, and outsmarts a giant. Across the Malay world, Sang Kancil is the small clever one who proves that wit beats size every time.
zh: 「桑坎契尔」——小鼷鹿——是马来与印尼民间故事中最受人喜爱的骗子英雄。他体型小巧，又秀气，根本无法和林中体格更大的动物正面抗衡，但他的机智总能救他一命。在他最有名的故事里，他想横渡一条宽阔的河，水里却有一条鳄鱼，正巴不得吃了他。
桑坎契尔扯着嗓子对鳄鱼说：「森林之王命我清点这条河里所有的鳄鱼，要给每位送份礼物。」鳄鱼听了正中下怀，立刻叫来兄弟堂亲，一只一只地排队横过河面。桑坎契尔便从一只鳄鱼的头跳到下一只的头上，一边大声点数——一、二、三、四——一路跳到对岸。
上了岸他才告诉鳄鱼：根本没有什么森林之王，也没有清点，更没有礼物。他纵身钻入林中，把一河鳄鱼气得团团转。其他故事里，他又设计把老虎困在倒下的树干下、骗蛇放他过去、戏耍巨怪。在马来世界各地，桑坎契尔就是那个一次又一次用「机智胜过体型」的小小聪明者。

en: Jaka Tarub was a young Javanese man who lived alone in the village of Tarub. One evening, walking by a forest pool, he saw seven beautiful women bathing in the water. They were heavenly nymphs, the bidadari, who had flown down from the sky and laid their flying scarves on the bank. Jaka, struck by the beauty of the youngest, hid her scarf among the rushes.

When the other six finished bathing they put on their scarves and flew home, but the youngest, Nawang Wulan, could not find hers and could not return to heaven. Jaka pretended to find her by chance and took her back to his hut as his wife. She agreed to marry him on the condition that he never ask how she cooked the household's rice.

She fed him from a single grain of rice in the pot each day. Years passed and they had a daughter. One day Jaka could not resist; he lifted the lid and saw the secret. From then on the rice grew normal, and Nawang Wulan had to use it as ordinary mortals did. While storing rice in the granary one day, she found her hidden scarf where Jaka had kept it. She put it on, told him she could no longer stay, and flew back to heaven, leaving him and their daughter on the earth.
zh: 查卡塔鲁布是住在爪哇塔鲁布村的一个年轻男子。某天傍晚，他在林中水潭旁散步，看到七位绝美的女子正在水里沐浴。她们其实是从天而降的仙女——「比达达里」——飞行用的薄纱披在岸边。查卡被最小的一位深深吸引，悄悄把她的薄纱藏进芦苇丛中。
其他六位浴罢披上薄纱飞回天上，最小的娜旺乌兰找不到自己的薄纱，再也飞不回去。查卡假装是无意路过把她遇见，请她到自己茅屋作妻。她答应嫁给他，但提出条件：他永远不能问她家里的米是怎么煮出来的。
她每天只用一粒米下锅，就足以供全家食用。多年过去，两人生下一个女儿。某天查卡再也按捺不住好奇，掀开锅盖一看，破了她的秘诀。从此饭锅里的米只能像凡人一样一粒粒地下。某日她在谷仓中收米，无意间发现了查卡当年藏起的那条薄纱。她重新披上薄纱，告诉他自己再也留不下来了，便飞回天上，把他与女儿留在了人间。

en: Ollantay was a great general of the Inca army under the emperor Pachacuti. He fell in love with the emperor's daughter, the princess Cusi Coyllur, and she with him. They married in secret, against all law: the daughter of the Sapa Inca could not be given to anyone outside the royal Inca lineage. When Pachacuti discovered the union, he was furious. He had his daughter shut away and Ollantay banished from the court.

Ollantay fled to the mountains around the fortress town that still bears his name, Ollantaytambo. He gathered loyal men and rebelled. For ten years he held the fortress against Pachacuti's armies. Cusi Coyllur was kept hidden in a cell in Cuzco; the daughter she had borne to Ollantay was raised by the Sun Virgins not knowing who her parents were.

After Pachacuti's death his son, the new emperor Tupac Yupanqui, captured Ollantay through stratagem. Brought back to the capital, Ollantay expected execution. But the emperor's daughter — Ollantay's own child, now grown — recognized her father at court, was reunited with her mother from her cell, and asked for clemency. Tupac Yupanqui granted it, restored Ollantay to his ranks, and reunited the family. The drama survives in Quechua and is one of the finest works of pre-Columbian South American literature still performed.
zh: 奥扬泰是印加帝国帕查库特克皇帝麾下的一位大将。他爱上了皇帝的女儿、公主库西科尤尔，公主也爱上了他。两人私下成婚——这违反一切法度：「萨帕·印加」之女不许嫁给印加王族以外的任何人。帕查库特克得知后勃然大怒，将女儿关押起来，又把奥扬泰逐出宫廷。
奥扬泰逃到那座如今仍以他为名的山间要塞——奥扬泰坦博。他召集忠勇之士，揭旗反抗。整整十年，他在那座要塞之上挡住了帕查库特克的大军。库西科尤尔被秘密囚禁在库斯科城内的牢室中；她为奥扬泰所生的女儿，则被「太阳贞女」们抚养长大，并不知自己的父母是谁。
帕查库特克死后，他的儿子图帕克·尤潘基继位，以计将奥扬泰擒回都城。奥扬泰本以为必死无疑。可此时图帕克·尤潘基的「妹妹」——其实就是奥扬泰那位早已长大的女儿——在朝堂上认出了父亲，又被人放出到母亲牢中相认，回头哭求皇帝赦免一家人。图帕克·尤潘基答应了，把奥扬泰恢复将职，让一家团聚。这部剧本以克丘亚语流传至今，是前哥伦布时期南美洲文学中至今仍可上演的杰作之一。

en: Among the Nyanga of the Congo, the hero Mwindo was born to a chief named Shemwindo who had sworn that no son would ever come from his many wives. When at last one wife gave birth, the child was Mwindo, born already speaking and carrying a small drum and a conga-scepter. The chief tried to kill him at once. He had the baby placed in a wooden drum and thrown into the river.

Mwindo did not die. He floated downstream, came ashore, and was raised by his father's sister. As soon as he was strong enough, he gathered allies and set out to bring his father to account. He pursued Shemwindo through the upper world, then down through the earth into the underworld of Muisa, the lord of the dead. He passed every test that Muisa set him.

He at last found his father, who had hidden himself in the lap of Muisa, and dragged him back to the village. Rather than kill him, Mwindo divided the chiefdom with him, taking the lower half of the land and leaving Shemwindo the upper. He commanded that, from then on, no chief should ever try to kill his own children. The long song of Mwindo's pursuit and return is still recited by Nyanga bards as the epic of how a wrong against a child was set right.
zh: 刚果尼安加人中，英雄姆温多生于酋长舍姆温多家。舍姆温多曾立誓，他的众多妻子谁也不能给他生儿子。最后终有一位妻子生下了一个孩子，那便是姆温多——他出生时已能开口说话，手里还握着一只小鼓和一根权杖。父亲当即想杀死他。他命人把婴儿装进一只木鼓，投入河中。
姆温多并没有死。他顺流漂下，上岸后被父亲的姐姐收养长大。等他足够强壮，他召集盟友，启程去找父亲算账。他先在上层世界追上舍姆温多，又一路追到地下，进入由亡者之主慕伊萨掌管的冥界。慕伊萨给他设的每一道考验，他都一一通过。
他最终在慕伊萨膝下找到了藏身的父亲，把他押回故里。姆温多并没有杀他，而是与他平分酋长之位：自己取了下半领地，把上半留给舍姆温多。他下令此后任何一位酋长，都不许再图谋杀害自己的孩子。这段「父子追逐与归位」的长诗，至今仍由尼安加吟游艺人讲唱，作为「一桩对孩子的不公如何被纠正」的史诗。

en: Among the Mongo and other peoples of the Congo rainforest, the bieloko (singular eloko) are small forest beings who haunt the deepest parts of the jungle. They are short, hairy, with grass for body covering and grass on their heads, and their mouths are full of sharp teeth that pierce anything they bite. They are jealous guardians of the fruits and game of the forest.

An eloko carries a small bell. Travelers who hear the bell deep in the woods are said to fall under a spell, drawn to its sound and unable to remember the path home. Once close enough, the eloko devours them. Those who hunt or gather without proper charms are most at risk.

Protection against the eloko is wearing a special amulet, often given by a healer, that breaks the bell's hold. Hunters whisper to one another that anyone who hears a sweet small bell in the forest where there should be none must stop their ears at once and turn back. The eloko remains, in modern Congo, an image of why the deep forest is not a place to go alone and never a place to take lightly.
zh: 在刚果热带雨林中的蒙戈等民族之中，「埃洛科」（复数 bieloko）是住在丛林最深处的小妖。他们身材矮小，浑身毛茸茸，身上和头上覆着丛丛草叶，嘴里长着尖利无比的牙齿，咬到什么便能贯穿什么。他们是林中果实与猎物的善妒守护者。
埃洛科手里常拿着一只小铃。林中深处听到这只小铃响声的人，据说会被铃声迷住，无可自抑地朝声音方向走去，竟想不起回家的路。等人凑得足够近，埃洛科便会一口将其吞下。猎人或采食者若没有合适的护身物便深入林子，最容易遭殃。
对付埃洛科要靠特别的护身符，通常由医人配给，可以解开那铃声的迷力。猎人之间互相低语：林中本不该响起的甜蜜小铃声若钻进了耳朵，要立刻塞耳掉头回走。今日的刚果仍以埃洛科作为一种警示——为什么深林不是一个人能去的地方，更不是一个可以掉以轻心的地方。

en: Yacumama, "Mother of the Water" in Quechua, is the great water-serpent of the Amazon. She is said to be enormous beyond reckoning, dozens of meters long, living in the deepest pools and lagoons of the river. She is the protector of all that lives in the water, and rivers and lakes are understood to be alive because of her.

She can rise out of the water as a column of steam, swallowing canoes and bathing animals into herself. Fishermen of the Amazon say that her breath can be felt on the river at certain hours, as a sudden cold rising from the surface. They speak of her with care and do not name her without need.

Travelers approaching a known Yacumama pool blow on a horn of seashell before going near. The serpent below, hearing it, lifts herself away, allowing them safe passage. Without that warning, those who enter her water do not always come back. From the Andes to the lower Amazon, she remains the great mother of the rivers and a reminder that the rainforest's water is not unprotected.
zh: 在克丘亚语里被称为「水之母」的亚库玛玛，是亚马逊河的大水蛇。她身躯巨大得无从计量，几十米长，住在江河与潟湖最深的水下。她守护所有水中生灵，亚马逊一带的人也认为，河湖之所以「活着」，正是因为有她在其中。
她能以水汽柱的形式从水里升起，把独木舟、连同岸边饮水的牲畜，一并卷入自身。亚马逊的渔人说，某些时辰能在河面上感到她的呼吸——一股从水面突然涌起的寒气。他们谈起她时小心翼翼，不到必要时不直呼其名。
靠近某处「亚库玛玛潭」的旅人，会先吹一只海螺号角再上前。水下的蛇听到号角声后会暂时退开，让人安然通过。若没有这一声警示就贸然涉水，闯入她水域的人，未必都能回来。从安第斯到亚马逊下游，她始终是众河之母，也提醒人们：雨林中的水，从来不是无人守护的。

en: In the Guarani tradition of Paraguay and northern Argentina, Pombero is a small, hairy spirit who walks the countryside at night. He is short, with very large feet, dark skin, and long fingers. He moves in absolute silence and can imitate the calls of any bird or animal. He prefers the hours just after dusk, when fields and roads grow quiet.

He is mischievous more than evil. He whistles at women alone, hides small objects, frightens horses, and at times slips into houses to leave the doors swinging. Children who whistle at night in the countryside are warned that they may catch his attention. Those who offend him may find their work undone, their water sour, or their dog gone missing.

He can also be bargained with. Country families leave a little tobacco, a sugar cane, or a small bottle of cana on a windowsill or at a tree at the edge of the property in the evening. If Pombero takes it, he may grant a favor — protect the chickens, lead a lost cow back, or keep a stranger from making it to the door. He is still spoken of seriously in many parts of rural Paraguay today.
zh: 在巴拉圭与阿根廷北部的瓜拉尼传统里，蓬贝罗是夜里出没乡野的一个矮小毛精灵。他个子不高，脚却出奇地大，皮肤黝黑，手指细长。他行走时毫无声响，又能模仿任何一种鸟兽的叫声。他最常出现的时辰是傍晚日落不久之后，田野和小路开始安静下来的时候。
他更多是顽皮，而非邪恶。他会冲独自走路的妇人吹口哨、把小物件藏起来、惊吓马匹，有时还会溜进屋里把门吹得左右晃。乡间的孩子被大人告诫：夜里不许在外吹口哨，不然就要把他招来。冒犯过他的人，会发现做好的活莫名作废，水变酸，或家中的狗一夜失踪。
他也可以被「打点」。乡间人家会在傍晚把一小撮烟草、一截甘蔗或一小瓶土制甘蔗酒放在窗台上，或留在自家地界边的一棵树下。蓬贝罗若收了，便可能答应替你做些事——替你看好鸡群、把走失的母牛领回来、或挡住夜里上门的陌生人。今日巴拉圭乡下，许多人仍然认真地谈论着他。

en: On the island of Viti Levu in Fiji, the great serpent Degei is said to live in a cave on the high ridge of the Nakauvadra mountains. He was the first being, in many Fijian traditions, and from him came the islands and their first people. He is usually pictured as enormous, with the upper body of a man or a god and the long tail of a serpent.

In one telling, a hawk laid two eggs on the ridge, and Degei kept them safe in his cave until they hatched. Out of the two eggs came a boy and a girl, the first humans of Fiji. Degei raised them and taught them how to plant yams and taro, to build canoes, and to fish on the reef. From those two children all Fijian peoples descended.

Degei still lies in his cave on the mountain. When he turns in his sleep, the islands feel earthquakes; when he stirs, there is thunder. Old Fijians made offerings to him at the foot of the Nakauvadra range and asked him to keep the rains in their proper season. He is the deep guardian of the islands he once made fit to be lived in.
zh: 在斐济维提岛上，大蛇德盖伊据说住在纳考瓦德拉山脊上的一处岩洞里。在斐济众多传统中，他是最初的存在，斐济群岛和最早的人类都从他身上来。他多被描绘得无比庞大，上身像人形或神祇，下身则是一条长长的蛇尾。
一种说法里，有一只鹰在山脊上产下两枚蛋，德盖伊把蛋安置在自己洞中守护，直到它们孵化。两枚蛋里走出了一对小男孩和小女孩，便是斐济最初的人。德盖伊把他们养大，教他们种山药、种芋头，造独木舟，到珊瑚礁上打鱼。斐济各支民族都从这一对孩子繁衍而下。
德盖伊至今仍卧在山中那处洞穴里。他在睡梦中翻身时，群岛便有地震；他打起精神时，天上便起雷声。古时斐济人在纳考瓦德拉山脚向他献祭，祈求他让雨水按时节降下。他是这片他亲手造得宜于人居的群岛深处的守护者。

en: In the Krkonose mountains between Bohemia and Silesia, the mountain spirit Rubezahl walked the high paths and forests. He could appear as anything — a peasant, a friar, a giant, a hare, a tree stump — and changed shape according to his mood. He liked to test the people who crossed his mountains.

Travelers who were kind to him in his disguises — sharing bread with an old man, helping a hurt animal, paying respect at his stone — found their packs lighter, their paths shorter, gold in their pockets. Travelers who mocked the strange figures they met or boasted of their wealth would suddenly find themselves walking in circles, lost in fog, or shedding the coins they had just won.

He hated being called "Rubezahl," which was a name given to him by someone he had once tricked and which means roughly "turnip-counter." Anyone who called him that to his face in his own mountains risked landslides, mist, or worse. Folktales of the mountain shepherds and miners of the Krkonose passed his stories on as a reminder that the high country is its own kingdom, with rules of courtesy that humans do not get to set.
zh: 在波希米亚与西里西亚之间的克尔科诺谢山中，山中精灵吕贝察尔常常出没在高山小径与林间。他能化作任何模样——农夫、修士、巨人、野兔、树桩——心情怎样，模样就跟着怎样变。他喜欢拿过路人来试一试他自己山中的规矩。
对他的化身存善心的旅人——肯把面包分给路边老人、肯救一只受伤的小动物、肯在他那块石头前行礼——上路后会发现自己的背包变轻、路变短，口袋里也会多出金币。嘲笑遇见的怪人、当众夸耀自家财富的旅人，则会忽然发觉自己在山里反复走同一条路，被浓雾困住，或刚到手的金币又一枚枚滚出口袋。
他最恨「吕贝察尔」这个名字——那是某个被他作弄过的人替他起的绰号，大意是「数萝卜的」。谁要是在他自己的山里当面这样喊他，便有滑坡、迷雾甚至更可怕的事情等着。克尔科诺谢山中的牧人和矿工世代相传他的故事，提醒后人：那片高山是它自己的王国，有它自己那一套礼数，由不得人间来订。

en: Krabat was a young Sorbian beggar boy who, in a Wendish folktale of Lusatia, was drawn by a dream to a black mill at the edge of a dark moor. The mill had room for twelve apprentices. Each morning they ground grain for ordinary customers; each night they worked secret magic for the master, and on certain days each year the master taught them the words of the dark book.

Krabat learned that every New Year's Eve one of the apprentices died, so that the master could remain alive another year. He saw friend after friend fall. He fell in love with a young woman from the nearby village, called only the Kantorka, the cantor's daughter, whom he had met one Easter morning when she sang in the dawn service. She did not know yet that he was at the mill.

On the New Year's Eve when it was Krabat's turn to die, the Kantorka came to the mill and asked the master for him. The master agreed only if she could pick him out from all twelve apprentices, who were turned into ravens lined up before her. Looking at the ravens, she heard one whose heart she knew was afraid for her. She named that raven. Krabat was returned to her, the mill's magic broke at once, and the master fell down dead. Otfried Preussler's novel later carried the tale to readers across Europe.
zh: 卢萨蒂亚地区的索布族民间故事中，年轻的乞儿克拉巴特某夜被一个梦境引到一片荒原边的「黑磨坊」前。磨坊里正好有十二个学徒名额。白天他们替普通客人磨面，夜里则替师父行各种秘术；每年特定日子，师父还会教他们「黑书」中的密语。
克拉巴特渐渐发现：每年新年除夕，必有一位学徒死去，好让师父再多活一年。他眼看一位接一位朋友倒下。其间他爱上了附近村子里的一位姑娘——人们只称她「康托卡」，意为「领唱者的女儿」——他们是在一个复活节清晨听她在唱晨歌时相识的。那时她还不知道他在黑磨坊里学徒。
轮到克拉巴特该死的那一年除夕，康托卡赶到磨坊，向师父讨人。师父答应放人，但条件是：她必须从十二个学徒中把克拉巴特认出来——这十二人此时全被变成乌鸦，排成一行立在她面前。她看着群鸦，听到其中一只乌鸦的心里正为她而战栗。她叫出了这只乌鸦的名字。克拉巴特随她还魂，磨坊的法力当场崩散，师父应声倒毙。后来奥特弗里德·普劳斯勒的小说，又把这段故事带到了整个欧洲读者面前。

en: In Balinese tradition, the world holds two great forces in constant tension. Barong is the king of good spirits, shown as a lion-like creature with a long bristling mane, gentle eyes, and a body operated in performance by two dancers.
